data: Alright, welcome back to another episode of our show. Today we diving into a topic that every single person in this country can relate to, whether you like it or not, whether you want to talk about it or not. We talking about studying in Singapore. It is a journey that starts before you can even properly hold a pencil.Wei Lin: Wah, Marcus, you really know how to pick the stressful topics. Just hearing the word "studying" already make me feel sian. My shoulders tense up already. I can feel the ghost of my Primary Six math tutor haunting me.

As an international student, I must say, the intensity here is on another level. Back in my country, we have homework, sure, we have exams, but here, it is like a full-time job on top of another full-time job. The pressure is just different. Everyone is running a race, but nobody knows where the finish line is.

Let us start from the very beginning. Primary school. Wei Lin, you grew up in the system. How was it for you? Was it as bad as people say, or is it just exaggerated?

Honestly, quite traumatic. My parents very kiasu. Primary One already send me for tuition. Not just one subject, but Math, English, Higher Chinese, all got extra classes. Weekend also no rest. Saturday morning Math, Saturday afternoon English, Sunday morning Chinese. I never got to play.

Same here. My mother always say, "If you don't go tuition, you fall behind." Then you look around, all your classmates also going tuition. So you feel like, wah, if I don't go, I will be the only one left behind. It is that kiasi feeling. You don't want to be the odd one out. You don't want to be the one who fail.

My dad was more stressed than me, I swear.

At the cost of our mental health, mostly. I remember in Secondary Four, I sleep maybe four hours a day. Wake up, go school, come back, tuition, do homework, sleep, repeat. My hair drop until I thought I going bald. I was drinking three packets of kopi-o everyday just to survive.

And then the split. JC or Poly. The great divide. The moment where paths diverge.

I went Poly. Actually, I think Poly quite good. More hands-on, less theory. You do a lot of projects, a lot of presentations. It prepares you for the real working world. But people look down on Poly, say it is for those who cannot make it to JC. That stigma is still there, although it is getting better slowly.

That is a very old mindset. Now Poly grads very sought after. Companies like that they have practical skills. But I went JC. A-Levels is a different kind of hell.

Why you say that? What makes it so bad compared to Poly?

Because the content so deep. H2 Math, H2 Physics, H2 Chemistry. You think you understand in class, then the tutorial questions come out, you look at it like, "What is this alien language?" And the grading curve very brutal. You need to be in the top percentile just to get a decent grade.

I did my A-Levels overseas, but my cousins here tell me the same thing. The bell curve means even if you score 90 marks, if the whole cohort score 95, you still get a U or an S. It is not about how much you know, it is about how much you know compared to the person sitting next to you. It is a zero-sum game.

Exactly. It breeds this toxic competition. You don't want to share your study notes because you afraid the other person will score higher than you. Very sad, but very true. You see people hiding their books during recess, pretending they never study.

Wah, CORS. The Course Registration System. The bane of every university student's existence. It is a love-hate relationship, but mostly hate. The anxiety during bidding week is real.

For our listeners who don't know, explain how it works. Make it simple for the uninitiated.

Okay, so you have this bidding system. You get points, and you use points to bid for modules. But everyone want the same few modules because the professor give easy A, or the class timing very good, like no 8 AM classes. So the points required to get those modules go through the roof.

Then the system always crash during the peak bidding period. You refresh, refresh, refresh, then suddenly, "System Error". By the time it load back, the module already full. Then you have to take some 8 AM class on a Friday, or a module that is 100 percent exam with no continuous assessment. Very painful. You kena sabo by the system.

Don't remind me. Every semester, at least two group projects. And there is always that one freerider. The one who never reply WhatsApp, never come to meetings, then one day before the presentation, they suddenly appear and say, "Eh, I can help with the PowerPoint design."

I also notice the library culture here is very intense. In my country, the library is a place to chill, maybe read a book, listen to music. Here, the library is a battlefield. You need tactical gear to survive.

You must go at 7 AM to reserve a discussion room. If you late, all the good spots taken. People leave their bags and laptops on the table to "chope" the seat, then go for lunch for two hours. You cannot touch their stuff, because if you do, they will scold you. They act like they own the library.

That is the price you pay for good food. Now, let us talk about the future. Graduation is coming. The five Cs. Cash, Car, Credit Card, Condo, Country Club. Is that still a thing, or is it a myth?

The five Cs is a bit outdated, but the pressure to get a high-paying job is still very real. Everyone want to go into finance, tech, or consulting. If you are not in those three, people look at you like you are failing in life. The societal expectation is very narrow.

And the internships. You need internship experience to get a job, but you need a job to get internship experience. It is a chicken and egg situation. Very frustrating. You send out hundreds of resumes and hear nothing back.

I applied for so many internships. The rejection emails are so standard. "Thank you for your interest, but we regret to inform you..." I receive so many, I can write a book. I have a whole folder just for rejection emails. It is demoralizing.

And the interviews. They ask you, "Where do you see yourself in five years?" I want to say, "Hopefully not working on a weekend," but I have to say, "I see myself growing with the company and taking on more leadership roles." Very fake. We all know it is fake, and the interviewer knows we know it is fake.

What about National Service for the guys? How does that affect the studying journey? Darren, you just finished NS, right? How was the transition?

Yeah, just ORD last year. NS is a whole different topic. You finish JC or Poly, then you disappear for two years. By the time you come out, your academic knowledge already rusty. You forget all the formulas, all the theories. Your brain is filled with weapon handling and route marching.

Then you go university, you feel like you are two years behind your female peers who went straight in. They are already in their second year, joining clubs, doing exchange programs, while you are still trying to remember how to solve a quadratic equation. It is a huge shock to the system.

But NS also build character, right? You learn discipline, you learn how to survive with very little sleep. Which is actually good preparation for university group projects. You learn to deal with difficult people.

Wah, Wei Lin, you really try to find the silver lining. But true lah, after NS, you appreciate the air-conditioned lecture theatre a lot more. And you appreciate your own bed. Sleeping on a camp bed for two years really makes you value a proper mattress. You never want to see a green uniform again.

Let us touch on mental health. It is a big topic now. Do you think universities are doing enough to support students who are struggling?
VB PRP VB IN JJ NN . PRP VBZ DT JJ NN RB . VBP PRP VB NNS VBP VBG RB TO VB NNS WP VBP VBG .

I think they are trying. There are more counselors available now, and they have mental health awareness weeks. They send out emails telling you to take care of yourself, to breathe, to rest. But the stigma is still there.

Yes. If you say you are stressed, people will say, "Eh, everyone stressed. Just push through." Or "You think you are the only one suffering?" It invalidates your feelings. You feel guilty for feeling stressed. You feel weak for asking for help.

And the workload doesn't decrease. They give you all these resources, but at the end of the day, you still have three essays due on the same day. The resources are just a band-aid on a bullet wound. The system itself is the problem. The structure needs to change, not just the support services.

So what is the advice for the younger generation? The ones currently in primary or secondary school, listening to this, or maybe their parents are listening. What would you tell them?
RB WP VBZ DT NN IN DT JJR NN . DT NNS RB IN JJ CC JJ NN , VBG IN DT , CC RB PRP$ NNS VBP VBG . WP MD PRP VB PRP .

I would say, don't let your grades define your worth. I know it is easy to say, hard to do. But your PSLE or O-Level results do not determine if you will be happy or successful in life. There are so many paths. Life is not a straight line.
PRP MD VB , VB RB VB PRP$ NNS VB PRP$ NN . PRP VBP PRP VBZ JJ TO VB , JJ TO VB . CC PRP$ NN CC NNS HYPH NN NNS VBP RB VB IN PRP MD VB JJ CC JJ IN NN . EX VBP RB JJ NNS . NN VBZ RB DT JJ NN .

Find your passion. If you like art, go art. If you like cooking, go culinary school. Don't force yourself into a course just because your parents say it has "good prospects." You will be miserable for four years, and then miserable for the rest of your life. Do what makes you happy.

And take care of your mental health. It is okay to take a gap year. It is okay to fail a module. The world will not end. You can always retake, or find another path. Your health is more important than a piece of paper. No degree is worth your sanity.

Wah, semester start already, I tell you, my body still not recover from the holiday. You know that feeling right? Like you just close your eyes for blink of an eye, then suddenly it is week three already. I look at my Canvas, so many announcements, so many readings. I feel like I need to cheong everything at once, but then I just end up scrolling TikTok for two hours. Very sian. The transition from holiday mode to academic mode is always so jarring. One day you are sleeping until noon, the next day you are waking up at seven in the morning to catch a bus that is already late.

Alamak, don't say it. I also like that. The holiday always feel so short, like blink and it is gone. Then you come back to school, the weather also damn hot. You walk from the MRT to the faculty, sweat already wet through your shirt. Then you reach the lecture theatre, the air-con so cold until you shiver. It is like our body cannot adapt to Singapore weather at all. But honestly, week one is always like that. You just agak-agak first, see which module is heavy, which lecturer is strict. You cannot plan too much, otherwise you will stress yourself out. I always tell myself to take it slow-slow, but then the syllabus come out and I panic.

True, true. But the bidding system, that one really make me stress. I spend so much time calculating my points, thinking I can get this one specific module because the timing is so perfect. No 8 AM class, no Friday evening class. Then bidding day come, I refresh the page until my finger also cramp. Then suddenly, system error. By the time it load back, the module already full. Wah lao, I was so angry. I feel like the system is designed to test our patience, not to help us study. It is like a game of chance, but with your entire semester schedule at stake.

I know, right? The CORS or whatever system they use now, always crash at the most critical moment. It is like a test of faith. You pray to whatever god you believe in, just hope the page load fast. And then you see your classmates, the kiasu ones, they already prepare backup plan A, B, C, D. They bid for the module, but they also bid for another section just in case. Then they end up with double the modules, then later on they drop the extra one. It is very annoying because they take up the slots, then later on release it, but by that time, you already settle for a worse module. Very siao. It is like they are hoarding the resources, even if they don't need it.

Exactly. And then you get the module, you think it is easy, but then the first tutorial come. The tutor ask you to discuss the reading, and you realize the reading is like fifty pages of dense academic jargon. You sit there, nodding your head, pretending you understand, but inside you are completely lost. Then the tutor look at you, "So, what do you think?" You just smile and say, "I think the author make a very valid point." Very generic, but it save your life. I call it the art of academic survival. You learn to speak a lot without actually saying anything substantial.

Hahaha, yes! The art of saying nothing but sounding smart. I master this skill already. But seriously, the tutorial culture here is quite unique. Sometimes you get a tutor who is very passionate, they want to discuss for two hours. Then you have classmates who never read, they just come to eat their breakfast quietly. Then the tutor ask them, they just say, "I agree with the previous speaker." It is like a cycle. But I must say, some modules are actually quite interesting. Like that one sociology module I take, we discuss about Singapore society, the class divide, the meritocracy myth. It really open my eyes. It make me question things I always take for granted.

Wah, that one sound deep. I always avoid those kind of modules because the essay writing is very demanding. You need to cite so many sources, and if your argument is not strong enough, the marker will tear your paper apart. I prefer the quantitative modules. Math, statistics. At least the answer is either right or wrong. No need to argue about the "nuance" of the author's intention. But then again, the midterm for those quantitative modules can be very brutal. One careless mistake, your whole calculation go wrong, then you lose ten marks just like that. It is very unforgiving.

That is the pain of STEM. But I think both sides have their own struggles. For humanities, you write ten pages, get a B minus. For STEM, you calculate for two hours, make one small mistake, get a C. It is all about how you manage your expectations. And then, of course, there is the group project. The ultimate test of university life. I think every student in Singapore has a group project horror story. It is like a rite of passage. You cannot say you have truly experienced university life until you have suffered through a terrible group project.

Don't remind me. The group project is the reason why I have high blood pressure at age twenty. You get assigned to a group, you create the WhatsApp group, you say "Hi everyone, let's work together!" Then, silence. Absolute silence. You ask, "When can we meet?" No reply. Then one week before the deadline, suddenly everyone become very active. "Eh, sorry I was busy with CCA." "Eh, I have family matter." Then they expect you to do everything. The freerider phenomenon is very real. It is like they appear out of nowhere just to put their name on the cover page.

So true. And the worst part is, you cannot complain to the professor because you don't want to be the "snitch". So you just swallow your anger, do the extra work, and then during the presentation, the freerider suddenly stand up and present their one slide very confidently. Or we learn to choose our group members carefully.

Yes, the "friend group" project is always the best. You find your close friends, you know their working style, you know who will do the research, who will do the slides, who will present. Then everything run smoothly. But sometimes, the university force you to work with random people, and that is when the chaos begin. I remember one time, my group mate submit the wrong file for the final assignment. The file name is "draft_final_final_v3", but it is actually just a blank document with the title. We almost fail the module because of that. I was so shocked, I cannot even scold him. I just feel very sian. My heart drop to my stomach.

Wah, that is a nightmare. I can feel the panic from here. But you know, despite all these stresses, the university life also have its very shiok moments. Like the canteen food. I know people always complain about the price and the hygiene, but honestly, where else can you get a plate of chicken rice for four dollars? It is a blessing. And the hawker centres around the campus, that is where the real bonding happen. You finish your tutorial, you are very hungry, you and your friends go to the canteen, order your favorite food, and then just complain about life. That is very therapeutic.

Definitely. The canteen is the heart of the university. I always go to that one economic bee hoon stall. The uncle there very nice, he always give me extra vegetables. "You student, need to eat more, study hard," he will say. Then I feel very touched. But the queue is always damn long. You need to queue for twenty minutes just to get your food, then you spend ten minutes eating because you have another class in half an hour. It is a very rushed makan experience. But it is part of the culture. You learn to eat fast, like a competitive sport.

And the bubble tea! You cannot survive university without bubble tea. It is the official coping mechanism. You fail your quiz, you drink bubble tea. You have a deadline, you drink bubble tea. You finish your deadline, you reward yourself with bubble tea. The sugar rush give you the temporary energy to keep going. But then later on, you realize you gain weight, and then you feel sian again. It is a vicious cycle. I try to switch to less sugar, but then it taste like water, so what is the point?

Hahaha, very true. But speaking of deadlines, the library culture here is very intense. Especially during the exam week or the reading week. You go to the central library at 8 AM, and all the discussion rooms are already booked. People leave their bags and laptops on the table to chope the seat, then they go for lunch for two hours. You cannot touch their stuff, because if you do, they will scold you. They act like they own the library. And the smell of the library at night, mix of instant noodles, coffee, and stress. Very unique Singaporean aroma. It is the smell of academic desperation.

I know that smell very well. It is the smell of desperation. Everyone typing furiously on their laptops at 2 AM. You look around, everyone look like a zombie, but they are still typing. And the sound of the keyboard, click clack click clack, it is like a symphony of stress. But somehow, there is a sense of camaraderie. You are all suffering together. You look at the person next to you, they look at you, you both nod, and then you go back to your work. It is a shared trauma. We are all in this sinking ship together, might as well make the best of it.

Shared trauma, I like that phrase. It is very accurate. But besides studying, what about the extracurricular activities? The CCAs. I feel like there is so much pressure to join a CCA, and not just join, but to become a committee member, then an executive committee member. Everyone want to pad their resume. The kiasu culture is very strong in this aspect. You see people running for president of the club, and they make campaign videos like they are running for prime minister. It is very intense for just a university club.

Yes, it is very exaggerated. Sometimes I wonder, is it for the passion, or is it just for the resume? Because when you apply for internships later on, the first thing they ask is, "What leadership roles do you have?" So you are forced to take on these roles, even if you don't really have the time or the interest. Then you end up burning out. You have to attend meetings at night, organize events on the weekend, and then you still have to study for your midterms. It is very draining. I see many of my friends drop out of their CCA halfway because they cannot handle the pressure. They just disappear from the group chat.

That is why I choose to keep my CCA involvement very small-small. I just join as a normal member, I attend the events when I am free, and that is it. I don't want the stress of organizing everything. I value my sleep more than a title on my resume. But I know that is not the mindset of everyone. Many people feel that if they are not in the E-Comm, they are falling behind. It is a very toxic mindset, but it is deeply ingrained in our education system since primary school. We are trained to collect badges and certificates like they are Pokemon cards.

True. The system condition us to think that we must always be achieving something, always be productive. Rest is seen as laziness. But I think more students are starting to realize that mental health is more important. There are more talks about burnout, more resources available, although sometimes it feels like it is just lip service. The university send out emails saying "Please take care of your mental health," but then they still assign three group projects and two major essays in the same week. The actions do not match the words. It is very hypocritical.

Exactly. It is like putting a band-aid on a bullet wound. They give you a wellness workshop, but they don't reduce the workload. It is very frustrating. But I guess we just have to learn to set our own boundaries. We have to learn to say no. It is not easy, especially in a culture that values hard work and obedience, but it is necessary for our survival. I start to block out my weekends for rest. No study, no CCA, just sleep and watch Netflix. And I feel much better. My productivity actually increase because I am not running on empty.

That is a very healthy approach. You must protect your peace. And you know, another big part of university life is the part-time job. Many students have to work to support themselves or to save up for an exchange program. I work as a tuition teacher on the weekends. It is quite tiring, traveling from one part of Singapore to another, but the pay is not bad. And it help me to revise my own knowledge. But sometimes, the parents can be very demanding. They want their child to get an A in one month, as if I am a magician.

I agree. The hustle culture is very real. But I think it also build character. You learn the value of money, you learn time management, and you learn how to deal with difficult people. You wear your best suit, you print dozens of copies of your resume, and you go from booth to booth, trying to make a good impression. But most of the time, the HR people just take your resume, smile politely, and then you never hear from them again. It is very demoralizing. You send out a hundred applications, and you get maybe two interview calls. You start to doubt your own worth.

The rejection is very hard to swallow. You think, "Am I not good enough?" But you must remember that it is a numbers game. Sometimes it is not about your ability, it is just about the timing or the specific requirements of the company. And the interview questions are always so generic. "Where do you see yourself in five years?" I always want to say, "Hopefully not working on a weekend," but I have to give the standard corporate answer. It feels very fake. You have to pretend you are passionate about data entry or filing documents.

Exactly. Let start with the foundation, the bilingual policy. On paper, it sound very straightforward and logical. English for work, global communication, and economic survival. Mother Tongue for cultural roots and moral values. But in reality, how many of us actually feel deeply connected to our so-called "Mother Tongue"?

Honestly, for a large portion of the population, the Mother Tongue is more like a "Second Language" that we forced to learn just to pass exams. Take Mandarin, for example. The Speak Mandarin Campaign in the eighties was very successful in one specific way: it got everyone to speak Mandarin instead of their native dialects. But the unintended consequence was that it severed the linguistic link between the grandparents and the grandchildren. My Ah Ma speak mostly Hokkien. I can understand maybe thirty percent of what she say, but I cannot reply in Hokkien. So when we sit down to eat, there is this invisible language barrier in our own home. It is quite sad, actually. We traded rich dialect diversity for a standardized Mandarin that many of us still struggle to speak fluently.

Speaking of expression, I think this is what confuse foreigners the most. The discourse particles. They hear "lah" and think it just mean emphasis. But we know it is much more complex than that. It carry so much pragmatic meaning.

Oh, absolutely. The particles are the soul of Singlish. Take "lah", for example. It can be reassuring, like "Don't worry, can one lah." It can be dismissive, like "I already tell you already, you still don't listen, what to do lah." Or it can be a marker of obviousness, like "Of course it hot lah, this is Singapore." Then you have "leh", which softens a statement or shows uncertainty, like "I not sure leh, maybe we should check first." And "lor", which is resignation, like "If no choice, then we just accept it lor."

And don't forget "mah". That one is very specific. It is used when you think the other person should already know the answer, or when you are stating something obvious that they are questioning. Like, "You never eat chicken rice before mah?"

Yes! "Mah" is so powerful. It carry this tone of mild exasperation or disbelief. And then there is "sia", which is often used to express shock or emphasis, usually negative. "Wah lao, the traffic jam so bad sia." Or "hah", which is a question particle, like "You going later hah?" These particles carry so much emotional weight that a direct translation to Standard English often lose the entire nuance of the sentence.

That pragmatic meaning is exactly why it is so hard to explain to an ang moh boss or a foreign colleague. I remember one time, I told my foreign manager that a project timeline is "a bit steady". And that judgment starts in school, right? The education system is very strict about grammar. Sometimes it cross the line into prescriptivism that ignore the reality of how language is actually used by the majority of the population.

Very strict. And it can be quite damaging to a child's confidence. For example, the classic correction of "who have" to "who has". In Standard English, "who" is singular, so it should be "who has". But in Singapore English, "who" is often treated as a plural or collective pronoun when referring to a group of people. So "the people who have" become "the people who have", and by extension, "the person who have". It is a systematic grammatical feature of our variety, not just a random mistake.

The subtitling debate is very real. I remember when that local movie got picked up by an international streaming platform, they added subtitles for the Singlish parts. Some people were offended, saying, "Why need to subtitle? If you don't understand, then you not the target audience." But others argued that it is necessary for accessibility and for preserving the nuance for non-locals.

It is a double-edged sword. On one hand, subtitling validate Singlish as a legitimate language variety that requires translation, just like any other foreign language. It acknowledge that Singlish has its own unique vocabulary and grammar that cannot be easily mapped onto Standard English.

On the other hand, it can feel patronizing, like we are apologizing for our own language. But I think, practically, it is a good thing. It help to document and standardize the spelling of Singlish words. If we want Singlish to be taken seriously, we need to start treating it with the same linguistic respect as any other language, and that includes proper transcription and translation.

So, looking ahead, what do you think is the future of the language situation in Singapore? Will Singlish eventually die out as we become more globalized, or will it evolve into something else?

I don't think it will die out. Language is very resilient, especially when it is tied to identity. As long as Singaporeans feel the need to distinguish themselves from the rest of the world, Singlish will survive. But it will definitely evolve. We are already seeing new slang terms emerge from the youth, influenced by internet culture, K-pop, and global trends, but localized with Singaporean grammar and particles.

Like how they use "can" or "cannot" in very creative ways. Or the way they shorten words. The core structure will remain, but the vocabulary will refresh itself. The real challenge is not the survival of Singlish, but the survival of our Mother Tongues and dialects. That is the area where we are seeing genuine language loss.

Hahaha, yes. The "fake Singlish". It is like when an ang moh try to use "shiok" to describe everything. "This coffee is very shiok." "The weather is shiok." But "shiok" is not just "good". That is a very real struggle for a lot of young creators here. The societal pressure to conform to traditional success metrics is huge. But let us talk about the content itself. TikTok trends move so fast. What was it like trying to keep up with the algorithm in the Singaporean context?

It is exhausting, to be honest. The algorithm is very ruthless. In the beginning, it was all about dance challenges and lip-syncing. But then, the audience matured, and the platform pushed for more original content. So we saw the rise of the "POV" trend. Point of view. And Singaporean creators really latched onto that because it is the perfect format for observational humor. "POV: You are an NSman trying to explain your vocation to your civilian friends." "POV: Your tuition teacher gives you homework on a Friday night." These trends resonate because they are hyper-local. They capture the shared trauma and shared joy of growing up in Singapore.

I notice that a lot of these trends also heavily feature Singlish. Which is interesting, because for a long time, the establishment was trying to push the "Speak Good English" movement and discourage Singlish. But on TikTok, Singlish is basically the default language.

Absolutely. Singlish is the great equalizer on TikTok. It does not matter if you went to a prestigious school or a neighborhood school, if you use the right "lah", "leh", or "lor" in your video, people will connect with it. It feels authentic. If you try to speak perfect, polished Queen's English in a comedy skit about eating at a hawker centre, it will feel very robotic and disconnected. The audience will smell the fake from a mile away. They will be like, "Wah, this guy try too hard." But when you say, "Eh, this chicken rice damn shiok, but the queue so long I almost sian," that is real. That is how we actually talk.

But with that authenticity comes a lot of responsibility, right? Because TikTok is heavily consumed by youths, and their brains are still developing. There is a lot of concern about the impact of these trends on their mental health and attention spans. What is your take on that?

That is a very valid concern. I will not sugarcoat it. The app is designed to be addictive. The "For You Page" or FYP is a dopamine machine. You scroll, you get a funny video, your brain releases a little hit of happiness, you scroll again, another hit. Before you know it, two hours have passed and you have not done your homework. We call it "doom scrolling." And for young people who are already stressed about school, exams, and peer pressure, this endless scrolling can become an escape mechanism. But it is a dangerous one.

And then there is the comparison culture.
CC RB EX VBZ DT NN NN .

Oh, huge. TikTok is a highlight reel. You see these other young creators, maybe they are the same age as you, but they are traveling to Japan, driving nice cars, wearing branded clothes, and they make it look so effortless. You are sitting in your room, eating instant noodles, stressing over your O-Level results, and you start to feel like you are falling behind. You feel like your life is boring. This is where the mental health issues creep in. Anxiety, depression, low self-esteem. I have received so many DMs from young fans saying they feel inadequate because their life does not look like the videos they see on their FYP. And it breaks my heart, because I know that behind the camera, a lot of those creators are also struggling, also in debt, also faking it till they make it.

Let us shift gears a bit and talk about the business side of things. A lot of young people see successful TikTokers and think, "Wah, easy money. I just post a video and brands will throw money at me." What is the reality of monetizing TikTok in Singapore?
VB PRP VB NNS DT NN CC VB IN DT NN NN IN NNS . DT NN IN JJ NNS VBP JJ NNPS CC VBP , `` NNP , JJ NN . PRP RB VBP DT NN CC NNS MD VB NN IN PRP . '' WP VBZ DT NN IN VBG NN IN NNP .

It is a lot harder than it looks. The market here is very small. Compared to Indonesia or the Philippines, our population is tiny. So the ceiling for growth is much lower. To make a sustainable living, you cannot rely on TikTok alone. You need to diversify. You need YouTube, you need Instagram, you need to do live streaming, you need to secure brand deals. And even then, it is not stable. One month you might make ten thousand dollars, the next month you make zero because the algorithm changed or a brand decided to pull their budget.

And the brand deals themselves can be tricky.

Very tricky. You have to balance being authentic with satisfying the client. Sometimes a brand will give you a very strict script, and they want you to say their product is the best thing since sliced bread. But you know it is not. And your audience is smart. They can tell when you are just reading a script for money. They will call you out in the comments. "Eh, Darren, you sell out already." So you have to fight for creative control. You have to find a way to integrate the product into your content naturally, in a way that is still funny or engaging. It is a delicate balance.

I think we are going to see a shift towards more niche, high-quality content. The era of just doing a silly dance for fifteen seconds is fading. The audience is getting smarter and more demanding. For example, there are creators now who are breaking down complex financial topics, or explaining Singaporean history, or doing deep dives into local hawker food culture, all in short, engaging formats. Before we wrap up, what is the one piece of advice you would give to a fifteen-year-old who is watching this right now and dreaming of becoming a full-time TikTok star?

I would say, finish your studies first. Do not drop out of school to chase clout. The internet is forever, and a bad decision you make at fifteen can haunt you when you are twenty-five. Treat TikTok as a hobby, a creative outlet, a place to experiment and learn. If it blows up, great. If it does not, you still have your education to fall back on. And secondly, be kind. The internet can be a very toxic place. Do not add to the toxicity. Use your platform to spread positivity, or at the very least, to make people laugh in a wholesome way. And lastly, touch grass. Seriously. Put the phone down, go outside, meet your friends in real life, eat some good makan. Real life is where the actual memories are made, not on a screen.

So, I was reading this article the other day about the latest population figures released by the government, and it really got me thinking about the whole discourse around migration and what it means to be a multicultural society in Singapore today. Like, the numbers are what they are, but the underlying sentiment, you know, it feels like it is shifting.B: Wah, don't even start. Every time they release some new population white paper or demographic update, the whole country goes siao. Immediately the forums light up, everyone is complaining about overcrowding, MRT packed like sardines, and the usual heartlander angst. It is like clockwork, man.

Exactly. That is the rational, policy-level view. But I think what we need to unpack is why the emotional reaction is so strong. It is not just about the numbers. It is about the feeling of displacement. For a lot of locals, especially the PMETs, they feel like they are being squeezed out of their own country. They see foreigners getting jobs, sometimes at lower salaries, and it creates this underlying anxiety about the 'Singaporean Core'.

True, true. And it is interesting because Singapore's entire history is built on migration. I mean, Raffles landed here in 1819, and what was this place? A fishing village. The people who built this country were migrants. The coolies, the traders, the labourers from China, India, the Malay archipelago. They were all migrants. So in a way, being anti-migration is almost anti-Singaporean history. But somehow, the narrative has shifted to 'us versus them'.

Because the context is different now. Back then, everyone was a migrant trying to survive. There was no 'local' to protect yet. Now, we have a established national identity, a 'Singaporean' identity. It is like, 'We worked so hard to build this unique culture, and now you are coming in and changing it.'

But is the culture really changing, or is it just evolving? I mean, look at our food. Our food culture is the ultimate example of successful multiculturalism. A Chinese uncle can make the best roti prata, and an Indian auntie can cook amazing Hokkien mee. That is integration at the most fundamental level. Makan is the great equalizer.

And that is where I think the younger generation is feeling a lot of friction. We grew up being taught 'racial harmony' and 'multiculturalism' as these abstract, textbook concepts. We have Racial Harmony Day in school, we wear different ethnic costumes, we eat different food. But when we step out into the real world, we realize that tolerance is not the same as acceptance. There are still microaggressions, subtle biases, and systemic inequalities that we are only now starting to talk about openly.

Sure. Like, for instance, the assumption that all Malays are religiously conservative, or that all Indians are good at math or IT. Or the constant questioning of a Singaporean of minority race, 'No, where are you really from? Your English is very good, where did you study?' It implies that being truly Singaporean defaults to being Chinese and speaking perfect, unaccented English.

I think that is a fair observation. There is definitely a distinction between 'local Chinese' and 'PRC Chinese' in the public discourse. Exactly. And this is where the role of the media and social media becomes very dangerous. In the past, if someone had a bad encounter with a foreigner, they might complain to their friends at the kopitiam. Now, they can post a video on TikTok or Facebook, and it can go viral in hours. The algorithms are designed to amplify outrage because outrage drives engagement. So one isolated incident of a foreigner being rude can be framed as 'This is what foreigners are doing to our country', and it fuels this massive wave of resentment.

That is a very apt metaphor. The salad bowl versus the melting pot. And the question is, how do we encourage the mixing? Because the government policies, like the Ethnic Integration Policy (EIP) in HDB flats, were designed to force mixing. To prevent the formation of racial enclaves. And it has worked to a certain extent. You look at most HDB estates, they are quite well-mixed. But living next to someone of a different race is not the same as being friends with them.

I think schools used to be the main place where this mixing happened naturally. You are in the same class, you do projects together, you play sports together. But now, with the streaming system, the emphasis on academic grades, and the rise of tuition culture, even schools are becoming quite segregated by class and ability, which often correlates with race and background. So the organic opportunities for cross-cultural friendship are diminishing.

That is a very insightful point, Mikaela. The meritocratic system, which we pride ourselves on, can inadvertently create silos. If you are in the top tier of schools, you are likely surrounded by people from similar socio-economic backgrounds, which often means a certain demographic. And if you are in the lower tiers, you might be surrounded by a different demographic, including more children of lower-wage migrant workers or new immigrants. So the 'shared experience' of being a Singaporean student is fracturing.

And then you have National Service. NS is supposed to be the great equalizer, right? The ultimate Singaporean experience. You put a rich kid and a poor kid, a Chinese and a Malay, in the same platoon, and they have to suffer together. And in many ways, it does work. I made some of my best friends in NS, guys from totally different backgrounds. We learned to communicate, we learned to trust each other with our lives.

Speaking of the workplace, I think this is where the real test of our multiculturalism lies. How do we handle diversity in leadership? If you look at the boards of major local companies or the top echelons of the civil service, it is still overwhelmingly dominated by a certain demographic. We talk a lot about diversity, but is it genuine diversity, or is it just tokenism?

I think it is slowly changing, but very slowly. There is a lot of talk about 'inclusive leadership' and 'unconscious bias training', but sometimes these feel like box-ticking exercises. Real change requires restructuring how we define 'merit' and 'potential'. If 'potential' is unconsciously associated with a certain accent, a certain university, or a certain cultural background, then the system will continue to reproduce the same homogeneous leadership, no matter how many diversity workshops we attend.

And this ties back to the migration debate. If we want to attract and retain the best global talent, we need to show that Singapore is genuinely inclusive, not just at the hawker centre level, but at the boardroom level. If a highly skilled professional from a minority background or a new immigrant feels like they are hitting a bamboo ceiling, they will eventually leave. And we cannot afford to lose that talent.

But then again, the local population will ask, 'Why are we bending over backwards to accommodate foreigners when our own people are struggling to get promoted?' It is a very delicate balancing act. The government has to constantly reassure the heartlanders that the 'Singaporean Core' is being protected, while simultaneously keeping the economy open and competitive. It is like walking a tightrope.

And the rhetoric used by the government is very important here. Sometimes, the messaging can feel a bit top-down and paternalistic. Like, 'You must be more gracious, you must be more welcoming.' But if the underlying structural issues, like the cost of living and the feeling of being left behind, are not addressed, telling people to be 'gracious' will just breed more resentment. People need to feel secure before they can be generous.

Absolutely. Maslow's hierarchy of needs applies to societies as well. You cannot expect a society to be highly cosmopolitan and open if its basic economic and social security needs are not met. So, addressing the anxieties around migration is not just about running harmony campaigns; it is about ensuring that the benefits of migration and economic growth are distributed fairly among all Singaporeans.

And I think we also need to give credit to the grassroots level. Despite all the macro-level tensions, on the ground, there are so many everyday acts of multiculturalism that go unnoticed. The Malay neighbour who helps to collect the Chinese auntie's parcels when she is not home. The Indian colleague who brings extra Deepavali sweets for the whole office. The volunteer groups that organize activities for migrant workers on their weekends. These small, everyday interactions are the real glue that holds the society together.

But we cannot rely solely on the kampung spirit to solve systemic issues. We need robust policies. For example, the foreign worker levy system. Or is it just a cost that big companies easily absorb, while small businesses struggle? We need to constantly review these policies to ensure they are fit for purpose.

And we also need to rethink how we integrate new immigrants. Right now, it is very much a 'sink or swim' approach. You get your PR or citizenship, and you are expected to figure it out. Not just a welcome packet with some brochures, but actual community mentorship programs where established Singaporeans are paired with new immigrants to help them navigate the system, understand the culture, and build a social network.

And it also breaks down stereotypes for the locals. When you mentor someone, you learn about their background, their struggles, their aspirations. That is the power of contact theory. Meaningful, sustained contact between different groups reduces prejudice. But the contact has to be on equal footing. If the contact is only in a master-servant dynamic, like a local and their foreign domestic worker, it does not necessarily break down prejudices; it can sometimes reinforce them. We need more peer-to-peer interactions.

Which brings us back to the importance of shared spaces. Community clubs, sports leagues, hobby groups. These are the places where people can interact as equals, united by a common interest rather than divided by their background. And we should also be more mindful of our language. The way we talk about 'foreigners' as a monolithic block is very problematic. There is a huge difference between a highly paid expatriate on an Employment Pass, a mid-level manager on an S Pass, and a construction worker on a Work Permit. Lumping them all together under the label 'foreigner' erases their vastly different experiences and contributions.

So, today we are diving into a topic that is, honestly, quite heavy but also very close to all of our hearts. National identity. What does it actually mean to be Singaporean in this day and age? Because I was reading this article the other day, and it got me thinking, are we actually united, or are we just coexisting very politely?B: Wah, you start with a bombshell already. But I think it is a necessary conversation. Because sometimes I feel like our national identity is just a slogan we see on banners during National Day. 'One united people.' But when you scratch the surface, it is quite complicated. It is not just one single thing that binds us together.

That is a very apt metaphor. The salad bowl versus the melting pot. And the question is, how do we encourage the mixing? Because the government policies, like the Ethnic Integration Policy (EIP) in HDB flats, were designed to force mixing. To prevent the formation of racial enclaves. And it has worked to a certain extent. You look at most HDB estates, they are quite well-mixed. But living next to someone of a different race is not the same as being friends with them.

I think schools used to be the main place where this mixing happened naturally. You are in the same class, you do projects together, you play sports together. So the organic opportunities for cross-cultural friendship are diminishing.

And then you have National Service. NS is supposed to be the great equalizer, right? The ultimate Singaporean experience. I made some of my best friends in NS, guys from totally different backgrounds. We learned to communicate, we learned to trust each other.

Speaking of the workplace, I think this is where the real test of our multiculturalism lies. How do we handle diversity in leadership? If you look at the boards of major local companies or the top echelons of the civil service, it is still overwhelmingly dominated by a certain demographic. We talk a lot about diversity, but is it genuine diversity, or is it just tokenism?

I think it is slowly changing, but very slowly. There is a lot of talk about 'inclusive leadership' and 'unconscious bias training', but sometimes these feel like box-ticking exercises. And this ties back to the migration debate. If we want to attract and retain the best global talent, we need to show that Singapore is genuinely inclusive, not just at the hawker centre level, but at the boardroom level. If a highly skilled professional from a minority background feels like they are hitting a bamboo ceiling, they will eventually leave. And we cannot afford to lose that talent.

But then again, the local population will ask, 'Why are we bending over backwards to accommodate foreigners when our own people are struggling to get promoted?' It is a very delicate balancing act. And the rhetoric used by the government is very important here. Sometimes, the messaging can feel a bit top-down and paternalistic. Like, 'You must be more gracious, you must be more welcoming.' People need to feel secure before they can be generous.

Absolutely. Maslow's hierarchy of needs applies to societies as well. You cannot expect a society to be highly cosmopolitan and open if its basic economic and social security needs are not met. So, addressing the anxieties around national identity is not just about running harmony campaigns; it is about ensuring that the benefits of economic growth are distributed fairly among all Singaporeans.

And I think we also need to give credit to the grassroots level. Despite all the macro-level tensions, on the ground, there are so many everyday acts of multiculturalism that go unnoticed. It is a common story across many families. The language shift happens so fast. We wear the baju kurung or the saree for Racial Harmony Day, but we don't know the history or the deeper meaning behind it.

That is the big fear. The historical precedent is that technology creates more jobs than it destroys. Wah lao. Twelve dollars for laksa in a hawker centre? I can go to a proper restaurant for that price. It is the gentrification of the hawker trade. And it creates this weird divide. You have the old-school hawkers struggling to survive, keeping their prices as low as possible, and then you have these new, trendy stalls charging premium prices. And the authorities sometimes encourage this, you know, to 'elevate' the hawker scene. But who is it elevating for? Not the average Singaporean who just wants a cheap, good lunch.

Exactly. And it is interesting because the definition of a hawker centre is also blurring. We have the traditional open-air hawker centres, then we have the air-conditioned 'food studios' or 'culinary hubs'. They look like hawker centres, they have the same layout, but the rents are higher, the prices are higher, and the vibe is completely different. It feels more like a food court.

Speaking of food courts, do you think there is still a distinct difference between a hawker centre, a coffee shop, and a food court?
VBG IN NN NNS , VBP PRP VB EX VBZ RB DT JJ NN IN DT NN NN , DT NN NN , CC DT NN NN .

I think so. A coffee shop, or kopitiam, is usually smaller, more neighborhood-based. It is where you go for your daily kopi and a quick breakfast. The relationship with the uncle or auntie is very personal. A food court is usually in a mall, air-conditioned, more expensive, and the food is more standardized. A hawker centre is the middle ground. It is large, it has a huge variety of food, it is open-air, and it is a true community hub.

I agree. The hawker centre is a great equalizer. You will see the CEO of a multinational company sitting at the same plastic table as a construction worker, both eating the same $5 plate of Hainanese chicken rice. That is a beautiful thing. It is one of the few remaining public spaces in Singapore where all social classes mix naturally.

True. And it is not just the customers who need to adapt. The hawkers also need to adapt to new technologies. The push for cashless payments is a good example. I fully support it, it is more hygienic, it is faster, and it helps with accounting. But for an elderly hawker who has been handling cash for forty years, learning to use a QR code system or a card terminal can be very daunting.

And that is where the UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage status comes in. Singapore's hawker culture was inscribed in 2020. It is a huge recognition, and we should be proud of it. But what does it actually mean for the hawker on the ground?
CC DT VBZ WRB DT NNP NNP NNP NNP NN VBZ RP . NNP POS NNP NN VBD VBN IN CD . PRP VBZ DT JJ NN , CC PRP MD VB JJ IN PRP . CC WP VBZ PRP RB VB IN DT NN IN DT NN .

That is the big question. On paper, it means our hawker culture is recognized as something of outstanding universal value. It should help with preservation efforts, with attracting tourists, with boosting the pride of the hawkers. But in reality, does it help the uncle selling $4 noodle pay his increased rent? Does it help him find a successor? Not directly.

No, it does not. The UNESCO status is more of a symbolic victory. The real work needs to be done on the ground. Better rental policies, more comprehensive training and incubation programs for aspiring hawkers, and a shift in societal attitude to value the hawker trade as a respectable, viable career.

And we, as consumers, also have a role to play. We need to be more gracious. We need to understand that a five-dollar meal is already a bargain, given the effort and cost that goes into it. We need to be patient when the queue is long, and we need to be polite when we order.

Absolutely. And we should also make an effort to explore beyond the famous, viral stalls. Some of the best food is hidden in the unglamorous, older hawker centres in the heartlands. So, I was reading this article the other day, right, and it got me thinking about the whole concept of cultural integration versus just tolerance, you know? Because I feel like in Singapore, we always talk about racial harmony, and it’s like this very polished, very curated thing that we show to the world, like during Racial Harmony Day or whatever, where everyone puts on the traditional dress and eats the food, but I wonder if we actually understand the deep, underlying differences that still exist, or if we just sweep them under the rug because we are so kiasu about causing any kind of racial friction, and I think that is a very dangerous way to look at it, because if we don't actually confront the differences and have the difficult conversations, then we are just living in this illusion of peace, and the moment something triggers us, the whole facade can just crumble, you know what I mean?B: Wah, you touching on the heavy stuff today hor, but I think you hit the nail on the head, because I always feel like this tolerance thing is very passive, you know? Like, tolerance implies that you are putting up with something, that you find it annoying or uncomfortable, but you just endure it for the sake of peace, but integration, or actual acceptance, that one requires effort, it requires you to actually step out of your own cultural bubble and try to understand why the other person does things differently, and that is very hard because human nature is to stick with people who are like you, people who speak your language, eat your food, share your values, so even though we are forced to live together in these HDB flats because of the ethnic integration policy, that doesn't mean we actually interact on a deep level, we just share the same corridor, press the same lift, and then go back to our own separate worlds, so the physical proximity is there, but the cultural intimacy is still very lacking, I think.

Exactly lah, and I think that is the crux of the issue, right, because when you look at the HDB experience, yes, it is a great social engineering tool to prevent the formation of ethnic enclaves, which we saw happen in other countries like the US or the UK, where you have entire neighborhoods that are just one race, and that leads to parallel societies that never intersect, so the government was very smart to mandate the mixing, but I think what they didn't account for is the fact that just putting people in the same building doesn't automatically make them understand each other's cultural nuances, like, you might have a Chinese family living next to a Malay family, and they might be very polite, say hello, maybe even exchange some food during Hari Raya or Chinese New Year, but do they actually understand the religious sensitivities, the dietary restrictions, the different concepts of time and social obligations, or are they just operating on this very superficial level of 'neighborly politeness' because they are afraid of making a complaint to the town council?

That one very true sia, because I remember when I was younger, we had this Indian family living below us, and my mom was always very careful about not dropping things, not making too much noise, not because she was a considerate person by nature, but because she was afraid of offending them or causing a racial incident, so it was this very tense, very calculated kind of coexistence, rather than a genuine, organic relationship, and I think that fear is still very prevalent in our society, this fear of saying the wrong thing, of being labeled a racist, so people just choose to avoid the topic altogether, which means we never actually learn how to navigate the differences, we just tiptoe around them, and that is not real harmony, that is just a cold war where everyone is too scared to fire the first shot.

And I think that fear is amplified now by social media, right, because in the past, if you made a cultural faux pas, maybe you just embarrassed yourself in front of your neighbors, and you could apologize, buy them a packet of fruit, and move on, but now, everything is recorded, everything is put online, and within five minutes, you are the villain of the internet, and people are dragging you for being insensitive or racist, and I think this cancel culture, which is very much imported from the West, is actually quite harmful to our local context, because our context is completely different, we don't have the same history of civil rights movements, our racial dynamics are shaped by colonialism, by the specific migration patterns of Chinese, Malays, and Indians to this island, and by the pragmatic policies of the government to ensure survival, so when you just blindly apply these American frameworks of systemic oppression and microaggressions to our local interactions, you end up creating a lot of unnecessary anxiety and division, because people are so afraid of being canceled that they just stop engaging with other races altogether, which is the exact opposite of what we want to achieve.

Wah lao, you talking about the cancel culture is damn accurate hor, because I see a lot of young people now, especially the Gen Z, they are so hyper-vigilant about political correctness, they are always scanning everything for hidden biases, and while I appreciate the intention, because we do need to be more mindful of our words, I think they sometimes lack the nuance to understand the local ground reality, like, they will get very angry about something that an older generation person said, not realizing that the older person is not being malicious, they are just speaking from the context of their time, from the language they grew up with, and instead of educating them, having a patient conversation, the young people just want to condemn them, call them names, and cancel them, and I think that is very counterproductive, because you are just pushing them further into their corner, making them defensive, and they will never actually change their mindset, they will just learn to hide their true feelings, which again, brings us back to this fake harmony that we were talking about earlier.

Ya lor, and I think this is where the concept of 'dialogue' comes in, right, but not the kind of sterile, scripted dialogue that you see in official forums, where everyone is sitting in a nice air-conditioned room, speaking in perfect English, giving very measured, politically correct answers, but the real, messy, uncomfortable dialogue that happens on the ground, in the void decks, in the hawker centres, where people can actually speak their minds, use their Singlish, express their frustrations without being immediately judged or canceled, because I think Singlish itself is a very interesting cultural phenomenon, because it is this great equalizer, it is the one language that belongs to everyone, regardless of race, so when you speak Singlish with someone of a different race, you are instantly breaking down that barrier, you are saying, 'Hey, we are both Singaporeans, we share this same linguistic culture,' but at the same time, it can also be a source of exclusion, because if you don't know the nuances, if you don't understand the context, you might offend someone without realizing it, so it is this double-edged sword that we all have to navigate every day.

Exactly, and I think the hawker centre is the perfect microcosm of this whole cultural dynamic, right, because it is this space where everyone, from the CEO to the cleaner, sits down at the same table, eats the same food, sweats in the same heat, and it is supposed to be this beautiful melting pot of our society, but if you look closely, there are still these invisible boundaries, like the unwritten rules about halal and non-halal, the anxiety of bringing your non-halal food into a Muslim-owned stall, the careful navigation of which table to sit at, which uncle to order from, and I think a lot of people, especially the younger generation who are used to the convenience of food delivery and air-conditioned malls, they don't even realize these subtle dynamics exist, they just think, 'Eh, we all eat here, what difference does it make?' but for the older generation, for the people who are deeply religious, these boundaries are very real, very important, and if you don't respect them, you are not just being rude, you are violating their core beliefs, so it requires a level of cultural intelligence that I think we are losing in this fast-paced, modernized society.

And I think this cultural intelligence is something that cannot be taught in a classroom, you know, you can have Racial Harmony Day every year, you can make the kids wear the traditional costumes, eat the traditional food, but that is just this very superficial, performative exercise, it doesn't actually teach them how to handle a real-life situation where their cultural values clash with someone else's, like, what do you do when your Malay friend invites you to their house for a meal, but you are a strict vegetarian, and you don't want to offend them by questioning their halal certification, but you also cannot eat the food, how do you navigate that situation with grace and respect, that is the kind of practical cultural intelligence we need to develop, and I think the only way to learn it is through exposure, through making mistakes, through having those awkward conversations and learning from them, not through avoiding the topic altogether because we are afraid of causing offense.

Wah, you giving the scenario very real hor, because I actually faced that exact situation before, I went to my friend's house for a housewarming party, and they are a very traditional Malay family, they prepared this huge feast, and I am a vegetarian, but I didn't want to make a big deal out of it, I didn't want to be that 'picky' guest, so I just ate the vegetables and the rice, and I tried to be very enthusiastic about it, but later on, the mother pulled me aside and asked if I didn't like the food, and I had to explain my dietary restrictions, and she was so apologetic, she felt so bad that she didn't prepare something for me, and that was the moment I realized, cultural differences are not just about avoiding offense, they are also about understanding the hospitality, the generosity, the underlying values of the other culture, and in this case, the Malay culture of 'membuka pintu rumah' (opening the house) is very much about feeding your guests abundantly, so my refusal to eat, even though it was for a valid reason, was seen as a rejection of their hospitality, so it taught me that you have to look beyond the surface level of the action, and understand the cultural intent behind it.

That is such a beautiful example, and I think that is exactly what is missing in a lot of these online debates about race and culture, right, because online, everything is reduced to black and white, you are either racist or you are not, you are either sensitive or you are thick-skinned, but in real life, human interactions are so much more nuanced, they are filled with these gray areas, these misunderstandings that can be resolved with empathy and communication, but we have lost the patience for that, we just want to react, to judge, to condemn, and I think the media also plays a big role in this, because for a long time, the local media, the dramas, the movies, they relied heavily on racial stereotypes to create comedy or drama, like the loud, aggressive Indian uncle, the strict, kiasu Chinese mother, the lazy, Malay worker, and while I know it was meant to be harmless fun, or a reflection of the ground reality at the time, it still reinforced these biases in people's minds, and it took a very long time for the media to start moving away from these caricatures, and start portraying minorities as complex, multi-dimensional characters, which I think is crucial for changing the societal narrative.

Ya, the media representation is very important, because for a lot of people, especially those who live in very homogenous environments, the media is their only window into other cultures, so if the media keeps showing them these one-dimensional stereotypes, that is all they will know, and that is how prejudices are formed, but I think we are seeing a shift now, with the rise of independent content creators, with platforms like YouTube and TikTok, where people from all backgrounds can tell their own stories, in their own voices, without having to go through the traditional gatekeepers, and I think that is very empowering, because it allows for a much more diverse, authentic representation of our society, but at the same time, it also means that there is a lot more noise, a lot more conflicting narratives, and it can be very overwhelming for the average person to sift through it all, so I think we need to develop a level of media literacy, where we can critically evaluate the content we consume, and understand the context behind the stories being told.

And I think this ties back to the whole concept of the CMIO model, right, the Chinese, Malay, Indian, Others classification, which was very useful for the early years of nation-building, because it provided a clear framework for policies, for representation, for ensuring that no group was left behind, but I think as our society becomes more complex, as we have more mixed-race marriages, more third-culture kids, more expats who have lived here for decades, this rigid box system is starting to feel a bit outdated, a bit restrictive, because people's identities are not so easily categorized, they are fluid, they are hybrid, they are a mix of different cultural influences, and by forcing them into these neat little boxes, we are actually denying the reality of their lived experiences, and I think there is a growing demand, especially among the younger generation, to move towards a more inclusive, more flexible understanding of identity, one that is based on shared values and shared experiences, rather than just racial or religious labels.

But at the same time, you cannot just dismiss the CMIO model completely, right, because it is still very much embedded in our policies, in our self-help groups like CDAC, MENDAKI, SINDA, which were set up to address the specific socio-economic challenges faced by each community, and if you just abolish the racial categories, you might lose the ability to target these interventions effectively, you might end up with a situation where the structural disadvantages faced by certain groups are masked by the overall national average, which is a very real concern, because even though Singapore is very wealthy, the wealth is not distributed equally, and there are still significant gaps in income, in education, in home ownership between the different racial groups, so I think the challenge is not to get rid of the racial categories, but to find a way to use them to promote equity, without reinforcing the silos and the divisions, which is a very delicate balancing act, and I don't think we have figured out the perfect formula yet.

Exactly, and I think this is where the concept of meritocracy comes in, right, because we always pride ourselves on being a meritocratic society, where everyone has an equal opportunity to succeed, regardless of their race or background, but the reality is, the starting line is not the same for everyone, like, a child born into a wealthy, English-speaking Chinese family in the central region, they have a very different set of advantages compared to a child born into a low-income, Malay-speaking family in the heartlands, so even if the system is completely fair, even if the exams are completely blind, the outcomes will still be skewed, because the inputs are so different, and I think this is a very uncomfortable truth that we need to confront, because as long as we pretend that everyone is starting from the same place, we will never be able to address the root causes of the inequality, and we will just keep blaming the individuals for not working hard enough, which is not only unfair, but also very divisive.

Wah, you going into the sociology of education now hor, but it is very relevant, because I think a lot of the racial tensions that we see, they are not actually about race, they are about class, they are about economic anxiety, because when people feel like they are struggling, when they feel like they are being left behind, they start looking for someone to blame, and it is very easy to blame the 'other' race, to say, 'Eh, they are getting all the help, they are getting all the scholarships, while we have to struggle on our own,' and this is the kind of narrative that is very dangerous, because it pits the different communities against each other, and it distracts from the real issue, which is the widening wealth gap, the lack of social mobility, the high cost of living, so I think if we want to truly achieve racial harmony, we also need to address these economic inequalities, because you cannot have a harmonious society when a large segment of the population is feeling economically marginalized and insecure.

And I think this economic anxiety is also fueling the anti-immigrant sentiment, right, because when the economy is good, when everyone is getting a bonus, people are more welcoming, more open to the idea of foreigners coming in to contribute to the economy, but when the economy slows down, when people feel like their jobs are being threatened, when they see the housing prices going up, they start to resent the foreigners, they start to feel like their own culture, their own identity is being diluted, and I think this is a very natural human reaction, this instinct to protect your own tribe, but in a globalized city-state like Singapore, where we rely so heavily on foreign talent, on tourism, on international trade, we cannot afford to give in to this xenophobia, because it will hurt our economy, it will hurt our global reputation, so we have to constantly remind ourselves of the pragmatic realities of our situation, and find a way to integrate the newcomers, to help them understand our local norms and values, while also being open to the new perspectives and skills that they bring.

Ya, the integration of foreigners is a very hot topic, and I think it is a two-way street, right, the foreigners need to make an effort to understand our culture, to respect our laws, to learn a bit of Singlish, to blend in, but at the same time, we as locals also need to be more welcoming, more understanding of the culture shock that they are going through, because I know a lot of expats who come here, they are very willing to integrate, they want to make local friends, they want to understand the ground reality, but they are often met with this very cold, very transactional attitude from the locals, who just see them as a source of income, or as a competitor for jobs, and that is very discouraging for them, and it creates this 'us versus them' mentality, which is not helpful for anyone, so I think we need to cultivate a more inclusive mindset, one that recognizes that being Singaporean is not just about your race, or your birth certificate, but about your commitment to this society, your willingness to contribute, to belong, to call this little red dot your home.

And I think this brings us back to the core question of what it means to be Singaporean, right, because if you ask ten different people, you will get ten different answers, some will say it is about the food, some will say it is about the safety, the cleanliness, the efficiency, some will say it is about the Singlish, the kiasu culture, the void deck gatherings, but I think at the end of the day, being Singaporean is about this shared experience of navigating this complex, multi-cultural, multi-religious society, it is about the daily, conscious effort to live together, to compromise, to find common ground, despite all the differences, and I think that is a very unique, very precious identity, one that we should not take for granted, because if you look at the rest of the world, so many countries are tearing themselves apart over racial and religious differences, and here we are, still managing to hold it together, not because we are perfect, but because we have this pragmatic, this survivalist mindset that tells us we have no choice but to make it work.

Wah, you ending on a very philosophical note hor, but I agree, I think this 'survivalist mindset' is very ingrained in our DNA, because we are this tiny little island with no natural resources, surrounded by bigger, more powerful neighbors, so we know that if we start fighting among ourselves, if we let the racial and religious fault lines crack open, we will be finished, so we have to constantly work at this harmony, it is not a static state, it is not something you achieve once and then you can just relax, it is a daily, continuous effort, it is about choosing to be patient when someone cuts you off in traffic, it is about choosing to smile and say 'never mind' when someone makes an insensitive comment, it is about choosing to see the humanity in the other person, even when their culture, their beliefs, their way of life is completely different from yours, and I think that is the true beauty of our society, not that we don't have differences, but that we have learned how to navigate them, how to celebrate them, and how to use them to make our collective identity richer, more vibrant, more resilient.

Exactly lah, and I think as we move forward, as we face new challenges, whether it is the aging population, the digital disruption, the climate crisis, we are going to need this resilience, this ability to come together across racial and religious lines, more than ever, because these are not issues that respect racial boundaries, these are issues that affect all of us, regardless of whether you are Chinese, Malay, Indian, or Eurasian, so I think the future of our multicultural society depends on our ability to shift our focus from our differences to our shared challenges, to our shared aspirations, and to build a sense of solidarity that is based on our common humanity, rather than just our racial or religious identities, and I think the younger generation, for all their flaws, for all their hyper-sensitivity, they actually have a very strong sense of social justice, they care deeply about these issues, they want to build a more equitable, more inclusive society, so if we can harness that energy, if we can guide them, mentor them, help them understand the nuances of our local context, I think we have a very bright future ahead of us.

I hope so lah, I really hope so, because sometimes I look at the online discourse, and I get very worried, I get very sian, because it seems like everyone is just shouting at each other, no one is listening, no one is willing to compromise, but then I go down to the ground, I go to the hawker centre, I go to the park, and I see the uncle and auntie of different races, sitting together, drinking kopi, playing chess, complaining about the weather, and I realize that the real Singapore, the real harmony, is not found in the Twitter threads or the Facebook comments, it is found in these small, everyday moments of connection, these quiet, unspoken acts of tolerance and acceptance, and as long as that ground reality is still there, I think we will be okay, we will be able to weather any storm, any challenge that comes our way, because at the end of the day, we are all just trying to survive, to thrive, to find a bit of happiness in this little red dot, and we need each other to do that.

So today we jumping straight into the topic lah, right? Local habits. Because I was thinking, you know, when we talk about Singaporean identity, we always talk about the big things, like the economy, the housing, the multiracialism. But what really defines us on a day-to-day basis are these tiny, weird, highly specific local habits that we all do, but we never really stop to analyze why we do them. Like, why are we so obsessed with queueing? Why do we treat a packet of tissue paper like it is a legally binding contract for a table at a hawker centre? These are the things that actually make us, us.B: Wah, you touching on the deep stuff already hor. But I think the tissue paper thing, the chope culture, that is like the ultimate Singaporean survival skill. You think about it, we are a small island, very high density, everyone is always in a rush, everyone is always competing for space. So the chope culture is basically our way of claiming territory in a very polite, very passive-aggressive way. You put a packet of tissue on the table, and you are saying, 'This space is mine, I have claimed it, please do not touch.' And the funny thing is, it works! Because every Singaporean respects the tissue. You will never see a local sit at a table that has a tissue on it. But if you put a ten-dollar note on the table, someone might just take the money and sit down. But the tissue? The tissue is sacred.

But you have to admit, it is a very flawed system, right? Because what if the person who chope the table never comes back? I have seen this so many times. You go to Maxwell or Newton, you see a table with an umbrella, a packet of tissue, and maybe an old magazine on it. You wait for ten minutes, fifteen minutes, nobody come. So are you allowed to sit? Technically, no, because the 'contract' is still active. But practically, if you are very hungry, and you are standing there with your tray of chicken rice, you are just going to move the tissue to the corner and sit down. And then the person comes back, and there is this whole awkward standoff. 'Eh, uncle, this one got people chope already.' 'Eh, I wait already half hour, nobody come, I sit already.' It is like a mini diplomatic crisis at the hawker centre.
