Exactly! And that is the beauty of it. It is this unspoken social contract that we all agree to follow, even though there is no law enforcing it. Well, actually, the government did try to enforce the tray return thing, and they put up signs saying you cannot chope with tissue, you must clear your tray first. But you know what? Singaporeans just ignore it. We still chope. Because the fear of losing your seat is much stronger than the fear of getting a fifty-dollar fine from a town council officer who is never around when you need him. We are very kiasu about our comfort, but we are also very good at finding loopholes in the rules.

Speaking of hawker centres, another habit that I think is very uniquely Singaporean is the way we complain about the food, but we still keep going back to eat it. You know what I mean? Like, my colleagues and I, we will spend forty-five minutes debating where to go for lunch. We will argue, 'Eh, that one too oily,' 'Eh, this one the uncle very stingy with the meat,' 'Eh, that place always very crowded, wait until we die also cannot get seat.' So we finally decide on a place, we walk there, we queue for twenty minutes, we sit down, we eat. And then what do we say? We say, 'Wah, today the noodles abit overcooked leh. Next time we go the other stall.' But then the next day, we go back to the exact same stall! Why? Because it is familiar, because it is convenient, and because deep down, we actually like it. We just need to complain, because complaining is a national sport. If we don't complain, we feel like we are not living life to the fullest.

I think the complaining is a way of managing expectations, you know? If you go in expecting it to be the best meal of your life, you will be disappointed. But if you go in already complaining that it is going to be salty or the portion is small, then when it is just 'okay', you feel like, 'Eh, not bad lah, quite shiok also.' It is a psychological defense mechanism. And also, it is a bonding exercise. You cannot bond with your colleagues by saying, 'Wah, this char kway teow is absolutely perfect, the wok hei is magnificent, I am in food heaven.' No, that one sound very weird, very pretentious. But if you say, 'Wah, this one damn oily, but the cockles quite fresh,' then everyone can nod and say, 'Ya lor, the cockles save it.' That is how we connect. Through shared, mild dissatisfaction.

That is so true. We are a very pragmatic people. We don't do extreme emotions, whether it is extreme joy or extreme anger. We just do this steady, mild complaining. And it applies to everything, not just food. The weather, for example. You step out of the office, it is 32 degrees, the humidity is like 90 percent, you are sweating through your shirt in two minutes. What do you say? You don't scream, 'Oh my god, the sun is killing me!' You just look at the sky, wipe your sweat, and say, 'Wah, today damn hot hor.' And the person next to you will say, 'Ya, very hot. Later sure rain.' And that is the entire conversation about the weather. It is very efficient.

But the weather habit is not just about complaining, it is about the preparation. The umbrella! Every Singaporean carries an umbrella. But it is not just for rain. It is a multi-tool. If it is sunny, you use it to block the UV rays, because we are very kiasi about getting tanned. We want to be white, you know? The 'Asian glow' is not a thing here, we want to be pale. So you see people walking with the umbrella tilted at a very specific angle to block the sun but still allow them to see the traffic. Then when it rains, you open it. And then if you are at the bus stop, and some random uncle is smoking, you use the umbrella to waft the smoke away from your face without actually confronting him. The umbrella is basically a shield against the elements and against other people's bad behavior.

And then you go from the 32-degree heat outside, into the MRT or the office, and the aircon is blasting at like 16 degrees. This is another huge habit. The 'layering' culture. You will see people walking around the CBD in the middle of the day wearing a thick cardigan, or a winter jacket, or even a scarf. Because they know that once they step into the office building, they are going to enter the Arctic circle. And the funny thing is, nobody questions it. You see a girl wearing a Uniqlo fleece jacket in July, and you don't think she is crazy. You think, 'Wah, her office aircon must be damn powerful.' We have completely normalized this extreme temperature fluctuation. Our bodies are just constantly in a state of thermal shock, but we just accept it as part of the Singaporean experience.

It is very true. We just adapt. We don't fight the environment; we just build workarounds. Like the ERP gantry. You know, the Electronic Road Pricing. Every other country has tolls, but they just pay it. In Singapore, the ERP is a psychological game. You see the gantry in the distance, and you start checking your cashcard balance. You are like, 'Eh, how much I got left? If I go through now, it will deduct two dollars fifty. But if I wait ten minutes, it will drop to one dollar fifty.' So you start slowing down, you start changing lanes, you start calculating the time-cost benefit of waiting ten minutes to save one dollar. And then the guy behind you start honking, because you are doing 40km/h in a 70km/h zone just because you are trying to time the ERP drop. It is this very specific, very local form of kancheong (anxiety). We are always calculating, always optimizing, always trying to squeeze out a few cents of savings, even if it costs us more in time and petrol.

Wah, the ERP habit is so real. I have this friend, he will literally take a detour through the small back roads of the heartlands just to avoid the ERP gantry on the CTE. He will add fifteen minutes to his journey, he will drive through all these small roads with the speed bumps and the school zones, but he will proudly say, 'Eh, I save three dollars lah.' And you are like, bro, your time is worth more than three dollars, and you are also wearing out your car's suspension on those speed bumps. But he doesn't care. To him, beating the system, avoiding the ERP, is a victory. It is the ultimate kiasu move. We don't want to pay for something if we can find a way to get it for free, even if the 'free' option is actually more expensive in the long run.

But that is the Singaporean mindset, right? We are very practical, very cost-conscious. Because we grew up in a society where resources are scarce, where land is expensive, where everything is imported. So every dollar counts. And that is why the queueing habit makes so much sense. When you see a queue, your brain immediately thinks, 'There must be a good deal at the end of this queue. There must be something worth my time and money.' And even if there isn't, the fear of missing out, the fear that everyone else is getting a good deal and you are not, is just too strong. You remember the Hello Kitty McDonald's disaster a few years ago? People were camping outside the drive-thru, they were fighting, they were tearing the glass, all for a tiny, cheap plush toy. It was completely siao. But it perfectly encapsulates our kiasu culture. If you don't queue, you lose face. If you don't get the limited edition item, you are left out of the conversation the next day at the office. 'Eh, you didn't get the Hello Kitty? Walao, you so slow.'

Yes, the 'bojio' culture. 'Bojio' means you didn't invite me. And it is the ultimate guilt trip in Singapore. If you go for a nice meal, or you buy a limited edition sneaker, and you don't post it on your story, or you don't invite your friends, you are a bad friend. We are very communal in our consumption. We don't just eat for ourselves; we eat to be seen eating, or to share the experience with the tribe. That is why the food reviews on TikTok and Instagram are so huge. Every weekend, you see these young people going to the newest, most aesthetic cafe in Tiong Bahru, taking fifty photos of their matcha latte, making sure the lighting is perfect, and then posting it with the caption, 'Finally tried this! Very shiok!' But you know they didn't even taste it properly because they were too busy arranging the croissant for the photo. But it is a habit. It is how we participate in the social fabric. If you are not consuming the same things as everyone else, you are invisible.

And it is not just the young people. The older generation also has their own version of this. The 'forwarding WhatsApp messages' habit. You know, the family group chat. Every morning, without fail, your auntie or your uncle will forward a message. It is usually a blurry picture of a flower with a quote about 'Good Morning' in a very weird font, or it is a fake news article about how eating this specific vegetable will cure cancer, or how the government is going to start charging you for breathing air. And you just have to reply with a thumbs-up emoji. You cannot argue with them, because if you argue, they will say you are unfilial, or you are being disrespectful to your elders. So you just swallow your frustration, you type 'Thank you Auntie, have a blessed day', and you mute the group chat. It is a daily ritual of submission.

But you have to admit, some of those WhatsApp messages are actually quite useful. Like the ones about the flash floods, or the traffic accidents. 'Eh, don't go PIE, got lorry overturned, jam until next week.' That one is very helpful. The Singaporean network is very efficient when it comes to sharing practical information. We are like a giant, decentralized early warning system. If there is a free sample being given out at a supermarket in Jurong East, within fifteen minutes, the WhatsApp groups will know. If there is a rogue taxi driver scamming tourists at the airport, within an hour, the whole of Singapore knows. We are very connected, very nosy, but in a way that keeps the community informed. It is the digital version of the kampung spirit, but instead of sharing a bowl of sugar, we are sharing links to Shopee sales and fake health advice.

That is a very good point. The kampung spirit hasn't disappeared; it has just evolved. It has moved from the void deck to the WhatsApp group chat. And it is also very evident in the way we handle public behavior. The 'vigilante' culture. You know, if someone is doing something wrong on the MRT, like putting their feet on the seat, or eating a forbidden durian, or even just talking very loudly, what do Singaporeans do? We don't confront them directly. No, that one is too confrontational, too risky. What we do is we take out our phones, we take a photo or a video, and we send it to Stomp, or we post it on the Singapore Road Tax subreddit, or we send it to Lianhe Zaobao. We outsource the shaming to the internet. 'Eh, look at this guy, so rude, put feet on the seat, shame him!' And then the internet will come in, they will dox the guy, they will write a very long, angry comment about the decline of moral values in Singapore. And we feel satisfied. We have done our civic duty. We have maintained the social order, without having to actually look the person in the eye and say, 'Eh, brother, put your feet down.'

Wah, the Stomp culture is so real. But it is very hypocritical also, right? Because the same person who takes a photo of someone littering and sends it to the news, will later go to the hawker centre and leave their tray on the table, or will drop a candy wrapper on the floor when they think nobody is looking. We are very good at policing others, but we are very bad at policing ourselves. We have this very strong sense of justice, but it is very selective. It only applies when it is someone else's bad behavior. When it is our own bad behavior, it is just 'small thing only', 'never mind lah', 'everyone do also'. It is this very strange cognitive dissonance. We want a clean, orderly, disciplined society, but we also want the freedom to bend the rules when it is convenient for us.

I think that is because we are a very rule-heavy society. From the time we are born, we are told what to do, what not to do, how to queue, how to cross the road, how to recycle. There are so many rules, so many fines. So naturally, the human reaction is to look for the loopholes, to find the blind spots. If the government says 'Do not litter', we will not litter in front of the CCTV. But if we are in a dark alley, or if we are in a taxi, we will throw the tissue paper out the window. Because the rule is not internalized as a moral principle; it is just an external constraint to be avoided. We behave well because we are afraid of the fine, not because we actually care about the environment. And that is why the tray return law had to be implemented. Because if you rely on our moral compass, we will just leave the tray. But if you threaten us with a fine, and you put an auntie there to watch us, suddenly we become the most environmentally conscious people in the world. We need the stick. The carrot doesn't work on us.

That is a very cynical but very accurate observation. We are a pragmatic people, and we respond to incentives and penalties. But I think there is also a soft side to our habits. Like the way we treat children. You see a cute baby on the MRT, and suddenly all the strict, kiasu Singaporeans turn into softies. The uncle who was just scolding you for not returning your tray will suddenly start making funny faces at the baby, doing the 'chee chee chee' sound, and asking, 'Wah, so cute ah, how many months already? Got eat rice already or not?' And the auntie who was judging your outfit will suddenly offer the baby a piece of candy, or ask you for parenting advice. We are very competitive, very stressed, very focused on the KPIs of life, but when it comes to the next generation, we have this collective, unspoken agreement to protect and nurture them. Even the grumpiest Singaporean has a soft spot for a child.

Ya, the kids are the one thing that can unite us. But at the same time, we also project all our kiasu anxiety onto them. The 'enrichment class' habit. You go to any tuition centre in Bishan or Bukit Timah on a Saturday, and you see these primary school kids, maybe seven, eight years old, carrying a backpack that is bigger than their body, looking like they haven't slept in a week. And the parents are outside, drinking kopi, comparing notes. 'Eh, your son doing what? Math? Oh, my daughter doing Math, Science, and Mandarin. She also got piano on Sunday.' It is this unspoken arms race. We want our kids to be happy, but we are also terrified that if they don't go to the right tuition centre, they will fail the PSLE, and then they will not get into a good secondary school, and then they will end up collecting cardboard. It is this very extreme, very local form of parental anxiety. We love our kids so much that we accidentally make their childhood very miserable.

But you cannot blame the parents, right? Because the system is very competitive. The PSLE is a high-stakes exam, and it determines the trajectory of the child's life. So of course the parents are going to do everything they can to give their child an edge. Even if that edge is just sending them to a 'brain training' class that is basically just teaching them how to guess the multiple-choice answers. It is a habit of survival. We are a small island with no natural resources. Our only resource is our people. So we have to optimize our people from a very young age. It is very sad, but it is the reality of the Singaporean experience. We are always optimizing, always upgrading, always trying to be one step ahead. Even when we are on holiday. You go to Japan, and you see Singaporeans rushing from one temple to another, taking photos, eating, and then rushing to the next spot. They are not relaxing; they are executing an itinerary. They are trying to maximize the ROI of their holiday. 'I paid two thousand dollars for this trip, I must see everything, I must eat everything, I must not waste a single minute.' We don't know how to just 'be'. We always have to 'do'.

That is so true. We cannot sit still. Even when we are trying to relax, we are doing it very efficiently. Like the 'staycation' habit. Instead of just staying at home and resting, which is free, we will book a hotel room down the road for three hundred dollars a night, just to experience a different environment. We order room service, we use the pool, we take photos in the bathrobe. We are paying a premium to pretend we are on holiday, while still being in our own country. Because going overseas is too much hassle, the flight is too long, the exchange rate is bad. So we bring the holiday to us. But even then, we are still calculating. 'Eh, this hotel include breakfast, so we don't need to eat outside, save money.' We are always balancing the desire for comfort with the need to be practical. It is this constant tug-of-war between our desire to enjoy life and our ingrained habit of saving for a rainy day.

And that rainy day always comes, right? Because the cost of living is always going up. So we have this habit of 'collecting' things. The freebies, the discounts, the loyalty points. You go to the supermarket, and you see this auntie carefully cutting out the stamps from the brochure to redeem a free plastic bowl. She is spending twenty minutes to save five dollars. And you think, her time is worth more than that. But to her, it is a victory. She beat the system. She got something for free. And then she goes home, and she puts the plastic bowl in her cupboard, next to the fifty other plastic bowls she got from previous promotions. We are a nation of hoarders, but we don't call it hoarding. We call it 'being practical', 'not wasteful'. We cannot throw things away, because 'still can use', 'sian jian' (waste not). So our houses are filled with Tupperware containers that have no lids, cables for devices we don't own anymore, and bags of bags. It is a very specific, very local form of attachment to material things, born out of a historical scarcity mindset.

But I think that habit is changing with the younger generation. The Gen Z and the millennials, they are more into minimalism, or at least they try to be. But they have their own weird habits. Like the 'cafe hopping' and the 'aesthetic' obsession. They don't collect plastic bowls; they collect experiences, but only if they look good on Instagram. And they are very vocal about social issues, which is different from the older generation who just keep quiet and complain in the family group chat. But they still have the same underlying kiasu DNA. They just express it differently. Instead of queueing for Hello Kitty, they are queueing for a limited edition Supreme brick, or they are fighting over a slot to join a specific spin class. The object of the queue changes, but the psychology is exactly the same. We are wired to want what everyone else wants, because in a small, crowded society, being different is risky. Being part of the herd is safe.

That is a very deep analysis. The herd mentality. We like to think we are individualistic, we like to complain about how everyone is the same, but we are actually very conformist. We follow the trends, we follow the rules, we follow the queue. Because the alternative is to stand out, and in Singapore, standing out can be dangerous. It can lead to social ostracization, or worse, a visit from the authorities. So we have developed this very specific set of habits that allow us to navigate this high-density, high-pressure society without causing too much friction. We chope our tables, we queue for our food, we complain about the weather, we pay our ERP, we forward our WhatsApp messages. These are not just random behaviors; they are the survival mechanisms of a people who have learned how to thrive in a very constrained environment.

Wah, you make it sound so poetic. The survival mechanisms of the Singaporean. But I think you are right. At the end of the day, these habits, whether it is the kiasu queueing, the hawker centre etiquette, or the WhatsApp forwarding, they are what bind us together. They are the glue of our society. Because when you see someone doing these things, you know they are one of us. You might not know their name, you might not know their race, you might not know their religion, but if you see them carefully folding their umbrella before entering the MRT, or if you see them arguing with the taxi uncle about the shortest route, you know, 'Okay, this one is a local.' It is this unspoken recognition. We are a rojak of different cultures, different backgrounds, but we share this very specific, very weird, very wonderful set of habits that make us Singaporean.

Ya, the rojak identity. A bit messy, a bit mixed, but very delicious if you know how to appreciate it. And I think that is the key. We have to appreciate our own habits, even the annoying ones. Because if we don't, who will? The foreigners will just see us as this efficient, robotic, kiasu bunch of people who never smile. But they don't see the warmth behind the complaining. They don't see the community behind the queueing. They don't see the love behind the auntie scolding you to return your tray. We have to own our habits. We have to be proud of our weirdness. Because in a world that is becoming more and more homogenized, where every city looks the same, where every mall has the same shops, our weird, specific, local habits are the only things that make us unique.

Very well said. So to all our listeners out there, the next time you are standing in a queue for a drink you don't even like, or the next time you are carefully placing a packet of tissue on a table to reserve your seat, just remember, you are not just doing a random act. You are participating in a grand, national tradition. You are keeping the Singaporean spirit alive. You are saying, 'I am here, I am kiasu, and I am not moving until I get what I want.' And there is nothing wrong with that. In fact, it is what makes us who we are.

topic: News and discussion of current events
data: what's up everybody, welcome to another episode of the podcast where we talk about the hottest news and the most interesting people, all with a touch of what, good old humor, man, good old humor, yeah, but today we actually going to talk about the news itself, like the meta of consuming news in Singapore, because I was thinking the other day, right, I was scrolling through my phone at like 2 AM, and I realized I was reading the same three articles from three different platforms, but the comments section for each one was like a completely different universe, you know what I mean? Like you go the mainstream media website, the comments are all very polite, very 'thank you government for the update, very informative', but then you go Reddit, wah lao, everyone is like calculating the GST impact until the year 2050, and then you go the WhatsApp family group, your auntie is forwarding some fake news about how drinking warm water with salt can cure inflation, and I was just sitting there thinking, like, how do we even process all this information, like our brains are just constantly in this state of kancheong, always waiting for the next policy announcement, the next ERP rate change, the next BTO flat launch, and it just makes you wonder, are we actually informed citizens, or are we just highly anxious consumers of doom-scrolling content, because it feels like every week there is some new crisis, some new scandal, or some new minister saying something that gets taken out of context and turned into a meme, and we just have to keep up, we have to keep reading, keep commenting, keep arguing, until we are so tired that we just order GrabFood and watch Netflix to numb our brains, which is very sad, but also very true, right?B: Ya, ya, I totally get what you're saying, and I think that's exactly why we do this show, right, because if we don't laugh about it, we will just cry, or worse, we will start arguing with our relatives during Chinese New Year, which is a lose-lose situation for everyone involved, because you know how it is, you sit down at the round table, the Yu Sheng is still being tossed, and suddenly your uncle starts talking about how the economy is going to crash because of some geopolitical thing he read on Facebook, and you are just like, 'Uncle, please, let us just eat the fish, it is getting soggy', but they don't listen, they just keep forwarding the articles to the family group chat, and then the next day, your mom will ask you, 'Eh, you see the message ah? Is it true ah? The government going to take away our CPF is it?' and you have to spend the next twenty minutes explaining that no, it is just a clickbait article from some random website, but they still don't believe you, because they trust the WhatsApp forward more than they trust you, which is very painful, but also very funny in a dark way, because it shows you how much people are just looking for confirmation of their own fears, they don't want the actual news, they want the news that confirms their bias, and that is the real challenge of being a news consumer in Singapore today, you have to navigate through all this noise, all this fake news, all this emotional clickbait, just to find the actual facts, and by the time you find the facts, you are already so exhausted that you just say, 'Wah lao, never mind lah, I just go buy lottery instead', which is probably what most people do anyway, right?

Exactly, exactly, the lottery mentality, because if the world is going to end, or if the economy is going to crash, might as well try our luck, right? But I think the interesting thing about the Singaporean news consumer is that we are very pragmatic, very 'show me the money' kind of people, so when the news comes out about CPF changes or whatever, we don't just read the headline and get emotional, no, we open up Excel, we start doing the compound interest calculations, we compare with what our parents got in the 90s, and then we come to the conclusion that, okay, we are going to work until we are 75, and then we just accept it and move on, which is very Singaporean, very 'suan' culture, we calculate everything, we optimize everything, but at the same time, there is this underlying current of frustration, like, why is everything so expensive, why is the kopi going up by 20 cents, and we will complain for three days, we will post on Reddit, we will write a long paragraph about the cost of living, but then we still go the same kopitiam and order the same kopi because where else to go, right? You cannot suddenly start brewing your own coffee at home, you don't have the time, you don't have the energy, you just want the uncle to pour it from that height, the 'tarik' style, even though it is just kopi, not teh tarik, but you know what I mean, the ritual is important, and the news is just like that, we complain about the news, we complain about the media, we complain about the politicians, but we still consume it every single day, because it is part of our daily ritual, like the morning kopi, you need your dose of news to feel like you are part of the society, otherwise you feel left out, you feel like 'bo jio', everyone is talking about the new policy at the office pantry, and you are just standing there like a zombie, so you have to read it, you have to know it, even if it just makes you more stressed, which is a very vicious cycle, but it is the cycle we live in, right?

Ya lor, the vicious cycle of the morning kopi and the morning news, but I think we also need to talk about the contrast, right, because one minute you are reading about some huge geopolitical crisis, like the war in Europe or the tensions in the Middle East, and it is very heavy, very serious, and you are like, 'Wah, the world is going to end, how to survive?', and then you scroll down, and the next article is about a wild boar family causing a traffic jam on the ECP, or some uncle falling asleep on the MRT and snoring so loud that the whole carriage has to move to another cabin, and you are just like, 'Wait, what? This is the same website? This is the same news app?', and it just gives you whiplash, because you are trying to process the fall of global democracy, but then you are also forced to process the fact that a wild boar is more important than the morning peak hour traffic, and I think that is the unique experience of consuming news in Singapore, we are so small, so constrained, that the global news and the local news just mash together in this very weird, very disjointed way, and you don't know whether to cry or to laugh, because on one hand, you are worried about the global supply chain affecting the price of chicken rice, but on the other hand, you are also very annoyed that the wild boar is making you late for your meeting, so your priorities are just completely messed up, but that is survival, right? You have to care about the big things, but you also have to care about the small things, because the small things are the ones that actually affect your daily life, you cannot eat global democracy, but you can eat chicken rice, so if the chicken rice price goes up, you will riot, but if the global supply chain collapses, you will just say, 'Alamak, no more chicken, I eat fish ball noodle instead', which is very pragmatic, very Singaporean, and I think that is why we need comedy, we need to laugh about it, because if we take everything so seriously, we will just go crazy, right?

Wah, you talking about the wild boar, I think we should actually do our 'one-shot comment' segment right now, because I saw this article yesterday, and I was just dying of laughter, it was about this wild boar that somehow managed to get into a premium condo in District 10, like Bukit Timah, where the houses cost like twenty million dollars, and the boar was just swimming in the pool, and the residents were calling the police, calling the animal rescue, everyone was panicking, like 'Oh my god, the property value is going to drop, the boar is looking at my Tesla', and I was just thinking, like, this boar probably has better taste in real estate than most of us, right? It just walked in, saw the pool, said 'Wah, shiok', and just started swimming, while the owners were probably in their air-conditioned living room, calling their lawyers to see if they can sue the boar for trespassing, which is just so atas, so typical of the District 10 mindset, you know what I mean? Like, you can't just call the uncle to come and catch the boar, no, you need a specialized task force, you need to ensure the boar doesn't scratch the marble flooring, and the comments section was just gold, everyone was saying, 'Eh, the boar also know which condo is good, next time I also want to be a boar', or 'Can the boar pay the maintenance fee already or not?', and it just shows you that no matter how serious the news is, Singaporeans will always find a way to make it about money, about property, about the kiasu need to protect our investment, even if the intruder is just a wild animal looking for a quick dip, and I think that is the beauty of our local news, it is so hyper-specific, so grounded in our daily anxieties, that you can't help but laugh, because it is just so ridiculous, but also so true to who we are, we are a people who will chope a table with a tissue packet, and we will also call the police on a wild boar for swimming in our pool, it is just the duality of the Singaporean experience, right?

Haha, ya, the duality of the Singaporean experience, from the void deck to the District 10 pool, and I think that is exactly why we can't just rely on the mainstream media to tell us who we are, because the mainstream media will just report, 'Wild boar enters condo, residents evacuated', very factual, very dry, but they miss the soul of the story, they miss the fact that the boar was probably just escaping the heat, just like all of us, and it just wanted to enjoy the facilities that we are all paying so much maintenance fee for, but we are too busy working to enjoy it, so the boar is just living our dream, right? And that is the kind of nuance that you only get when you talk about it, when you banter about it, when you bring in the humor, because the news is not just about the facts, it is about how the facts make us feel, and in Singapore, the facts usually make us feel stressed, make us feel anxious, make us feel like we are not doing enough, not earning enough, not saving enough, so if we can just take a step back, look at the wild boar in the pool, and laugh about it, then maybe, just maybe, we can survive the week without having a mental breakdown, and that is the public service we are trying to provide, right? We are not here to give you the facts, you can get the facts from CNA, you can get the facts from the Straits Times, we are here to give you the feeling, the vibe, the 'good old humor' that helps you process the facts, because let's be honest, if you just read the news every day, you will think Singapore is just a bunch of people running around in a panic, chasing after BTO flats, complaining about the ERP, and fighting over the last chicken rice at the hawker centre, which is true, but it is also a very beautiful, very chaotic, very vibrant truth, and we need to celebrate that chaos, not just report it like a robot, right?

Ya, ya, celebrate the chaos, I like that, because I think a lot of people misunderstand what we do, they think, 'Eh, you guys are just a comedy podcast, you don't talk about serious issues', but I think talking about serious issues with comedy is actually the most serious thing you can do, because it means you are brave enough to look at the ugly, the awkward, the uncomfortable parts of our society, and say, 'Hey, this is weird, let's talk about it', instead of just sweeping it under the rug like we usually do, because Singaporeans are very kiasi about causing offense, very kiasi about rocking the boat, so we just keep quiet, we just nod and smile, and then we go to Reddit and type our angry anonymous comments, which is very passive-aggressive, very toxic, but if we can just bring it out into the open, if we can just joke about it, then it loses its power, it becomes just another part of the shared experience, like the weather, like the MRT breakdown, like the fact that you can never find a parking lot at VivoCity on a weekend, these are the things that bind us together, not just the national day parade, not just the singing of the national anthem, but the shared trauma of the daily commute, the shared anxiety of the cost of living, and the shared laughter when we see a wild boar swimming in a twenty-million-dollar condo pool, and I think that is a very powerful thing, it is a very human thing, and it is something that AI cannot replicate, which brings me to my next point, because I was reading this article about AI news presenters, and I was just thinking, wah, if AI takes over the news, how is it going to understand the wild boar story? It will just say, 'Wild boar causes property value depreciation', it won't understand the joke about the boar paying the maintenance fee, it won't understand the sigh of the uncle who just wants to eat his chicken rice in peace, and that is why we are safe, right? Because AI doesn't have a soul, it doesn't have the 'sian' factor, it doesn't know what it feels like to stand in the queue for forty-five minutes for a drink that is just average, so it can never truly understand the Singaporean condition, which is a very strange thing to be proud of, but I am proud of it, right?

Wah, you going deep already hor, the AI cannot understand the 'sian' factor, that is very profound, but I think you are right, because the 'sian' factor, the 'kancheong' factor, these are very emotional, very cultural things that you cannot code into an algorithm, you can code it to say 'Cost of living increases by 2 percent', but you cannot code it to say 'Wah lao, the kopi also go up, how to live ah?', that requires a certain level of existential dread that only a Singaporean can truly feel, because we are so used to having everything so efficient, so clean, so predictable, that when something goes wrong, even a small thing like the price of kopi, it feels like a personal attack, like the universe is against us, and we need to complain about it, we need to share it with our friends, we need to post it on social media, just to feel like we are not alone in this struggle, and that is the community aspect of the news, it is not just information, it is a social glue, it is the thing that we talk about at the void deck, at the hawker centre, at the office pantry, it is the common language that allows us to connect with strangers, because if you see someone else complaining about the ERP rates, you immediately bond with them, you are like, 'Eh, same here, I also take the CTE every day, I also cry every time I pass the gantry', and that is a very powerful connection, something that AI can never understand, because AI doesn't pay ERP, AI doesn't have to chope a table at Maxwell, AI doesn't have to deal with the auntie who scolds you for not returning your tray, so AI can never be truly Singaporean, which means our jobs are safe, at least for now, until they invent a robot that can complain about the weather while holding a packet of tissue, but I think that day is still far away, right?

Haha, ya, the robot holding the tissue packet, that is the ultimate singularity, the day the AI achieves consciousness and its first act is to chope a table at the hawker centre, that is the day we know we are truly doomed, but until then, we have to keep doing this, we have to keep talking, keep joking, keep processing the news in our own weird, own chaotic, own very Singaporean way, and I think that is what our listeners appreciate, they don't come here for the hard-hitting journalism, they come here for the banter, for the relatability, for the feeling that someone out there understands their pain, understands their 'sian', understands their frustration with the system, but also loves this little red dot despite all its flaws, because at the end of the day, where else are you going to go? You can complain about Singapore, you can complain about the heat, you can complain about the kiasu people, you can complain about the expensive cars, the small flats, the strict laws, but when you go overseas, and you eat a meal that is not shiok, and you drink a coffee that is not properly 'tarik', and you see people jaywalking without looking, you suddenly miss the efficiency, you miss the safety, you miss the fact that you can walk around at 3 AM without being scared, and you realize, 'Wah, actually Singapore not so bad lah', and that is the love-hate relationship we all have, and it is the love-hate relationship that fuels our news, fuels our comedy, fuels our podcasts, because we care, we care so much that it hurts, and we just want to make it better, or at least we want to laugh about it so it doesn't hurt so much, right?

Ya, ya, the love-hate relationship, the 'can complain but cannot leave' mentality, which is very true for a lot of us, because we are so rooted here, our families are here, our food is here, our memories are here, so we are stuck in this very complex, very emotional bond with the country, and the news is just a reflection of that bond, it is the daily reminder of what is working, what is not working, what is changing, and what is staying the same, and I think as long as we keep talking about it, as long as we keep questioning it, as long as we keep laughing about the wild boars in the District 10 pools and the uncles sleeping on the MRT, we are keeping the spirit of Singapore alive, the real Singapore, not the one in the brochures, not the one in the official speeches, but the real, messy, noisy, vibrant Singapore that we all live in every single day, and that is a very important thing to preserve, especially in a world that is changing so fast, where everything is becoming so automated, so standardized, so devoid of character, we need to hold on to our quirks, our slang, our 'lah' and 'leh' and 'lor', our obsession with food, our kiasu nature, our ability to find humor in the most stressful situations, because that is what makes us human, that is what makes us Singaporean, and that is what we are going to keep bringing to you every single week, with a touch of what, good old humor, man, good old humor, yeah, so before we wrap up, I just want to say thank you to all the listeners out there, thank you for tolerating our rants, thank you for sending in your Telegram messages, thank you for arguing with us in the Reddit comments, because without you, we are just two guys talking to ourselves in a studio, which is actually what we are doing, but with you, it feels like a conversation, it feels like a community, and that is very shiok, very heartwarming, so thank you, and we will see you in the next episode, where we will probably talk about some other crazy news, some other wild boar, some other ERP rate hike, or some other WhatsApp forward from your auntie, so stay tuned, stay kiasu, and remember to chope your seats with tissue paper, bye everybody!

topic: The older generation and elderly care
data: Hello everyone, welcome back to the podcast. Today we got a very, very heavy topic, but I think it is something we all need to talk about, especially since we are in our late twenties, early thirties now. We starting to see our parents get older, our grandparents even older. So today we talking about the older generation and elderly care in Singapore. B: Wah, straight into the deep end already hor. But ya, I think it is very relevant. Because lately I always see these news articles about the silver tsunami, the aging population, and I just think, wait, I still haven't even figure out my own career path, now I need to think about how to take care of my parents when they retire? It is very sian already just thinking about it.

Sian is one thing, but the reality is very scary. You look at the math, right? CPF, Medishield Life, the retirement sum. The government always say we need to save more, top up more. But for the older generation, especially the ones in their sixties, seventies now, they don't have this CPF buffer like us. They rely on cash, they rely on their kids. And that is where the friction come in.

Exactly. And it is not just about money. It is about the lifestyle gap. Like, just last week, I bring my grandma to the polyclinic. Right? And I try to teach her how to use the self-service kiosk to collect the queue number. She just stare at the screen, then she look at me, and she say, 'If I press the wrong button, the machine will explode or not?'

Hahaha! Alamak, that one is the classic fear. They think the digital world is very fragile. One wrong press, money gone, or the whole system crash.

Ya lor! And I try to explain, 'No lah, popo, it is just a touch screen, cannot break one.' But her hands shaking already. She so afraid of doing the wrong thing. And then the auntie behind her in the queue start to sigh, very loud, very obvious. And my grandma immediately step aside, say, 'Never mind, never mind, you go first. I wait for the human counter.'

That one is the digital divide, man. It is very real. We always talk about Smart Nation, everything must be app, everything must be digital. Singpass, parking app, order food at the hawker centre using the QR code. Very convenient for us. But for them? It is like a wall. They feel left out. They feel stupid. And that feeling of incompetence, it really eat into their confidence.

I think that is the most sad part. You know, our parents' generation, they are the ones who build this country. They work so hard, they sacrifice so much. Now suddenly, they cannot even order a kopi at the kopitiam because they don't have the app, or they cannot pay because nobody take cash already. They feel like a burden. They feel like they don't belong in this new Singapore.

Ya, and the hawker centre is the best example. You go there now, half the stalls got the self-ordering kiosk. You see the uncle and auntie stand there, looking at the screen, then look at the queue, then look at the screen again. Very cheong. They don't want to hold up the queue, so they just walk away. They go to the stall that still accept cash, even if the queue is twenty people long.

But you cannot blame the hawker owners also. They want to save cost, they want to be efficient. The margin for char kway teow is what, like twenty cents per plate. They cannot afford to have a cashier just to take the cash. So the system push the elderly out. It is very pragmatic, very Singaporean, but very cruel at the same time.

So what is the solution? We cannot just say, 'Eh, you old already, you go learn.' You cannot teach an eighty-year-old to use an iPad overnight. We need more patience. We need the community to step in. Like the digital ambassadors at the CC, the Community Centres. I see these young volunteers, they sit down, they teach the aunties how to use WhatsApp, how to video call their grandchildren.

That one is good, but is it enough? Because the volunteers come and go. Once the event finish, the auntie go home, she forget already. She don't dare to press. The real solution must come from the family. We must be the ones to teach them. But who got time? We work until like dog, OT until midnight, come home just want to sleep. How you want to sit down for two hours to teach your dad how to reset his password?

That is the harsh reality of the sandwich generation. We squeezed in the middle. We need to care for our young kids, we need to care for our old parents, and we need to care for our own careers. Something got to give. And usually, the elderly care is the one that get sacrificed. We just give them money, we hire a helper, or we send them to an Active Ageing Centre during the day.

But sending them to a centre, is that enough? I mean, the Active Ageing Centres are quite good, they got activities, got exercise classes, got communal meals. But it is very structured. It is not the same as being at home, being part of the community. You know, the void deck culture.

Wah, the void deck culture. That one is dying already hor. You go to the void deck now, it is just people rushing to the MRT, or the kids playing on their iPads. The uncles playing chess, the aunties chatting while hanging laundry, that one is becoming rare. Because everyone stay in their own air-conditioned box.

But you see, the elderly, they thrive on that unstructured social interaction. They like to go down to the void deck, just sit on the bench, watch the world go by, complain about the weather, complain about the government. That is their social network. When you take that away, when you put them in a high-rise flat alone all day because the kids are working, they get depressed. They get lonely.

Loneliness is the real killer. I read this article recently, they say loneliness in the elderly is as bad as smoking fifteen cigarettes a day. It increases the risk of dementia, heart disease, everything. And in Singapore, we have so many elderly living alone. The kids migrated overseas, or the kids are busy working, or the spouse passed away. They just sit in that four-room flat, watching TV from morning to night.

And that is why they fall for scams! You think they stupid meh? They not stupid. They just lonely. The scammer call them, 'Hello auntie, I am from the bank, your account got problem.' The auntie know it might be fake, but the scammer is so polite, he call her every day, he ask her how she is, he listen to her complain about her knee pain. He give her the emotional support that her kids don't have time to give. So she transfer the money. Not because she believe the story, but because she want to keep talking to him.

Wah, that one is very dark, but very true. The psychology of the scam is not about greed, it is about connection. The scammers are basically monetizing their loneliness. And we, the society, we just blame the old people for being careless. 'Wah lao, so obvious also can fall?' But we don't ask why they are so vulnerable in the first place.

Exactly. We need to fill that emotional void. And it is not just about calling them once a week and ask, 'You eat already or not?' That one is very transactional. We need to actually engage with them. We need to find out what they like, what they used to do.

Speaking of engaging, what about the language barrier? This one I always feel very guilty about. My grandpa only speak Hokkien. My parents speak a bit of Hokkien, but they reply me in English or Singlish. Me? I only know like, 'Jiak bue', 'Gong ga', basic stuff. When I go visit my grandpa, we just sit there. He try to talk to me, I just smile and nod. I don't know what he saying.

That one is the dialect loss. It is a huge issue. The government promote Speak Mandarin Campaign in the 80s, they want to phase out the dialects. So the younger generation, we don't speak it. But the older generation, their mother tongue is the dialect. It is the language of their emotions, of their memories. When you force them to speak Mandarin or English, they feel like they lose their identity.

Ya, and a lot of the deep, cultural wisdom, the proverbs, the stories of how they survive during the Japanese occupation, or how they came to Singapore on a boat, all that is in the dialect. When they pass on, that language die with them. We lose that connection to our roots.

I try to learn a bit of Hokkien recently. I use this app, I watch some YouTube videos. But it is very hard. The tones, the slang. And my grandpa always laugh at me. He say my accent very 'ang moh'. But I see his eyes light up when I try to speak. Even if I say it wrong, he happy that I am making the effort.

That is the key, man. The effort. They don't expect us to be fluent. They just want to know that we care enough to try. Because for them, language is love. You know how Asian parents are, they don't say 'I love you'. They say, 'You want to eat what? I go cook for you.' Or they cut the fruit, put it on the table, and just walk past your room. That is their love language.

Wah, the fruit cutting. That is the universal Asian parent love language. My mom also like that. She will scold me until like dog, then five minutes later, she come out with a plate of sliced apples. 'Eat, vitamin C.' That is her way of saying sorry, that is her way of saying she care.

Haha, ya! And we must learn to read those signs. Because when they get very old, when they start to lose their mobility, or they get dementia, they cannot cut the fruit for us anymore. Then we need to be the ones to cut the fruit for them.

But let us talk about the physical care. Because that one is the real nightmare. When they cannot walk, when they need help to go to the toilet, when they cannot feed themselves. Who is going to do it? We cannot just quit our jobs to become full-time caregivers. The financial impact is too huge.

So we hire a Foreign Domestic Worker, the helper. But then, is it fair to the helper? And is it good for the elderly? You have a young girl from Indonesia or Philippines, who barely speak English, taking care of an old man who only speak dialect. They cannot communicate. The old man feel frustrated, the helper feel stressed. It is a very difficult dynamic.

And the cost! The helper salary now what, almost eight hundred, nine hundred dollars. Plus the levy, plus the food, plus the medical insurance. It is like having another kid in the house. And for the lower-income families, that is a huge chunk of their salary. That is why the government give the Foreign Domestic Worker Levy concession, but it is still very expensive.

That is why some people consider the nursing home. But in Singapore, the nursing home got a very bad stigma. You send your parents to the old age home, people will say you are unfilial. 'Wah, this guy very xiao, send his own mother to the home, just want to get the flat.'

Ya, the face culture. The Asian guilt. We feel like if we don't take care of them personally, we are bad children. But we need to change this mindset. The nursing homes now are very different from the old days. They are not just places where you dump the old people and let them die. They got proper medical care, they got physiotherapy, they got social activities.

True. I visit this nursing home in Bedok recently. Very clean, very bright. The staff are very trained. The elderly there, they got friends, they got routines. They don't have to worry about cooking, about cleaning, about falling down in the toilet and nobody know. And the Asian family dynamic, right? That one is the root of a lot of the trauma. Because in our culture, mental health is still very taboo. You tell your parents you are depressed, they will say, 'You got food to eat, got roof over your head, what you depressed about? You just play phone too much.' They don't understand that the depression is not about the material needs, it is about the emotional validation. It is about the pressure to be the filial son or daughter, the pressure to make them proud, the pressure to not lose face.

Exactly. And that is why the youth culture is so heavily focused on 'setting boundaries'. You hear young people talk about 'cutting off toxic family members', 'going no contact', 'protecting my energy'. To the older generation, this is very xiao, very unfilial. 'How can you not talk to your mother?' But to the youth, it is an act of survival. Because they realize that if they don't set boundaries, they will be consumed by the expectations of the family.

It is a huge generational clash. The older generation survived by sacrificing their individual needs for the collective good of the family. That was the social contract. You suffer now, so your children can have a better life. But the youth today, they are saying, 'Why must I suffer? Why can't I have a good life now?' They are rejecting the narrative of sacrifice. And that is terrifying to the older generation, because they think the youth are being selfish.

But is it selfish? Or is it just a different kind of pragmatism? The older generation sacrificed because they believed in the upward mobility. They believed that if they suffered, their kids would definitely be better off. But the youth today, they look at the future, and they don't see that upward mobility. They see climate change, they see AI taking over jobs, they see a world that is very unstable. So they say, 'If the future is so uncertain, why should I sacrifice my present?' That is not selfish, that is just rational.

And I think that paradox is what makes the youth culture so fascinating. Because they are navigating this very unique space. They are global citizens, they are plugged into the internet, they know what is happening in the US, in Europe, in Korea. They are influenced by all these global trends. But at the same time, they are deeply rooted in the local context. They still speak Singlish, they still eat at the hawker centre, they still complain about the weather, they still chope their seats with tissue paper.

And I think the youth culture is also about this negotiation between the global and the local. They want to be part of the world, but they also want to be Singaporean.

And the language! The Singlish that the youth speak now is very different from the Singlish our parents speak. It is mixed with a lot of internet slang, a lot of Mandarin, a lot of Malay. You hear them say, 'Wah, this one damn shiok, no cap, steady bom pi pi.' It is a very fluid, very hybrid language. It reflects how they consume media. They watch Korean dramas, they watch American TikToks, they listen to Chinese rap, and they just mash it all together into this very unique Singaporean youth dialect.

Because the language, the references, the memes, they change so fast. By the time the uncle understand what 'skibidi' means, the youth already moved on to the next meme.

Ya, the attention span is a huge issue. But I don't think it is because the youth are 'stupid' or 'lazy'. It is because the environment is designed to be like that. The algorithms are designed to keep you scrolling, to give you a dopamine hit every three seconds. So of course your attention span is short. You are training your brain to consume information in bite-sized pieces. And then people complain that the youth don't read long articles, don't watch long movies. Bro, we don't have the time! We are working, we are studying, we are trying to maintain our social media presence, we are trying to 'heal'. Where got time to watch a three-hour movie?

But I think there is a pushback, right? You see this trend of 'digital detox'. The youth who are so burned out by the screens, they start to seek out analog experiences. They go to the pottery class, they go to the film photography workshop, they go to the community garden. They want to touch grass, literally. They want to do things that cannot be digitized, things that require patience and slow focus. It is a way of rebelling against the hyper-connected world.

The 'touch grass' movement. I like that. Because for a generation that lives entirely on the internet, the physical world has become this exotic, almost foreign landscape. You see them go to the park, and they are taking photos of the trees like they are in a national geographic documentary. 'Wah, look at this leaf, so aesthetic.' It is very funny, but it is also a bit sad. They are disconnected from the natural world, because they are so plugged into the digital world.

But is it disconnected? Or is it just a different kind of connection? Because you look at the youth activism today, it is all driven by the digital world. The climate change movement, the mental health awareness, the social justice issues. The youth are using the internet to organize, to speak up, to demand change. They are not just passive consumers; they are active creators. They are using their platforms to challenge the status quo, to call out the injustices that the older generation just accept.

That is a very good point. The 'call-out culture'. The youth today, they have zero tolerance for injustice. They will call out a brand for greenwashing, they will call out a politician for saying something insensitive, they will call out a friend for making a problematic joke. To the older generation, this is very 'cancel culture', very toxic. They say, 'Why you guys so sensitive? Cannot take a joke?' But to the youth, it is about accountability. It is about saying, 'We are not going to accept the bad behavior just because it has always been like that.'

But the problem is, the 'call-out culture' can also become very performative. You see people calling out others just to get likes, just to show how morally superior they are. It becomes a competition of who is the most 'woke', who is the most 'aware'. And if you make one mistake, if you say one wrong thing from ten years ago, they will dig it up and cancel you. It is very kiasu, very fearful. People are afraid to speak, afraid to share their opinions, because they are afraid of being dragged by the mob.

I agree, the mob mentality is very real. And it can be very damaging. But I think it is also a reflection of the powerlessness that the youth feel. Because in the real world, they don't have the power to change the policies, to change the economy, to change the system. So they exercise their power in the digital world. They use the 'call-out' as a weapon, because it is the only weapon they have. It is a way of saying, 'You might have the money and the power, but I have the narrative.'

They want to control their own image, their own voice.

I think the biggest challenge, and it is something I talk about with my friends back in Australia, is the identity crisis of a global city. Singapore has spent the last fifty years building itself into this hyper-efficient, hyper-modern, global hub. You are a financial center, a tech hub, a logistics powerhouse. But in doing that, you have to constantly adapt to the global economy, which means constantly changing. It feels like Singapore is like a smartphone that is always downloading a new software patch. Every time I walk down the street, a new HDB block is being built, a new MRT line is opening, a new policy is rolled out. But the question for the future is, in this relentless pursuit of upgrading, where is the soul? How do you maintain the local culture, the kampong spirit, the unique Singaporean identity, when you are trying so hard to appeal to the rest of the world?

I see that anxiety here. In Australia, we have a very laid-back culture. We work to live. Here, it feels like you live to work. The hustle culture is very intense. I have friends in their twenties who are working sixty, seventy hours a week, and then they go to networking events on the weekend. They are terrified of falling behind. But is that sustainable for the future? Can a society run on pure adrenaline and fear of failure for another fifty years?

That is the million-dollar question. The 'strawberry generation' label that the older folks keep throwing around—it is very unfair. The youth are not fragile; they are just facing a completely different set of circumstances. The older generation had the pioneer spirit, they saw the upward mobility, they knew that if they worked hard, they would get a bigger HDB flat, they would send their kids to a better school. But the youth today, they look at the property prices, they look at the global instability, and they feel like the ladder has been pulled up. So they are redefining success. They are saying, 'I don't want the car, I don't want the BTO in the mature estate, I just want a four-day work week, I want mental health days, I want to travel.'

Exactly! They want work-life balance. But the system, the corporate culture here, still very traditional. The boss still expect you to stay in the office until 8 PM just to show face, even if your work already finish. That one is the real challenge. How to change the mindset of the corporate leaders, how to make them realize that burning out your employees is not a good long-term strategy. And then you add the tuition culture into the mix. Siao liao, the tuition industry in Singapore is bigger than the GDP of some small countries. Parents so kiasu, they start sending their kids for enrichment classes when they are still in diapers. 'Eh, my son already can read Mandarin at two years old!' For what? To compete for a place in a good primary school? The future of our education system needs a massive reset, or we are just going to produce a generation of very smart, very depressed robots.

I have noticed the tuition culture. It is fascinating and a bit sad. You see these little kids, carrying backpacks that are bigger than their bodies, looking so tired. Back home, kids are playing footy or surfing after school. Here, they are going from school to tuition to piano lesson. But I also see the results. The discipline, the academic excellence. It is a double-edged sword. You produce a very capable workforce, but do you produce innovative thinkers? Innovation requires failure, it requires messing around, it requires boredom. If every minute of a child's life is scheduled and optimized, where is the space for creativity?

Wah, you talking until very deep already. But let us talk about another future challenge, the demographic one. The silver tsunami. The birth rate dropping to record lows. We are aging very fast. As a foreigner, I feel this tension sometimes. It is the 'Two Singapores' dynamic. You have the global Singapore, the CBD, the expats, the high-flyers who are very comfortable. And then you have the heartland Singapore, the HDB dwellers, the working class who are feeling the pinch of inflation and feeling left behind. When the economy is good, everyone is happy. But when times are tough, the foreigners become the scapegoats. It is a delicate balancing act for the government. You need foreign talent to keep the economy growing, to fill the manpower gaps, because your birth rate is too low. But if you bring in too many, the locals feel squeezed.

It is the classic dilemma of the global city. We want the economic benefits of globalization, but we want the social cohesion of a small village. And it is very hard to have both. The CMIO model—Chinese, Malay, Indian, Others—it was very useful for the early days of nation-building. It gave us a framework for representation, for self-help groups. But the future is much more complex. We have more mixed-race marriages, we have third-culture kids, we have permanent residents who have lived here for twenty years but are not citizens. The definition of what it means to be Singaporean is going to have to evolve. It cannot just be about your race or your passport. It has to be about shared values, shared experiences.

The hawker centre! That is another thing I worry about for the future. The aging hawker uncles and aunties. They are in their sixties, seventies, still standing over a hot wok for twelve hours a day. The younger generation, they don't want to do this job. It is too hard, too hot, the margin is too small. The government is trying to attract young 'hawkerpreneurs', giving them grants, putting them in air-conditioned food courts. But is it the same? The soul of the hawker centre is in the grit, in the noise, in the history. If we lose the traditional hawkers, do we lose a part of our national heritage?

I have seen that. It is very frustrating for them. They feel stupid, they feel like they don't belong in this new, high-tech Singapore. And that is a dangerous feeling. The future of Singapore is not just about building smart sensors and autonomous vehicles. I love that idea. And I think that is where the hope lies. Singapore has this incredible capacity to adapt. When there is a crisis, you guys pull together. During the pandemic, the way the community stepped up to help the migrant workers, to deliver food to the elderly, it was beautiful. The challenge for the future is to maintain that solidarity when there is no immediate crisis, when things are relatively stable. How do you keep the empathy alive when everyone is just busy chasing their own KPIs?

That is the ultimate test of a mature society. It is easy to be resilient when you are fighting for survival, like in the 1960s. It is much harder to be compassionate when you are comfortable, when you are distracted by the endless consumerism, the global noise. And speaking of global noise, we cannot talk about the future of Singapore without talking about geopolitics. We are a tiny island, no natural resources, no hinterland. We rely on trade, we rely on a rules-based international order. But the world is becoming more fragmented. The US and China are in this intense rivalry. Singapore has to navigate this tightrope. We cannot afford to pick sides, but the pressure to pick sides is increasing.

Wah, the geopolitics very headache. Every time I read the news, I see the US doing this, China doing that, and I just think, 'Eh, why you guys cannot just play nice? My BTO flat price very dependent on the global supply chain, you know?' It sounds very funny, but it is true. The existential threat to Singapore is not just the ocean rising; it is the global order collapsing. If the world stops trading, if the shipping lanes get blocked, if the tech war escalates, we are the first to suffer. We are the little shrimp in a tank of whales.

From an outsider's perspective, that is the most terrifying thing about Singapore. You are so successful, so wealthy, but you are so vulnerable. In Australia, we have a massive continent, we have resources, we have the US security umbrella. Here, you guys have to rely on your wits, your diplomacy, your relevance. The government always says Singapore must remain relevant. If the world doesn't need Singapore, we disappear. So the future of Singapore is deeply tied to the future of the world. You guys have to be the honest broker, the neutral ground, the place where people come to talk, to do business, to resolve disputes.

That is the 'Singapore Inc.' model. We run the country like a corporation. We are efficient, we are reliable, we are safe. But the future requires more than just being a good corporation. It requires being a good citizen of the world. It requires us to contribute to global solutions, whether it is climate change, whether it is AI governance, whether it is pandemic preparedness. We cannot just be a free rider on the global system. We have to add value. And I think the youth understand this. They are very globally aware. They are not just looking at what is happening in their HDB block; they are looking at what is happening in Gaza, in Ukraine, in the US elections. They are demanding that Singapore has a voice on these global issues.

Ya, the youth are very vocal. They want to know where Singapore stands on the big issues. But at the same time, they are also very pragmatic. They know that we cannot just preach to the world. And that duality is what makes Singapore so fascinating to me. You are a city of contradictions. You are a strict, rule-abiding society, but you have this vibrant, chaotic, beautiful street culture. You are a tropical island, but you are obsessed with winter fashion and traveling to Japan to see the snow. You are a global financial hub, but you still eat your meals on a plastic table at a hawker centre. The future of Singapore, I think, is about embracing these contradictions. Not trying to smooth them out, not trying to become just another generic global city like London or New York. But leaning into the weird, the unique, the rojak nature of your identity.

So today we jumping straight into the topic lah, right? Work-life balance. Or as we call it in Singapore, the great myth. The unicorn. The thing everyone talk about, but nobody actually get to experience. Because I was reading this Reddit thread the other day, right? This very viral post on the Ask Singapore subreddit. This guy, fresh graduate, just started his first proper corporate job after the pandemic. And he basically wrote this long, long essay asking, 'Is it me going crazy with a 44-hour work week in Singapore?' And he detail his whole day. Wake up 6:30 AM, rush to take the MRT, squeeze until like sardine, reach office 8:30 AM. Work until 6 PM, but because the culture here, you cannot just pack up and leave at 6 PM sharp. You must wait for the boss to leave, or at least look busy until 7 PM. Then you take the MRT back, reach home 8 PM, eat dinner, shower, sleep, repeat. And he just ask, 'Where is the life? When do I live?' And I read the comments, wah lao, the comments very jialat. Half the people say, 'Welcome to adulthood, bro, get used to it.' The other half say, 'I feel you, I also want to resign every single day.' And it just make me think, like, is this really the Singaporean dream? We work so hard, we study so hard, we get the degree, we get the job, but we basically just become a robot that go from the HDB flat to the CBD and back?B: Wah, that one very real sia. The 44-hour work week is just on paper only. In reality, if you are in a decent MNC or even a local SME, you are looking at 50, 60 hours easily. Because the culture here is very toxic in this aspect. You see the guy next to you still sitting at his desk at 7:30 PM, you dare to pack up and go home? You think the boss will think you are efficient? No, the boss will think you don't have enough work, or you don't have 'tanking power'. You know what is tanking power? That one is the Singaporean superpower. It means how long you can tank, how long you can endure the suffering without complaining. The older generation, our parents, they got very high tanking power. They work 6 days a week, sometimes 7 days, they never complain. They just tahan. Tahan until they retire, tahan until they get diabetes and high blood pressure. But now, the younger generation, they look at this and they say, 'Eh, why must we tahan? We not machine one.' And the bosses, they cannot accept it. They call the young people the strawberry generation, they say they are fragile, they say they cannot take pressure. But actually, it is not that we cannot take pressure. It is that we realize the pressure is not leading anywhere. The pie is not getting bigger, but the slices are getting smaller.

Exactly lah! That is the core issue. The older generation, they work hard because they see the result. They see the kampong turn into HDB flats, they see the GDP go up, they see their kids go to university. They know their hard work got a tangible return. But now? You work 60 hours a week, you sacrifice your weekend, you miss your friend's wedding because you got urgent project. For what? So that the CEO can buy a third car? So that the company can declare record profits and then retrench 10% of the staff via Zoom? The social contract is broken. The promise was, 'You work hard, you will get a good life.' But the reality is, 'You work hard, you will get a burnout and an HDB loan that take 30 years to pay off.' And then the government and the media, they always talk about work-life balance. They have seminars, they have podcasts, they have CNA articles saying, 'Companies need to embrace flexi-work, need to care for mental health.' But it is all just talk talk, never do. Because at the end of the day, the KPI is still the KPI. The profit margin is still the profit margin. You cannot just tell the boss, 'Eh, I need work-life balance, so I only do 50% of my work.' The boss will just find someone else who is willing to do 100%. And because we are a small island, because we are so kiasu about losing our jobs, we just keep quiet and we tank it.

Ya lor, the kiasu culture really come into play here. We are so afraid of losing out, so afraid of being retrenched, that we overcompensate. We become the first to reach the office, the last to leave. We reply to WhatsApp messages at 10 PM on a Sunday. And that brings me to the WhatsApp culture. Wah, the WhatsApp is the devil's tool for the Singaporean worker. In the past, when you leave the office, you are done. The physical boundary is there. You go home, you close the door, the work stay in the office. But now? The office is in your pocket. The boss can ping you while you are eating dinner with your family, while you are on the toilet, while you are trying to sleep. And the worst part is the expectation of immediate reply. You see the message come in, you see the 'typing...' bubble, your heart rate go up. You think, 'Wah, what wrong now? The server crash? The client angry?' You open it, and it is just, 'Eh, can you check this email?' And you have to reply immediately. If you don't reply, you are not dedicated. If you reply too fast, you train the boss to expect you to be available 24/7. It is a lose-lose situation. So what people do? They do the 'k' reply. Or they wait for 15 minutes before replying, just to show that they are 'busy' but still 'responsive'. It is this whole psychological game that we play every single day, and it is very, very draining. It is not the actual work that tires you out; it is this constant state of kancheong, this hyper-vigilance, that exhaust your mental battery.

The mental battery, yes! That one is very chim but very true. We talk about physical tiredness, but the mental load is what kill us. And this is where the whole 'flexi-work' or 'work from home' thing become a bit of a scam. During the pandemic, everyone was like, 'Wah, WFH is the future! We can work in our pyjamas, we can save time on commute, we can have work-life integration!' But what actually happen? The boundary completely disappear. When you work from home, your bedroom become your office. Your dining table become your meeting room. You wake up, you open your laptop, you work. You take a break, you walk to the kitchen, you open your laptop again. You never actually leave the office. And the bosses, they think because you are at home, you must be free. So they schedule more meetings. They expect you to be online on Teams from 9 AM to 6 PM, with the green dot always on. If your dot turn yellow or grey, they will message you, 'Eh, you step out ah?' So you have to buy this mouse jiggler thing just to trick the system! It is so ridiculous. We are treating adults like primary school kids who need to be monitored every five minutes. And then they call it 'flexi-work'. Flexi-work in Singapore usually just means you can choose which cafe to sit at while you reply to emails, but you still cannot log off. It is not flexibility of time; it is flexibility of location for your suffering.

Haha, the mouse jiggler! I got friends who use that one. Very sian. But you know what is even more sian? The whole debate about the four-day work week. Because lately, a lot of countries in Europe, like the UK, like Iceland, they do the trial for the four-day work week, and the result very positive. Productivity go up, mental health improve, people are happier. And then the Singaporean media pick it up, and everyone start to hope. 'Wah, can we get four-day work week also?' And then the local bosses and the policymakers come out and say, 'Eh, we need to look at the context. Singapore is different. We are a small node in the global economy. If we reduce the hours, our productivity will drop, our competitiveness will drop, and we will be eaten by the region.' So what is the compromise? Some companies say, 'Okay, we do four-day work week, but you must compress your 40 hours into 4 days.' So you work 10 hours a day, four days a week. Bro, by day three, your brain already melt. You cannot focus. You just sit there staring at the screen, pretending to work. That one is not work-life balance; that is just work-life compression. You are just speeding up the treadmill so you can fall off faster. And then some companies say, 'Okay, four-day work week, but your pay will be pro-rated.' So you get 20% pay cut. Then who want to take? Only the people who are already rich and don't need the money. For the normal person who got loan to pay, who got parents to feed, you cannot afford to take a pay cut just to get one extra day off to do laundry and sleep.
