Ya, the economic reality is the ultimate buzzkill for all these work-life balance conversations. Because when you talk about work-life balance, people always bring up the example of the Europeans or the Australians. They say, 'Look at them, they work 35 hours a week, they go to the beach on Wednesday, they value their family time.' But what they don't tell you is the cost of living there, the tax rate there, and the cultural context. In Singapore, we are obsessed with the 'simple life'. You go on TikTok, you see all these young people who quit their high-paying corporate jobs to go be a barista in London, or to run a small bakery in Bali. They post these aesthetic videos, saying, 'I finally found myself. I am living my truth. I don't need the corporate rat race.' And you watch it, and you feel very inspired. You think, 'Wah, I also want to quit. I also want to go find myself.' But then you wake up the next morning, you look at your CPF statement, you look at your HDB BTO loan letter, you look at the price of the car you want to buy, and you realize, 'Alamak, I cannot find myself. I need to find my next paycheck.' Because in Singapore, the 'simple life' is actually very expensive. You think you can just live simply? The cost of living here is so high. Even if you don't eat at the fancy restaurants, even if you just eat at the hawker centre, the price of chicken rice keep going up. The ERP rates keep changing. The GST go up to 9%. You need money just to exist here. So the 'simple life' that you see on TikTok is usually funded by a trust fund, or by parents who own multiple properties. For the average Singaporean, the rat race is not a choice; it is a survival mechanism.

Exactly! The HDB loan is the ultimate chain that bind you to the corporate world. You sign that 25-year loan, you basically sell your soul to the bank and to your employer. You cannot just 'quiet quit'. You cannot just 'act your wage'. Because if you lose your job, the bank don't care about your mental health. The bank will still want their monthly installment. And this is why the generational clash in the office is so intense right now. You got the older bosses, the Boomers and the older Gen X, they are the ones who bought their HDB flats in the 90s when it was cheap, when the economy was booming. They work hard, they got the rewards. So they look at the younger staff and they say, 'You guys are so entitled. You want high pay, you want flexi-time, you want mental health leave. In my time, we just work hard and keep quiet.' They don't understand that the game has changed. The rules are different now. The ladder is broken. And then you got the Gen Z and the younger Millennials, they are the ones who are saying, 'Eh, the company is not my family. I am here to trade my time for money. When the clock hit 6 PM, I am done.' And the older bosses call this 'quiet quitting'. They say it is a lack of loyalty. But bro, loyalty is a two-way street. When the company retrench 500 people in one day because the global economy slow down, do they show loyalty? No. They do it via a mass email. So why the employee need to show loyalty by working on the weekend for free? It is a transaction. But the older generation, they still operate on this kampong spirit, this idea that the company is a family. And that is where the friction come from.

The 'company is a family' narrative is the biggest toxic lie in the corporate world. Especially in Singapore. Because Asian culture is very big on hierarchy, on filial piety, on respect for elders. So the boss act like the father of the family. He expect you to obey, he expect you to sacrifice for the 'family'. And if you complain, he will say you are disrespectful, you are rocking the boat. And the HR department, they always organize these team building events, these family days, to reinforce this idea. 'Eh, we are all one big family here!' But you know what? When you are in a real family, if you are sick, they take care of you. If you are struggling, they help you. In the corporate 'family', if you are sick, they ask for a MC from the polyclinic, and if you take too many MCs, they will call you in for a performance review. It is very hypocritical. And this is why the mental health crisis in Singapore is so severe. We are living in this pressure cooker. The government always say Singapore is a success story, the shining city on a hill. And it is true, we are very safe, very clean, the streets are nice, the MRT is efficient. We live in a bubble. But inside this bubble, the pressure is immense. Because we are so small, we have no natural resources, the government always instill this siege mentality in us. 'We must be competitive. If we stop running, we will die. The region is catching up.' So they pass this anxiety down to the companies, and the companies pass it down to the employees. Every single person is running on a treadmill that is going faster and faster. And if you fall, you don't just fall behind; you fall out of the system. You become irrelevant. And that fear, that existential dread, is what keeps us working until 9 PM. It is not because we love the work. It is because we are terrified of being left behind.

The siege mentality, yes. That one is very deep. We are always taught that we are a little red dot, we are vulnerable, we are surrounded by bigger neighbors. So we must work harder, we must be smarter, we must be more efficient. And that might have been true in the 1960s, in the 1970s, when we were just trying to survive. But now? We are a first-world country. We have reserves, we have infrastructure, we have a highly educated population. Can we not relax a bit? Can we not say, 'Okay, we have made it. Now we can actually enjoy the life we built?' But the mindset is still stuck in the survival mode. The leaders, they are so afraid of complacency. They think if we rest, we will become soft, we will become like the Europeans who are always on strike and lazy. But they don't understand that rest is not laziness. Rest is recovery. Rest is what allow you to be creative, to be innovative. If you are always in survival mode, your brain is just focused on the next immediate threat. You cannot think long-term. You cannot innovate. You just react. And that is why a lot of companies in Singapore are very good at executing, but very bad at innovating. Because everyone is too tired to think outside the box. They just follow the SOP, they just do what the boss say, they just tank it. And then we wonder why our productivity growth is slowing down. We wonder why we are not producing the next big global tech unicorn. Because the people who are supposed to create the future are too busy replying to WhatsApp messages about the roster for next week's shift.

Wah, you hit the nail on the head. The innovation deficit. Because innovation require slack. It require spare time, spare mental bandwidth to just daydream, to just play around with ideas. But in the Singaporean corporate culture, every minute must be optimized. Every minute must be monetized. You go to a seminar, they teach you how to manage your time, how to use the Pomodoro technique, how to block your calendar for 'deep work'. But then your boss will just fill up your calendar with meetings anyway. So your 'deep work' time become 11 PM to 2 AM, after everyone else go home. And that is when you do the actual work. So you are basically shifting your work life to the middle of the night, which mess up your circadian rhythm, which make you more tired the next day, which make you less productive, which mean you need to stay back later. It is this vicious cycle. And the government, they try to help, right? They introduce the Progressive Wage Model, they talk about the right to request for flexi-work arrangements. But it is all very voluntary. It is very 'soft law'. They cannot force the boss to respect your boundary. Because if they make it too strict, the MNCs will just move to Vietnam or to Indonesia, where the labor is cheaper and the people are more 'hungry'. So we are trapped in this geopolitical reality. We need to keep the investors happy, we need to keep the economy growing, so we sacrifice the well-being of the workers. And the workers, they just complain on Reddit, they complain on the podcast, and then they go back to work on Monday and they tank it again.

Haha, the cycle of complaining and tanking. That is the Singaporean way. We are very good at complaining, I must admit. We complain about the weather, we complain about the ERP, we complain about the chope culture, and we complain about the work culture. But complaining is also a form of coping, right? It is a way to release the steam from the pressure cooker. If we don't complain, we will explode. And you know what is the ultimate coping mechanism? Makan. When the work is too sian, when the boss is too toxic, when the 44-hour work week is killing you, what do you do? You go eat. You go the hawker centre, you order a plate of char kway teow, extra lard, extra cockles, and a bowl of bak kut teh. And for that 30 minutes, you forget about the KPI, you forget about the WhatsApp, you forget about the BTO loan. The food is the great equalizer. The food is the only thing that give you immediate, tangible satisfaction. You work hard, you get money, you use money to buy good food. That is the basic transaction of life in Singapore. And that is why the hawker culture is so important. It is not just about the food; it is about the communal release. You see the uncle sweating over the wok, you see the auntie washing the dishes, you see the people sitting in their singlet and shorts, slurping the noodles. Everyone is equal at the hawker centre. The CEO and the cleaner, they both eat the same $4 chicken rice. And for that brief moment, the hierarchy disappear. The work-life balance might be a myth in the office, but at the hawker centre, the balance is restored. You eat until you shiok, you drink the kopi peng, and you think, 'Okay, life not so bad lah. Tomorrow go work and tank again.'

Haha, very true. The hawker centre is our therapy. Cheaper than a psychologist, and the food is much better. But seriously, I think the conversation about work-life balance in Singapore needs to shift. We cannot just keep blaming the bosses, or blaming the government, or blaming the kiasu culture. We also need to look at our own complicity. We are the ones who reply to the email at 10 PM. We are the ones who volunteer for the extra project because we want the bonus. We are the ones who compare our salary with our friends and feel inadequate. We are trapped in this system, yes, but we also keep the system running. If everyone suddenly decide to log off at 6 PM sharp, if everyone suddenly decide to ignore the WhatsApp message after hours, the system will have to change. The boss will have no choice but to respect the boundary, because if they fire everyone, who will do the work? But we are too afraid to be the first one to do it. We are too afraid of being the outlier. So we wait for the government to pass a law, like the 'right to disconnect' law in Europe. But in Singapore, we don't have that culture of collective bargaining, we don't have that culture of striking. We are very individualistic in our suffering. We suffer alone in our cubicle, we suffer alone in our HDB flat. And that is why the change is so slow. Because the change require collective action, and we are too tired, too kancheong, and too kiasu to organize.

Ya, the collective action part is very difficult. Because the system is designed to make us compete against each other. From primary school, we are ranked, we are graded, we are compared. 'Eh, your son get A or B?' 'Eh, your company give you how many months AWS?' We are always looking sideways to see if the person next to us is running faster. And that makes it very hard to build solidarity. When you see your colleague struggling, your first instinct is not to help them; your first instinct is to make sure you don't get dragged down by them. Or you think, 'Good, now the boss will see that I am more capable.' It is a very toxic mindset, but it is ingrained in us from young. So when we talk about work-life balance, it is not just about changing the labor laws; it is about changing the culture. It is about unlearning this hyper-competitive, kiasu mindset. It is about realizing that your worth is not defined by your job title, or your salary, or how many hours you tank in the office. But that is very hard to do when the whole society is screaming at you that you must be successful, you must be productive, you must be useful. If you are not producing, you are a burden. And that is the deepest fear of the Singaporean. To be a burden. To be the one who cannot keep up. So we run, we run until our legs give way, until our mind break. And then we wonder why the suicide rate is high, why the depression rate is high, why the birth rate is dropping to record lows. Because who want to bring a child into this pressure cooker? Who want to subject their kid to the same rat race that they are suffering in? The DINK lifestyle, the Double Income No Kids, it is not just because people are selfish. It is because people are tired. They look at their own life, they look at the 44-hour work week, they look at the cost of tuition, and they say, 'No thank you. I cannot even balance my own life, how I want to balance a family?'

Wow, that is a very heavy point, but it is the reality. The birth rate is the ultimate vote of confidence in the work-life balance of a country. And in Singapore, the vote is a resounding 'no'. We are too tired to reproduce. We are too tired to date, we are too tired to socialize. You ask the young people, 'What you do on the weekend?' They say, 'Sleep. I just want to sleep.' Because the weekday drain them so much. They don't have the energy to go out, to explore, to fall in love. They just want to recharge. And that is a very sad state of affairs. Because the weekend is supposed to be the time you live your life, not just the time you recover from your work. But in Singapore, the weekend is just the recovery period for the next work week. It is this endless loop of work, sleep, recover, work, sleep, recover. And the government know this, that is why they keep talking about the 'well-being economy', they keep talking about 'skillsfuture', they keep telling us to 'learn how to learn'. But they don't understand that you cannot learn if your brain is fried. You cannot upskill if you are working 60 hours a week. The very idea of 'skillsfuture' assume that people have the time and the mental bandwidth to go for classes. But the reality is, the people who need to upskill the most, the lower-wage workers, the PMEs who are under pressure, they are the ones who have the least time. They are the ones who are working multiple jobs, or working overtime just to make ends meet. So the policies are very top-down, very out of touch with the ground reality. They think if they just build more parks, more PCNs, more community clubs, people will go and relax. But people don't go, because they are too tired. They just want to go home, turn on the aircon, order GrabFood, and watch Netflix until they fall asleep. That is the maximum capacity for leisure that the average Singaporean has.

Haha, the GrabFood and Netflix routine. That is the national pastime. Because going out require effort, require money, require social energy. And after 5 days of dealing with difficult clients, difficult bosses, difficult colleagues, your social battery is at 0%. You don't want to see any more people. You don't want to talk to anyone. You just want to be alone in your air-conditioned room. And that is why the loneliness epidemic is also very real. We are so connected digitally, we are always on WhatsApp, always on Instagram, always on LinkedIn. But we are very disconnected physically and emotionally. We don't have the time to build deep relationships. We don't have the time to just sit down at the void deck and talk until 2 AM like the older generation used to. The void deck is now just a place you pass through to get to the MRT. The community is gone. The kampong spirit is dead, replaced by the corporate spirit. And the corporate spirit is very lonely. Because in the corporate world, everyone is a competitor, everyone is a networking opportunity. You don't have friends; you have connections. You don't have colleagues; you have stakeholders. And that is a very cold way to live your life. You reach 40 years old, you look around, you got the HDB flat, you got the CPF, you got the job title. But you don't have anyone to share it with. Because you spend all your time building the career, and you forget to build the life.

That is the ultimate tragedy of the Singaporean success story. We built this beautiful, efficient, wealthy city. But we forgot to build the soul. We forgot that the purpose of the economy is to serve the people, not the other way around. The people are supposed to be the end goal, not the means to the GDP growth. But somewhere along the way, we got confused. We think that if the country is rich, we will be happy. But we are not happy. We are stressed, we are anxious, we are tired. We are running on a treadmill that is going nowhere. And the scariest part is, we don't know how to get off. Because getting off mean you fall. And in Singapore, falling is not an option. So we just keep running. We keep tanking. We keep complaining. And we keep eating the char kway teow to comfort ourselves. Because what else can we do? We cannot strike, we cannot protest, we cannot just quit and go live in the forest. We are Singaporeans. We are practical. We are pragmatic. We know that this is the best place in the world to live, even with all its flaws. We know that if we go overseas, we will miss the safety, we will miss the food, we will miss the convenience. So we stay. And because we stay, we have to make it work. We have to find the little pockets of joy. The little moments of work-life balance. The Friday evening beer with the colleagues who you actually like. The Sunday morning kopi at the kopitiam, reading the newspaper, watching the uncle play chess. The one day a year where you take leave and go to Japan to eat the sushi and see the snow. These are the crumbs of balance that we collect, and we hoard them like they are gold. Because we know that the rest of the year, we belong to the company. We belong to the economy. We belong to the little red dot.

Wah, you make it sound very poetic, but also very sad. The crumbs of balance. But I think that is the reality. We are not going to get the European 4-day work week anytime soon. We are not going to get the right to disconnect law. The government will always prioritize the economy over the individual well-being, because that is the survival instinct of this nation. So we have to find our own way to survive. We have to set our own boundaries, even if the boss don't respect them. We have to learn to say 'no', even if it mean we miss out on the promotion. We have to learn to prioritize our mental health, even if the company say it is not important. Because at the end of the day, when you are on your deathbed, you are not going to think about the KPI you achieved in 2024. You are not going to think about the WhatsApp messages you replied to at 10 PM. You are going to think about the people you loved, the food you ate, the moments you laughed. And if you spend your whole life tanking for a company that will replace you in a week, you are going to regret it. So we need to shift the narrative. We need to stop glorifying the hustle. We need to stop praising the people who work 80 hours a week. We need to start praising the people who log off at 6 PM to go watch their kid's football game. We need to start praising the people who take their mental health day. We need to change the culture from the ground up. And it start with us. It start with us refusing to reply to the email after hours. It start with us telling our boss, 'Eh, I cannot do this weekend, I got plan.' It start with us being okay with being average. Because in a society that is obsessed with being the best, choosing to be average is an act of rebellion.

Choosing to be average is an act of rebellion. I like that. Because the whole education system, the whole corporate system, it is designed to make you want to be the top 1%. But the reality is, 99% of us are not going to be the top 1%. We are going to be the average. We are going to be the normal people who do normal jobs, live in normal HDB flats, and eat normal chicken rice. And that is okay. That is actually the majority. But the media, the society, they only celebrate the top 1%. They only talk about the CEOs, the founders, the scholars. They make the average people feel like they are failures. And that is where the insecurity come from. That is where the kiasu come from. We are all terrified of being average. So we overwork, we overstretch, we burn ourselves out, just to prove that we are not average. But it is a trap. It is a game that is rigged from the start. So the ultimate work-life balance hack is to just opt out of the game. To say, 'I am average, and I am happy with that.' To say, 'My job is just a job. It is not my identity. It is not my soul.' And when you detach your self-worth from your productivity, suddenly, the 44-hour work week don't seem so scary. The WhatsApp message from the boss don't cause you anxiety. Because you know that you are more than your job. You are a human being. You are a friend, a son, a daughter, a partner. You are a person who like to eat good food, who like to watch the sunset, who like to sleep in on a Sunday. And no boss, no KPI, no corporate culture can take that away from you, unless you let them. So the next time your boss ping you at 9 PM, just look at the phone, smile, and say, 'Noted, I will check it tomorrow.' And then turn off the notification. Because your peace of mind is more important than their urgency.

Wah, that one is the ultimate power move. 'Noted, I will check it tomorrow.' I need to practice saying that. Because my default mode is always panic. I always think if I don't reply, the world will end. But actually, the world never end. The server never crash because you delay the reply by 12 hours. The client never die because you don't send the deck at midnight. It is all in our head. It is the conditioning. We are conditioned to be on call, to be available, to be subservient. And breaking that conditioning is very hard. It require a lot of courage. Because the first time you do it, your heart will beat very fast. You will think the boss is going to fire you. But then you realize, nothing happen. The sun still rise the next day. The MRT still run. The kopi still taste good. And you realize that you have the power. You have the power to set the boundary. Because at the end of the day, they need you more than you need them. They need your labor to make their profit. But you can always find another job. You can always find another way to survive. You are not irreplaceable to them, but you are irreplaceable to your family, to your friends, to yourself. So don't let them treat you like a machine. Don't let them drain your soul. Because once your soul is gone, no amount of AWS, no amount of CPF, no amount of BTO flat can buy it back. So protect your peace. Protect your time. Protect your life. Because work will always be there. The emails will never stop. The KPIs will always increase. But your life, your time, your health, that is finite. That is precious. So spend it wisely. Spend it on the people you love. Spend it on the food you enjoy. Spend it on the things that make you feel alive. And leave the rest for the company. Because the company will not cry at your funeral. They will just post a LinkedIn tribute and then hire your replacement the next week. So don't give them your whole life. Give them your 9 to 6. And keep the rest for yourself.

Very well said, bro. Very deep. I think that is the perfect note to end on. The reality check. We are in a tough system, we are in a pressure cooker, we are in a kiasu society. But we have the power to choose how we respond to it. We can choose to tank, or we can choose to live. We can choose to be a robot, or we can choose to be a human. And I hope, for all the listeners out there who are feeling sian, who are feeling kancheong, who are feeling like the 44-hour work week is killing them, I hope you hear this. You are not alone. We are all in this together. We are all just trying to survive the little red dot. So be kind to yourself. Set your boundaries. Reply to the WhatsApp when you are ready. And when the stress get too much, just go down to the hawker centre, order a kopi, and watch the world go by. Because at the end of the day, life is not about the work you do. It is about the kopi you drink, the friends you laugh with, and the moments you cherish. So don't let the corporate world steal that from you. Alright, I think we have covered enough heavy stuff for one day. My brain is already full. I need to go and eat some good food to recover from this podcast.

topic: Holidays and family traditions in Singapore
data: So, we jumping straight into it lah, right? Today’s topic is holidays and family traditions in Singapore. B: Wah, this one is a heavy topic. Because honestly, the calendar here is just non-stop. One holiday finish, next one already creeping up.

If it was that easy to just 'be happy', we wouldn't be depressed in the first place! Toxic positivity invalidates the person's feelings. It makes them feel guilty for being sad. What they need is validation. They need someone to say, 'Wah, that sounds very hard. I’m here for you.'

True, true. But we also have to acknowledge that for some people, the stress and the mental health issues are very severe. It’s beyond just needing a friend to listen. It requires professional help. And the cost of therapy in Singapore is still a barrier for many. A single session with a clinical psychologist can cost two to three hundred dollars. For a fresh grad earning three thousand, that is a huge chunk of their salary.

Yeah, the financial barrier is very real. That’s why we need to talk about the systemic issues. Mental health cannot just be an individual responsibility. It has to be a societal and structural priority. The government has done some work, like the Beyond the Label campaign, and there are subsidies at IMH or the polyclinics. But the waiting list is very long, and the stigma in the community is still there.

And what about the workplace? If companies really care about mental health, they need to change the culture. Not just put a ping-pong table in the pantry. They need to enforce the right to disconnect. They need to ensure that the workload is manageable. They need to train their managers to spot the signs of burnout and to have empathetic conversations, not just KPI-driven conversations.

Wah, if they can do that, I think a lot of people will be very happy. But you know the reality. The bottom line is always king. As long as the company is making money, they don't care if you are crying in the toilet. It’s very cynical, but it’s the truth. That’s why a lot of young people are now choosing to quit the rat race. They are doing 'quiet quitting', or they are starting their own small businesses just to have control over their time.

The side hustle culture is also a double-edged sword, I think. On one hand, it gives people a sense of purpose and extra income. On the other hand, it means you are never really resting. You work your 9-to-5, and then you go home and pack parcels for your Carousell shop, or you edit videos for your TikTok. You are monetizing your hobbies. When do you just... exist? When do you just sit and do nothing?

That is a very profound point. We have forgotten how to just rest without feeling guilty. In Singapore, doing nothing is seen as a waste of time. 'Time is money.' So even on your weekend, you feel like you must be productive. You must go to a cafe, read a book, learn a new skill. You cannot just lie on the bed and stare at the ceiling. If you do that, you feel lazy, and then you stress about being lazy. It’s a vicious cycle.

Wah, the guilt of resting. I experience that every weekend. I plan to just sleep in and watch Netflix, but then I wake up at 11 AM, and I feel this pang of guilt. I think, 'Wah, I should go exercise, or I should read that book.' So I force myself to go to the gym, and I end up feeling more tired. We need to unlearn this productivity mindset. Rest is not a reward for working hard; rest is a basic human need.

Correct. And we need to redefine what success means. The traditional checklist is not for everyone. Some people don't want to be a CEO. Some people don't want to get married or have kids. I think that’s where the younger generation is pushing the boundary. Gen Z and the younger millennials are very vocal about their mental health. They are setting boundaries at work. They are forcing the older generation and the corporate world to adapt.

Wah, the family pressure. That one is a classic. Every Chinese New Year or Mid-Autumn Festival, the relatives will start the interrogation. 'Got girlfriend or not? When buying house? You have to smile and nod, and inside you are dying.

Hahaha, the 'smile and nod' strategy. We all know it well. But seriously, setting boundaries with family is one of the hardest things to do in an Asian context, because filial piety is deeply ingrained. You are taught to respect your elders, to listen to them. So when you try to assert your own needs, you feel like you are being a bad child. The guilt is very heavy.

And that emotional labor is real. It’s invisible work, but it drains your battery. This is why self-care is not just about face masks and bubble baths. Sometimes, self-care is saying 'no' to your relatives. Sometimes, self-care is turning off your phone. Sometimes, self-care is going to therapy and doing the hard work of unpacking your childhood trauma.

Wah, you went deep already. But it’s true. We often trivialize self-care. We think it’s just a spa day. But real self-care is very unglamorous. topic: Working from home vs back to office
data: What’s up, everybody? Welcome back to another episode of our show. Your favourite thrice-weekly podcast where we dissect the hottest topics with a touch of, what do we call it, Terrence?Host B: Good old, honest Singaporean banter, man.

True, true. But today, we are not just celebrating our return to the studio. We are tackling a topic that has been dominating our WhatsApp groups, our LinkedIn feeds, and our dinner table conversations. The great debate: Working from home versus the dreaded back-to-office mandate.

Wah, this one is a hot potato. Every time this topic comes up, people get very emotional. Some people treat the office like a prison, and some people treat their home like a distraction factory.

I think for me, the biggest issue is the commute. Let’s be real. Before COVID, I spend easily two and a half hours a day just travelling. MRT breakdown, bus late, then the ERP jam. By the time I reach the office, I already sian. My energy is gone. I just want to go back home and sleep.

But the bosses, they always say, 'Eh, you use the travel time to listen to podcast, to learn something new, to be productive.'
CC DT NNS , PRP RB VBP , `` UH , PRP VBP DT NN NN TO VB IN NN , TO VB NN JJ , TO VB JJ . ''

Productive my foot. You try standing in a packed MRT at 8:30 AM on the East-West line, squeezed between an uncle with a huge backpack and someone eating a bao. The train suddenly brake, everyone sway together like sardines. How to be productive? You just try to maintain your balance and not step on anyone’s foot, while your phone is blasting some random TikTok audio because someone forgot their headphones.

That is a very valid point. And from a management perspective, I understand why companies want people back. They talk about 'water cooler moments', spontaneous collaboration, company culture. But let’s be honest, how many of these water cooler moments actually lead to groundbreaking ideas? Most of the time, it’s just people complaining about the aircon being too cold or discussing what to order for lunch.

Shiok lah, discussing lunch is the most important part of the day! But I see what you mean. There is this concept of 'presenteeism'. You know, when you are in the office, you feel the pressure to look busy. Even if you finish your work by 4 PM, you cannot just pack up and leave. You have to stay until 6 PM or 7 PM, typing loudly on your keyboard, moving your mouse, just so the boss sees you are still there.

Exactly. It’s a performance. And it’s very tiring. When you work from home, the metric is purely output. Did you finish the report? Yes. Then you can close your laptop and go exercise, or cook, or spend time with your family. In the office, the metric is often visibility. 'Oh, he is the last one to leave, must be a very hardworking staff.' Actually, he might just be very slow or inefficient.

Or he is waiting for the ERP rates to drop before he drives home! That one is classic Singaporean kiasu behaviour. Save two dollars on ERP, but waste one hour of your life sitting in the office doing nothing, pretending to read emails.

Wah, that is so true. The 'agak-agak' departure time. You calculate the traffic, you check the ERP sign, and you time your exit perfectly. But then, your boss suddenly walks out of his office and says, 'Eh, got a quick meeting?' and your whole plan is ruined. Then you reach home, traffic peak hour, siao liao.

But we cannot romanticize working from home completely. It has its own set of problems. The biggest one is the blurring of boundaries between work and personal life.

Oh, I know this one very well. Your dining table becomes your office. Your bed is right behind you. It is so tempting to just bring the laptop to bed and reply to 'just one more email'. And then suddenly, it is 11 PM, and you are still working. Your whole evening is gone.

And the worst part is, because you are at home, people expect you to be available 24/7. 'Eh, you are at home, can help me check this document now or not?' They forget that being at home does not mean you are on standby. Home is supposed to be your sanctuary, your place to rest and recharge.

Yes, and I read somewhere, I think it was a very good advice, about how to separate the two. You know that trick where you visualize leaving your work stress on a specific tree on your way home?

The significant tree trick! Yes, I heard of that. You find a big tree near your office or along your route. When you leave work, you mentally dump all your work problems, your annoying colleagues, your pending tasks onto that tree. You say, 'Okay, I leave it here. See you tomorrow.'

And when you come back the next day, you 'pick up' the work from the tree. It sounds a bit siao, but psychologically, it helps to create a physical boundary. The problem with WFH is that the tree is inside your house. You cannot escape it. The laptop is staring at you from the corner of the room.

Exactly. So you have to create artificial boundaries. For example, I have a rule: no laptop in the bedroom. And at 7 PM, I shut down my computer, and I do not check my work Slack or email until the next morning. I put my phone in another room.

But can you really do that? What if your boss is the type who sends messages at 9 PM and expects an immediate reply? If you don't reply, next day he will give you the death stare. Or he will passive-aggressively mention it in the team meeting.

That is where you need to manage expectations. You can set your Slack status to 'Offline after 7 PM'. Or you just don't reply. If it is a true emergency, they will call you. If they don't call, it can wait until tomorrow. We need to stop glorifying the 'always-on' culture. It is not sustainable. It leads to burnout, and then the company loses a good employee.

Speaking of burnout, I feel like there is a huge generational divide on this topic. The older generation, the boomers, or even some older millennials, they have this 'tahan' culture. They think if you are not suffering a bit, you are not working hard enough.

Yes! They will say, 'Last time we work until 10 PM, no complaint, now you young people so soft, want to go home at 6 PM sharp.' They don't understand that the world has changed. The cost of living is higher, the pressure is higher, and the technology means we are never truly off the clock. Last time, when you leave the office, you are done. Now, the office is in your pocket.

And the younger generation, Gen Z, they are pushing back. They are talking about 'quiet quitting' or 'acting your wage'. Which basically means, I will do exactly what is in my job description, and not a single thing more. I will not volunteer for extra projects, I will not answer calls on the weekend.

Some people say quiet quitting is just a fancy term for being lazy or doing the bare minimum. But I think it is a reaction to being overworked and underappreciated for so long. It is a self-preservation mechanism. You give them 100 percent, they give you more work, not more money. So why bother?

I agree. If a company pays you for eight hours of work, you give them eight hours of work. If they want more, they need to pay more. It is a simple transaction. But in Singapore, we are so kiasi. We are scared that if we set boundaries, we will be labelled as 'not a team player' or 'difficult to work with', and then we won't get promoted.

That fear is real. And it is exploited by bad management. A good manager evaluates you based on your output and the quality of your work, not on how many hours you sit in your chair. But unfortunately, not all managers are good. Some are just insecure and need to see you physically present to feel like they are in control.

That is the micromanagement trap. And it is so demoralizing. You feel like a child being watched by a strict teacher, instead of a professional adult who is trusted to do their job. You end up doing busy work just to look busy.

So, what is the solution? We cannot all just quit and become digital nomads in Bali. Although, wah, that sounds very shiok right now. Sitting by the beach, working on a laptop, drinking coconut water.

I think the hybrid model is the most realistic compromise. Maybe two or three days in the office, and the rest at home. It gives you the best of both worlds. You get the face-to-face interaction for meetings and team bonding, but you also get the focus time and comfort of working from home.

But even the hybrid model has its own headaches. The coordination is a nightmare. Everyone wants to work from home on Mondays and Fridays to extend their weekend. So the office is empty on those days. Then on Tuesdays, Wednesdays, and Thursdays, the office is packed, and you cannot find a meeting room or a parking lot.

And don't get me started on the 'hot desking'. You come to the office, and you have to book a desk on an app. Then you reach there, and the desk is dirty, or the chair is broken, or someone left their half-eaten sandwich on the table. It is so sian. You spend 15 minutes just wiping down the table and looking for a monitor cable.

topic: Can we use Singlish at work or not?
data: Alright everyone, welcome back to the show. Today we are diving into a topic that literally gets everyone worked up in the office pantry. The great debate: can we use Singlish at work or not?Host B: Wah, this one is dangerous territory. You ask this question in any Singaporean workplace, and suddenly the whole team split into two camps. One side say must code-switch properly, speak standard English, look professional. The other side say, 'Eh, we are in Singapore, why must act like ang moh? Just speak normally lor.'

Exactly. And I think the confusion comes from the fact that nobody really draw a clear line. What is 'normal' and what is 'unprofessional' is very subjective. Depends on who you ask, depends on your industry, depends on who your boss is.

Let's start with the basics. When we say 'work', what kind of work are we talking about? Because a local hawker stall using Singlish to chat with regular customers is completely different from a multinational banking firm dealing with overseas clients.

Very true. If you work in a SME, mostly local clients, your boss also speak Singlish, your team also speak Singlish, then using standard English the whole day will make you sound like a robot. Or worse, people will think you are trying too hard to be superior. 'Why this person so formal? Got problem with him or what?'

And sometimes, Singlish is actually a tool for efficiency. You know how it is. When you are in a meeting and you need to make a quick point, standard English can be quite wordy. But in Singlish, you just say, 'This one cannot lah, budget not enough, client want everything for free.' Three sentences become one. Everyone understand immediately.

But that is exactly where the risk come in. If you are in an environment with foreign colleagues or expatriates, they might not catch the nuance. 'Cannot lah' sound dismissive to them. 'Budget not enough' sound unprofessional. They want to hear structured reasoning, not colloquial shorthand.

Alamak, but if you spend half your meeting translating Singlish to standard English for the ang moh, the meeting will never end. You waste so much time. That's why I think code-switching is a mandatory skill in modern Singapore workplace. You don't abandon Singlish completely, you just know when to switch it on and off.

Yes, but the problem is many people treat code-switching like a switch you can just flip. It's not that simple. It's about linguistic competence. You need to know your audience, read the room, and adjust your register accordingly. If you can't do that, then maybe it's safer to stick to standard English until you build more confidence.

I remember my first job at an MNC. The official company language was English, right? But halfway through a project meeting, the local managers suddenly start chatting in Hokkien and Mandarin, then drop a few Malay words like 'agak-agak' or 'tolong'. The foreign consultant just sit there looking confused, smiling politely, but you can see the sweat forming on his forehead.

Wah, that is classic. But honestly, is that Singlish or is that just mixed language? Because Singlish is not just mixing words. It's a whole grammar system. The particle system, the omission of articles, the lack of past tense markers. That's what makes it distinct.

Correct. And when you use those grammatical features in a professional setting, that's when HR usually raise an eyebrow. Like if you write an email saying, 'I already send the file yesterday, you check already or not?' The grammar is technically incorrect in standard English, but in Singlish it's perfectly fine. However, to a client or a senior manager, it might read as careless.

So the medium matters a lot. Written versus spoken. I think most Singaporeans agree that spoken Singlish in internal meetings is acceptable, sometimes even encouraged for team bonding. But written communication, especially emails to external parties, must be cleaned up.

But even then, what about internal chats? Slack, WhatsApp, Microsoft Teams. Do you still need to write full standard English? Sometimes I just type 'ok leh', 'noted mah', 'can lor', and everyone understand. Why must I write 'Understood, I will proceed accordingly. Thank you.' It feel so robotic.

I think it depends on company culture. Some organizations have strict communication guidelines. They monitor tone and language even in internal channels. Others don't care as long as the work get done. But you have to be careful. Text messages can be forwarded, screenshots can be taken. What seems casual to you might be used against you later if things go sour.

That's the kiasi mindset creeping in again. We so scared of getting into trouble that we over-correct everything. We start sounding like corporate manuals. 'Per our previous correspondence, kindly revert at your earliest convenience.' Wah, nobody talk like that in real life.

But if you don't use that language, people might think you are not serious. I tell you, during my performance review, my manager actually say, 'Your technical skills are strong, but your communication needs to be more polished.' I ask him what he mean. He say my reports too conversational, too many filler words, too informal. I was like, 'Boss, I write how I speak. What's wrong with that?'

And that's a valid feedback, actually. Not because Singlish is bad, but because workplace communication often requires precision and clarity. When you omit articles, drop linking verbs, or use vague particles, you leave room for misinterpretation. In a fast-paced corporate environment, misinterpretation can lead to costly mistakes.

So it's not about right or wrong, it's about function. If your goal is to build rapport and keep the team relaxed, Singlish works beautifully. If your goal is to draft a contract, present a financial forecast, or address a crisis, you need standard English to ensure everyone is on the same page.

I agree with that. But the problem is we often judge people based on their language choice rather than their actual competence. I know very smart people who speak perfect standard English, but their ideas are shallow. And I know brilliant engineers who speak heavy Singlish, but their solutions save the company millions. Yet, the engineers often get passed over for promotion because they 'lack executive presence'.

That is a real bias. 'Executive presence' is often just code for 'speak like an ang moh' or 'sound polished and neutral'. It's unfair, but it's the reality of globalized business. Many MNCs still use Western communication standards as the benchmark. If you don't fit that mold, you have to work twice as hard to prove your value.

So what's the solution? Do we just accept that Singlish will always be seen as inferior in professional settings? Or do we push back and redefine what professional communication means in Singapore?
RB WP VBZ DT NN . VBP PRP RB VB IN NNP MD RB VB VBN IN JJ IN JJ NNS . CC VBP PRP VB RB CC VB WP JJ NN VBZ IN NNP .

I think we need to meet in the middle. We can't force the whole world to learn Singlish, but we also shouldn't erase our own linguistic identity just to fit a foreign mold. The key is contextual intelligence. Know when to lean in, know when to step back. Treat language like a toolkit, not a rulebook.

Exactly. And I think younger Singaporeans are already getting better at this. They grow up consuming global content, working with international teams, but they also value their local culture. They don't see Singlish as 'broken English'. They see it as a cultural marker. They use it strategically.

That's a good point. I notice that in tech startups and creative agencies, Singlish is almost the default. It's part of the brand identity. 'We are local, we understand the market, we speak the language of the people.' It builds trust. You walk into a traditional law firm or audit house, and the vibe is completely different. Very stiff, very formal.

Because their clients expect that formality. If a lawyer start his court submission with 'Wah lau, your honour, this case very sian one', he will probably get disbarred. The industry dictates the register. You can't just impose your personal style on a field that relies on tradition and precedent.

But even within formal industries, the internal culture is shifting. I know accountants who use Singlish during team huddles to decompress after a long audit season. I know bankers who switch to colloquial speech when training fresh grads because it makes the information more digestible. It's about adaptability.

Let's talk about the digital age. Zoom calls, remote work, hybrid setups. How has that changed the Singlish debate? When everyone is on camera, speaking into a microphone from their HDB flat, does the language barrier become more or less important?

I think it actually makes it more complicated. On Zoom, you lose a lot of non-verbal cues. You can't read body language as well, you can't catch subtle reactions. So people rely more on clear, structured speech. If you mumble or use too many local particles, foreign participants might tune out.

And there's also the audio quality issue. If your mic is bad, or your internet is lagging, Singlish particles like 'lah', 'leh', 'lor' can get distorted or missed entirely. Then the message lose its tone. What sounds friendly might sound sarcastic. What sounds reassuring might sound dismissive.

In the corporate world, to some extent, yes. The general standard of written English in Singapore is very high. topic: Foreign workers in Singapore — fair treatment?
data: Alright, welcome back to another episode. Today we are diving into a topic that is always sensitive, always relevant, and frankly, something we don't talk about openly enough. Foreign workers in Singapore. Are they treated fairly? To help us unpack this, we have a very special guest. He is originally from Shenzhen, China, and has been working in Singapore's tech sector for the past four years. Welcome to the show, David.Guest B: Hello, hello. Thank you for having me. Very happy to be here. Actually, I listen to your podcast quite a lot during my MRT commute, so it is a bit surreal to be on the other side of the microphone now.

RB , PRP VBP IN PRP$ NN PDT DT NN IN PRP$ NNP NN , IN PRP VBZ DT NN JJ TO VB IN DT JJ NN IN DT NN RB .

Wah, that one is a huge compliment. But since you mention the MRT commute, let's start there. You been here four years. When you first landed at Changi Airport, what was your immediate impression of Singapore? Was it what you expected?

To be honest, my first impression was the heat. It is so humid. I step out of the airport, and my shirt already stick to my back. But beyond the weather, I was quite surprised by the language. I thought, okay, I am Chinese, Singapore has a large Chinese population, I can just speak Mandarin and everything will be smooth. But very quickly I realize, the Mandarin here is very different. And the English, wah, the Singlish, it is a whole different level.

TO VB JJ , PRP$ JJ NN VBD DT NN . PRP VBZ RB JJ . PRP VBP IN IN DT NN , CC PRP$ NN RB VBP IN PRP$ NN . CC IN DT NN , PRP VBD RB JJ IN DT NN . PRP VBD , UH , PRP VBP NNP , NNP VBZ DT JJ JJ NN , PRP MD RB VB NNP CC NN MD VB JJ . CC RB RB PRP VBP , DT NNP RB VBZ RB JJ . CC DT NNP , NNP , DT NNP , PRP VBZ DT JJ JJ NN .

Hahaha, yes. The classic trap. Many people from China or Taiwan come here thinking they can just use standard Mandarin, and then they get hit with the 'lah', 'leh', 'lor', and the mixed Malay and Hokkien vocabulary. Do you remember your first 'alamak' moment?
NNP , UH . DT JJ NN . JJ NNS IN NNP CC NNP VBP RB VBG PRP MD RB VB JJ NNP , CC RB PRP VBP VBN IN DT `` RP '' , `` RP '' , `` NN '' , CC DT JJ NNP CC NNP NN . VBP PRP VB PRP$ JJ `` NN '' NN .

Oh, definitely. My first month here, I go to a hawker centre near my office. I want to order chicken rice. I point to the stall and I say to the uncle, 'Uncle, I want one plate chicken rice, less oil, more cucumber, thank you.' Very polite, standard Mandarin. The uncle just look at me, blink two times, and say, 'Can, can. But you want to pack or makan here?' I was so confused. I didn't know what 'makan' meant. I just nod and say 'yes'. Then he shout to the back, 'One chicken rice, dabao!' I end up standing there with a plastic bag, realizing I have to eat on the go. I felt so siao at that time.

UH , RB . PRP$ JJ NN RB , PRP VBP IN DT NNP NN IN PRP$ NN . PRP VBP TO VB NN NN . PRP VBP IN DT NN CC PRP VBP IN DT NN , '' NNP , PRP VBP CD NN NN NN , JJR NN , RBR NN , VBP PRP . '' RB JJ , JJ NNP . DT NN RB VBP IN PRP , VBP CD NNS , CC VB , `` MD , MD . CC PRP VBP TO VB CC VB RB . '' PRP VBD RB JJ . PRP VBD RB VB WP `` JJ '' VBD . PRP RB VBP CC VBP `` UH '' . RB PRP VBD IN DT NN , `` CD NN NN , NNS . '' PRP VBP RP VBG RB IN DT JJ NN , VBG PRP VBP TO VB IN DT NN . PRP VBD RB NN IN DT NN .

That is a rite of passage, my friend. Every foreigner has that hawker centre initiation story. But let's move from the hawker centre to the office. You work in tech. There is this narrative that Singapore is becoming a hub for Chinese tech companies, and with that comes a certain work culture. How does the workplace reality here compare to what you left behind in Shenzhen?

DT VBZ DT NN IN NN , PRP$ NN . DT NN VBZ DT NNP NN NN NN . CC VB PRP VB IN DT NNP NN IN DT NN . PRP VBP IN NN . EX VBZ DT NN IN NNP VBZ VBG DT NN IN JJ NN NNS , CC IN DT VBZ DT JJ NN NN . WRB VBZ DT JJ NN RB VBP IN WP PRP VBD RB IN NNP .

This is a very interesting point. In China, especially in the tech sector, we have this term called 'neijuan', which translates to involution. It means intense, often pointless, internal competition. Everyone is working 996, 9 AM to 9 PM, 6 days a week, just to prove they are working hard, even if the actual output doesn't increase. When I first came to Singapore, I thought, wow, this is paradise. People actually leave at 6 PM. The office becomes empty. I thought I achieved work-life balance.

PRP VBD PRP VBD NN HYPH NN NN .
But I guess the reality is a bit more complicated than that, right?
CC PRP VBP DT NN VBZ DT NN RBR JJ IN DT , JJ .

Yes, very complicated. I soon realize that the 6 PM exodus is mostly the local Singaporean staff. They are very strict about their boundaries. They will say, 'Sorry, I have family commitment,' or 'I have my kid's tuition class,' and they just log off. But for the foreign EP holders, especially those from China or India, there is a hidden, unspoken expectation. The management assumes that because you are on an employment pass, you have no family here, no roots, so you should be available to work late.

Wah, that is a very sharp observation. So it is a double standard. The locals are protected by this unwritten rule of 'family first', but the foreigners are expected to be the perpetual workhorses to justify their visa sponsorship.

Exactly. And it is not just about working late. It is about the 'kiasu' culture clashing with the 'neijuan' culture. Singaporeans are kiasu, they want to secure their position, but they also want to follow the rules. They will work hard, but they will also make sure they take their annual leave. For some of my Chinese colleagues who just arrived, they don't take leave because they are afraid of being seen as not dedicated. Then the local colleagues start to resent them, because it makes the whole team look bad, like we are all supposed to be working 24/7. It creates a very toxic tension.

It is frustrating, to be honest. Because the term 'foreign talent' is so broad. It lumps me, a software engineer on an EP, together with a construction worker on a Work Permit, or a domestic helper. I think this is where the 'fair treatment' question really fails. The system is inherently tiered. As an EP holder, I can rent a nice condo, I can bring my family, I can change jobs relatively easily. But for the guys working in construction, or the F&B sector, or the delivery riders, their lives are completely controlled by their employers. I live in a condo, and right next to it, there is a massive construction site. I see these workers every day. They wake up at 5 AM, they work in the blazing sun, they eat simple meals from a plastic container, and they live in dormitories that are far away from the city centre.

And the recent years have shown us how fragile their situation is. During the pandemic, they were the most affected, locked down in dorms, yet they were the ones building the hospitals and keeping the city running.

Yes, and even now, post-pandemic, the inequality is very visible. I see delivery riders rushing through the red lights because the algorithm penalizes them for being late. I see them waiting in the rain outside the fancy restaurants, unable to even sit inside to rest because they are not 'patrons'. It makes me feel very sian and guilty. I benefit from this system. My food is delivered cheaply and quickly because their labor is undervalued. Is that fair? No. But as an individual, what can I do? I can tip them, I can say thank you, but I cannot change the structural exploitation.

Oh, absolutely. When I first tried to rent a room in an HDB flat, I contacted maybe ten different landlords. I spoke to them in very polite, standard Mandarin or English. Yes. And I understand the landlords' fears, right? They are afraid of overcrowding, they are afraid of cultural clashes, they are afraid of the wear and tear. The 'chope' culture. This one really confuse me at first. I go to a food court, I see a packet of tissue paper on a table, but no one is sitting there. I think, oh, this table is free. I go to sit down, and then an auntie come over and glare at me, saying, 'Eh, young man, this one got people already, you cannot sit.' I was so embarrassed. In China, if a table is empty, you sit. You don't reserve it with a piece of tissue. It took me months to understand that the tissue packet is a legally binding contract in Singapore.

Hahaha! Yes, the tissue packet is sacred. It is the ultimate symbol of Singaporean kiasu-ism. But on a more serious note, what about the language barrier? We expect foreigners to speak English, but when they encounter Singlish, they often feel lost. Do you think locals make an effort to adjust their language for foreigners?

I think it is a mixed bag. In a professional setting, yes, Singaporeans are very good at code-switching. They will speak very clear, standard English. But in casual settings, like the hawker centre or the kopitiam, they don't switch. And I don't necessarily blame them. Why should they change how they speak to their own friends just because a foreigner is standing nearby? But it does create a barrier. I remember one time, my local colleague was complaining about a project, and he said, 'Wah, this client really siao one, keep on changing requirements, I damn sian already.' I just stood there, smiling, nodding, but I had zero idea what 'siao' or 'sian' meant in that context. I had to go back to my desk and Google it.

Yes, now I can say 'I damn sian' with the best of them. But it highlights a point. If we want fair treatment and integration, it has to be a two-way street. Locals can be a bit more patient and explain the slang, and foreigners must make an active effort to learn the culture, not just live in an expat bubble.

Speaking of the expat bubble, there is this perception that high-earning foreign professionals don't even try to integrate. They live in condos, send their kids to international schools, eat at the same expat bars in Holland Village or Clarke Quay, and never interact with the heartlands. Do you agree with this stereotype?

I think it is a stereotype for a reason, but it is not the whole truth. Yes, there are definitely people who choose to live in that bubble. It is comfortable. It is easy. You don't have to deal with the cultural friction. But I also know many foreigners who actively try to integrate. They take the MRT, they eat at hawker centres, they try to learn about Hari Raya or Deepavali. The problem is, sometimes the local society pushes them away. If a foreigner tries to speak Singlish and gets it wrong, they might be mocked or called 'cringe'. If they ask a question about local customs, they might be told, 'If you don't like it, you can go back to your country.' That kind of hostility makes people retreat into the bubble.

That is a very valid point. The 'love it or leave it' mentality is very strong in Singapore. We are a small, vulnerable nation, so we are very protective of our identity. But that protectiveness can sometimes cross the line into xenophobia.

Exactly. And it is ironic, because Singapore's entire success is built on foreign labor. From the construction workers who built the skyline, to the domestic helpers who allow Singaporean women to participate in the workforce, to the tech talents who drive innovation. Without foreigners, Singapore's economy would collapse. Yet, the narrative is often framed as 'foreigners are a burden' or 'foreigners are taking our resources'.

It is a cognitive dissonance. We want the economic benefits of globalization, but we don't want the social friction that comes with it. So, David, if you had a magic wand and could change one thing about how foreign workers are treated or perceived in Singapore, what would it be?
PRP VBZ DT JJ NN . PRP VBP DT JJ NNS IN NN , CC PRP VBP RB VB DT JJ NN WDT VBZ IN PRP . RB , NNP , IN PRP VBD DT JJ NN CC MD VB CD NN IN WRB JJ NNS VBP VBN CC VBN IN NNP , WP MD PRP VB .

I would change the narrative around the word 'foreigner'. Right now, it is a monolithic, negative label. I would want the government and the media to differentiate more clearly between the different tiers of foreign workers, and address the specific issues of each group. For the lower-wage workers, we need strict enforcement of labor laws, better living conditions, and a pathway to fair treatment that doesn't rely on the benevolence of their employers. For the mid-to-high level professionals, we need to foster a culture of genuine inclusion, not just tolerance. We need to be seen as contributors to the society, not just temporary economic units.

And what about the locals? What is your message to the Singaporeans listening to this?

My message is to practice a bit more empathy. Before you judge a foreigner for not returning a tray, or for speaking loudly, or for not understanding the queueing system, remember that they are navigating a completely new set of rules in a high-pressure environment. And also, recognize your own privilege. The fact that you were born here gives you a safety net that no foreigner has. A little bit of patience and a smile can go a very long way.

topic: How to find love in Singapore
data: Alright, welcome back to another episode of our show. Today we are tackling a topic that every single person in Singapore either loves or absolutely dreads. How to find love in this tiny little red dot. Host B: Wah, straight to the point. No warm-up, just attack the single people’s heart directly. You know some people listening to this right now are already feeling the pressure, right?

It is happening, but maybe not in the way people think. It is not like one day you come to the office, and a robot is sitting at your desk. It is more subtle. For example, in software development, AI can now write basic code, debug simple errors, or generate documentation. So, the demand for junior developers who only do repetitive coding tasks is definitely dropping. Companies are expecting one senior developer to do the work of three juniors, with the help of AI.

That's the travel bug, man. It changes your brain. You come back and you look at Singapore differently. You appreciate the safety, the cleanliness, the efficiency. That's why so many young Singaporeans now talking about the digital nomad lifestyle. You see all these posts on LinkedIn, 'I quit my corporate job, now I work from a beach in Thailand.'

That's the thing about Singaporean food culture. We are very spoiled. We have access to amazing hawker food every day, so when we travel, we are always comparing. 'Eh, this char kway teow not wok hei enough.' 'Eh, this chicken rice the rice not fragrant.' We are very critical. But when we find something good, we will queue up for it like madmen. You see a long queue of locals at a pho stall in Hanoi, the Singaporean instinct will kick in. 'Wah, so many people queue, must be very good.' Then we go and queue for one hour, just to eat a bowl of noodles.

Hahaha, the kiasu queueing culture. It follows us everywhere. Even overseas, we cannot resist a queue. I was in Jakarta, and I saw a queue outside some viral bakery. Totally. We justify our actions because we already commit the time and effort. But seriously, the bakery and cafe culture in SEA is very interesting. In Singapore, the cafe hopping is very expensive. You pay eight dollars for a latte, twelve dollars for a slice of cake. It's an everyday luxury that drains your wallet. But in Vietnam or Thailand, you can get an iced coffee with condensed milk for like one dollar, and it is so strong and sweet, it wakes up your soul. And the cafes there, they have so much character. Some are in old colonial shophouses, some are hidden in small alleys, with lots of plants and vintage furniture. You can sit there for three hours, just people-watching.

Yes, the cafe culture is very strong. But you know what I miss the most when I am overseas? The convenience of Singapore. In Singapore, you want to eat at 2 AM, you just go downstairs, the hawker centre or the mamak stall is still open. You want to buy groceries, the supermarket is open till midnight. You want to send a parcel, the courier is everywhere. In other SEA countries, everything closes early. In Bali, by 10 PM, the small shops on the street already start rolling down the shutters. If you want to eat late, you have to go to the touristy areas, and the price will triple.

The convenience is something we take for granted. The 24-hour economy. And the safety. I can walk alone at 3 AM in Singapore, and I feel completely safe. When I was in Manila, my friend tell me, 'Don't walk on the street at night, take a Grab even if it's just five hundred meters.' I was a bit shocked. I mean, I know Manila has a reputation, but to be that careful? It makes you realize how much we pay for that safety and order. We complain about the strict laws, the fines, the Nanny State. But when you are in a place where the rules are loose, you realize the rules are there to protect you.

That's a very mature way to look at it. When you are young, you want freedom, you want to rebel against the system. You think Singapore is too boring, too restrictive. 'Agak-agak' is the keyword. You must be flexible. I remember one time I went to Langkawi with my family. We book a nice resort by the beach. You have to let go of your need for control.

Your mother is very kiasu, but I understand her. When you pay good money, you expect good value. But you are right, you cannot control everything. Especially when you travel to the islands. The weather is a big factor. You plan to go island hopping, but then the wind is too strong, the boats cannot sail. You just have to sit by the pool and drink coconut water. In Singapore, if it rains, we just take the MRT or go to the mall. The weather doesn't stop our plans. But in the islands, the weather is the boss. You have to surrender to nature.

Surrender to nature, very poetic. But seriously, the island life is very relaxing. I love the slow pace. You wake up, you don't have a schedule. You just see where the wind blow. You eat when you are hungry, you sleep when you are tired. No KPI, no deadlines. Just the sound of the waves. That's the ultimate reset for the brain. I think that's why so many people burn out in Singapore and then go to the islands to recover. The islands heal you.

They do, but they also test your patience. The 'island time' concept. Everything is slow. You order a beer at the beach bar, it takes thirty minutes to arrive. Not because they are lazy, but because the bar is far from the kitchen, and the staff is busy chatting with another guest. In Singapore, if the waiter take thirty minutes to bring a beer, you will wave your hand and shout, 'Excuse me!' Over there, you just lean back, look at the sunset, and wait. You have to sync your internal clock with their rhythm. If you fight it, you will be miserable.

Syncing the internal clock, I like that. It's like a mental shift. You stop looking at your watch. You stop checking your phone every five minutes. You just exist in the moment. I think that's the real value of travel. It's not just about seeing new places or eating new food. It's about changing your state of mind. It's about breaking the autopilot mode that we live in every day in Singapore. Wake up, go to work, eat, sleep, repeat. Travel forces you to be present, because everything is new, and your brain has to process all the new stimuli.

But then after two weeks, the stress of work kicks in, and you go back to being a zombie.

Bali was last year. This year, I am thinking of doing a bit of a deeper dive into Indonesia. Not just Bali. I want to go to Yogyakarta, see the Borobudur temple at sunrise. And then maybe go to Raja Ampat for diving. I hear the marine life there is insane. But it's a bit of a trek. You have to fly to Sorong, then take a boat. It's not the usual 'fly and chill' holiday. It requires some effort.

Wah, Raja Ampat? That's next level. That's for the serious divers and nature lovers. The logistics must be a nightmare. But the pictures I see on Instagram, the water is so clear, the coral is so colorful. It looks like another planet. But you must be careful with the itinerary. Don't try to pack too many things. I know Singaporeans, we always want to maximize every dollar. 'I pay for the flight, I must see ten things in one day.' Then you end up exhausted, and you don't enjoy anything. You must build in some buffer time. Some 'agak-agak' time.

I am trying to learn that. In the past, my itinerary is like a military operation. 8 AM breakfast, 9 AM museum, 12 PM lunch, 2 PM temple. Very sian to follow my own plan. Now I try to just put one or two main activities per day. The rest is just wandering. Getting lost is part of the fun. Sometimes you turn a corner and you find a small local market, or a hidden cafe, or a street dog sleeping in the sun. Those are the moments you remember. Not the famous landmarks that you take a selfie in front of and then leave.

Getting lost is good, but you must know how to get back. I am very kiasi about my phone battery and my data roaming. I always make sure I have enough data, and I download the offline maps. Because when you are lost in a foreign country, and your phone die, that is true panic. No Grab, no translate app, no way to call for help. You are just a stranger in a strange land. I have a friend who went to rural Vietnam, his phone got stolen. He don't speak Vietnamese, he don't have money, he have to use hand gestures to ask the police for help. Very traumatic experience.

Alamak, phone stolen is the worst nightmare. That's why I always keep a photocopy of my passport and some emergency cash in a separate bag. You can lose your phone, you can buy a new one. But if you lose your passport, the whole trip is ruined. You have to go to the embassy, wait for days to get an emergency travel document. Very headache. Travel insurance is a must. I know young people think they are invincible, they say, 'I never get sick, I don't need insurance.' But then they slip in the bathroom and break their leg, and the medical bill is ten thousand dollars. Then they cry.

Correct, the medical bill. The healthcare in some SEA countries is not cheap if you go to the international standard hospitals. In Singapore, we are so used to the subsidized healthcare. You go to a polyclinic, you pay a few dollars. You go to a government hospital, it's still reasonable. But overseas, if you go to a private hospital for tourists, they will charge you like it's in the US. So the insurance is non-negotiable. It's the one thing you should not 'agak-agak' on. You must read the coverage carefully. Make sure it covers adventure sports if you are going to do scuba diving or trekking.

Good. Peace of mind is worth the extra fifty dollars. So, besides the diving, what else you want to do in Indonesia? You must try the food. Indonesian food is very diverse. It's not just nasi goreng and satay. Every island has its own specialty. In Yogyakarta, you must try the Gudeg. It's a young jackfruit stew, very sweet and savory. And the sambal, wah, the sambal in Indonesia is on another level. They use so many different types of chilies and spices. It's not just hot, it's complex.

I am very excited for the food. I am a bit worried about the spice level, though. I thought I can handle spicy food because I eat Singaporean mala and Thai tom yum. But my friend tell me, the Indonesian sambal is different. It's a slow burn. It starts at the back of your throat, and then it slowly creeps up. You eat it, you feel fine, then ten minutes later, your face start sweating, your nose start running. I must prepare my stomach. I will bring some antacids just in case.

Hahaha, the slow burn. I experience that in Padang food. The nasi padang in Singapore is quite localized, it's not that spicy. But when I went to West Sumatra, the authentic one, I eat one piece of rendang, and I need to drink three glasses of iced tea. But it's so good, you just keep eating. The coconut milk, the spices, the tender meat. It's a flavor explosion. And the way they serve it, they stack the plates on their hands, ten plates high, and they walk to your table and open the lids one by one. It's a performance. You just point at what you want. 'That one, and that one.' Very exciting.

Yes, the Nasi Padang experience. It's like a buffet, but they bring the food to you. You have to be careful, because they will put everything on the table, and you only pay for what you touch. If you touch the fish, you pay for the fish. So you must use your eyes to order, don't use your hands to point too closely, or the auntie will think you want to take it. Very tricky. But the variety is amazing. You can have ten different dishes on your table, and you just mix and match with the rice. It's a feast.

That's the beauty of SEA food culture. It's very communal, very generous. The portions are big, you always order too much, and then you have to pack it back to the hotel. 'Tapao' is a universal language. And the price is so reasonable. You can eat like a king for a few dollars. In Singapore, we are always calculating the cost per bite. 'This restaurant is too expensive, the portion too small.' But over there, you just enjoy the abundance. You don't feel guilty about ordering three different dishes.

The abundance is very satisfying. But you know what I miss when I am eating overseas? The efficiency of the hawker centre. In Singapore, you go to Maxwell, you chope a table with a tissue packet. You go and order, you pay with PayNow, you get your food in five minutes. You eat, you throw the trash, you leave. The whole process is thirty minutes. In Vietnam, you sit down, the auntie give you a menu, you order, then you wait. And wait. Then the food come, but they forget the chili. You have to wave and ask for the chili. Then you eat, you wait for the bill. The bill come, you have to ask for the receipt. The whole process is two hours. You don't go back to work, you just live there.

Hahaha, the two-hour lunch. That's the European style, but it's also very common in SEA. Time is not money over there. Time is for enjoying life. You sit at the cafe, you drink your coffee, you smoke your kretek, you talk to your friends. Nobody is rushing you. The table is yours for as long as you want. In Singapore, if you sit at a cafe for two hours after finishing your drink, the staff will start giving you the 'eye'. They will come and clear your table, even if you are still sitting there. The turnover rate is very important. The rent is too high, they cannot afford to let you loiter.

The rent is the root of all evil. It dictates everything. The speed of life, the size of the portions, the stress level. When you travel to places where the rent is low, you can feel the collective blood pressure drop. People are more relaxed, they smile more. They have time to chat with you. 'Where you from? Singapore? Oh, very nice. You like it here?' In Singapore, if you talk to a stranger, they will think you are a scammer or you want to sell them something. They will put on their earphones and look away. We are so guarded.

We are very defensive. We build walls around ourselves. The HDB flat is our castle, and we don't let people in. But when we travel, we are forced to break those walls. We have to talk to the taxi driver, we have to ask the shop owner for directions, we have to interact with other travelers at the hostel. And we realize that people are generally good. They are helpful, they are kind. It restores your faith in humanity. You come back to Singapore, and you try to be a bit more open. You smile at the cashier, you say 'thank you' to the cleaner. Small things, but it makes a difference.

That's a very good takeaway. Travel makes you a better person. It makes you more empathetic, more patient, more grateful. You realize that your way of living is just one of many ways. The Singaporean way is not the 'correct' way, it's just the way that works for our specific context. And when you see how people live in other countries, with less money, with fewer resources, but they are still happy, it makes you question our definition of success. Is it just about the salary, the car, the condo? Or is it about the quality of your daily life? The time you spend with your family, the joy you get from a simple meal?

Very philosophical, bro. I like it. You should be a life coach. But you are right, the definition of success is very narrow here. We are all running on the same treadmill. But when you step off the treadmill, even just for a week, you realize there is a whole world out there. And the treadmill is just a machine. You can choose to step off it permanently, or you can choose to run on it a bit slower. You have the power to choose. Travel gives you that perspective. It gives you the courage to design your own life, instead of just following the default script.

Exactly. The default script. Primary school, secondary school, JC, uni, NS, work, marry, buy house, have kids, retire, die. Very linear. Very predictable. But when you see the digital nomads in Bali, or the artists in Yogyakarta, or the monks in Chiang Mai, you realize there are so many other scripts. You can write your own. It might not be the most stable path, it might not make your parents happy, but it's yours. And I think that's the ultimate luxury. Not the private pool villa, but the freedom to choose.

Well said. But let's be real, not everyone can just quit their job and become a digital nomad. Some people have loans, they have aging parents to take care of, they have responsibilities. The freedom to choose is a privilege. But even if you cannot change your whole life, you can still take breaks. You can still travel. You can still inject a bit of that freedom into your daily life. You can choose to take a long weekend off, you can choose to explore a new neighborhood in Singapore, you can choose to try a new hobby. You don't have to fly to the other side of the world to find yourself. You can find yourself in a kopitiam in Tiong Bahru.

True, true. The micro-adventures. You don't always need a big trip. Sometimes you just need to change your routine. Take a different route to work, go to a park you never visit, talk to a colleague you usually ignore. Keep your mind open. But I must admit, the big trips are very special. The anticipation, the packing, the airport excitement, the landing in a new country, the smell of the air. It's a sensory overload that you cannot replicate at home. That's why we keep doing it, even though we complain about the packing and the jet lag.
