The airport excitement is very real. Changi Airport is so nice, it makes you want to travel just to experience the airport. You go to Jewel, you eat a nice meal, you buy some duty-free, you sit at the gate and people-watch. And then you board the plane, and you are in your own little bubble for a few hours. You disconnect from the world. No work emails, no WhatsApp messages. Just you, the seat, and the in-flight movie. It's a transition space. You are leaving one life and entering another. I love that feeling of being in the air. You are nowhere, you are everywhere.

The transit lounge is my favorite place. You are in a foreign country, but you don't have to clear immigration. You are in limbo. You can eat at a Thai restaurant in the Bangkok airport, buy some Japanese snacks, and sit on a sofa that looks like it's from a Scandinavian design store. It's a melting pot of cultures. You see people from all over the world, dragging their suitcases, looking tired but excited. You share a universal bond with them. You are all travelers, all seekers. You nod at each other, you share a smile. It's a very special community, even if it's temporary.

The universal bond of the traveler. I like that. We are all looking for something. Some are looking for relaxation, some are looking for adventure, some are looking for themselves. But we are all moving. We are all seeking a change of state. And when we find it, even just for a moment, we feel alive. That's the magic of travel. It wakes you up. It shakes you out of your complacency. It reminds you that the world is big, and you are small, and that is a very comforting thought.

Very comforting. It puts your problems into perspective. You worry about your promotion, you worry about your relationship, you worry about your HDB valuation. But then you stand in front of the Angkor Wat, or you dive in the coral reef in Raja Ampat, and you realize, 'Wah, my problems very small.' The world has been here for millions of years, it will be here long after I am gone. My KPI is not that important. My BTO queue number is not that important. What is important is that I am here, I am breathing, I am experiencing this moment. That's the ultimate truth.

Heartland mall is where the real Singapore shopping culture live. Orchard is for tourists and expats mostly. But Tampines, Jurong, Bedok, that's where locals actually spend time. And you notice the tenant mix also different. More local brands, more homegrown cafes, less luxury flagship. You see Uniqlo, you see Decathlon, you see FairPrice Finest, all next to each other. Very practical.

Language barrier in shopping actually not that bad. Most retail staff switch to Mandarin, Malay, Tamil, or just basic English depending on customer. You hear it all the time. "Hello, welcome, you looking for anything?" then immediately switch to "Ni yao zhao shen me?" or "Ada apa yang boleh saya bantu?" It seamless. Singaporeans code-switch naturally. But sometimes the Singlish mix is funny. You go to small boutique, staff say "This one very soft, you try look. Comfortable one. If you buy today, I give you small discount. You think how?" That's the pitch. Not aggressive, just friendly.

People bring fold chair, power bank, snacks. Like camping trip.

Community support is strong, but sustainability is bigger question. You buy local cotton tote bag, you use it once a week, you keep it in drawer. Is it eco-friendly? Or just another form of consumption with better marketing? We talk about reducing waste, but we still want new thing every season. Fast fashion, fast tech, fast food. Everything fast. Singapore pace life force us to prioritize convenience over longevity. You don't have time to repair, you replace. You don't have space to store, you discard.

Mind also need heart. You don't just process transaction, you feel emotion. You don't just fulfill order, you create moment. You don't just deliver product, you share joy. You don't just collect payment, you build trust. You recognize shopping as human act, not mechanical routine. You honor dignity, you respect choice, you celebrate freedom. You don't control, you accompany. You don't dictate, you support. You don't judge, you listen. You walk alongside customer, not ahead, not behind. You move together, you grow together, you succeed together. That is shopping. That is Singapore. That is us.

Let us start with the daily logistics. Jia En, I know you have been dealing with this personally with your dad recently. Do you want to share a bit about what a typical day looks like for you?

Alamak, where do I even start? Okay, so my dad is seventy-two, and he has diabetes and high blood pressure. My mom passed away a few years ago, so it is just him and me. On a normal weekday, I have to wake up at six in the morning to make sure he has eaten his breakfast and taken his medication. Then I rush to catch the MRT to get to the office by nine. But then, the anxiety starts. I am sitting in meetings, but my phone is on loud, just in case the domestic helper calls, or the polyclinic calls, or heaven forbid, the hospital calls.

I took a Grab straight to the polyclinic. By the time I got back to the office, the meeting was over, and my boss gave me this look. You know the look. The 'your career is over' look.

That is a classic, painful scenario. And the sad part is, many employers in Singapore still operate with this very rigid, old-school mindset. They see caregiving as a personal problem, not a workplace issue. They think, 'Why cannot you just hire a helper?' But they do not understand the financial and emotional complexity of that.

Let us talk about that. The helper situation. It is not as simple as just paying someone to take over, right?
VB PRP VB IN DT . DT NN NN . PRP VBZ RB RB JJ IN RB VBG NN TO VB RP , JJ .

Not at all. First of all, the cost. The monthly levy, the salary, the insurance, the medical check-ups. For an average middle-income family, hiring a full-time live-in helper can easily cost over one thousand to fifteen hundred dollars a month. That is a huge chunk of the household income. And then there is the issue of trust. You are leaving your vulnerable elderly parent with a stranger. What if the helper is not trained in elderly care? What if they do not speak the same dialect? My dad only speaks Teochew. If the helper only speaks English or Mandarin, communication is a huge barrier.

So true! My dad gets very frustrated when he cannot express himself. He will just say, 'Never mind, never mind,' and then he does not eat properly because he is too proud to ask for help. And then I come home, and I have to play detective. 'Dad, why is the rice still in the cooker? Did you eat?' He will say, 'I ate already, I ate at the coffee shop.' But I know he is lying because his blood sugar readings are all over the place. It makes me so angry and sad at the same time.

That emotional toll is something we rarely quantify. We talk about the financial cost, but the mental load of constantly worrying, feeling guilty, and trying to manage everything is immense.

Absolutely. And this is where the concept of filial piety, which is so deeply ingrained in our Asian culture, becomes a double-edged sword. We are taught from young that we must take care of our parents. It is our duty. But in modern Singapore, the economic reality is that both husband and wife often have to work full-time just to survive the high cost of living. So you have this clash between traditional expectations and modern economic survival.

And do not even get me started on the sibling dynamics. I am an only child, so I bear the full brunt of it. But I have friends who have siblings, and the fights over who does what are legendary.

Yes, and it gets very messy. Often, the daughter who lives closest to the parents ends up doing the majority of the hands-on caregiving, while the son who lives overseas or is 'too busy with his career' just sends a red packet during Chinese New Year and thinks his duty is done. This causes massive resentment. I have seen families completely fracture over this. The caregiver burns out, feels unappreciated, and the relationship with the parents and siblings deteriorates.

That is a very common pattern. So, Darren, from a systemic perspective, what support is actually out there for these working caregivers? We hear about government grants, but are they accessible?

There are schemes, yes. For example, the Home Caregiving Grant provides some monthly cash payouts to help with caregiving expenses. There is also the Caregiver Relief, which gives a tax rebate. But the reality on the ground is that the amounts are often not enough to cover the actual costs. And the application processes can be quite bureaucratic and confusing for the elderly parents to navigate on their own. The working child ends up having to take time off work just to fill out forms and go to the Community Development Council to apply for aid. It is a bit of a catch-22.

And what about medical leave? Can we use our own medical leave to care for our parents?

Technically, no. Your medical leave is for your own health. Some progressive companies offer 'caregiver leave' or allow you to use your annual leave flexibly, but this is still the exception, not the norm. Most workers have to burn their precious annual leave, or worse, take no-pay leave, which affects their bonus and career progression.

Wah, that one hits hard. The gender disparity in caregiving is so real. Society still expects women to be the default caregivers, even if they are working full-time. It is like having two full-time jobs.

And the burnout is real. Caregiver burnout is a recognized psychological condition. The chronic stress, the lack of sleep, the financial strain, and the emotional grief of watching your parents decline. It leads to depression, anxiety, and physical health issues for the caregiver. We are seeing more and more middle-aged adults in my practice who are breaking down because they have been holding everything together for too long.

So what can we do? We cannot just end the podcast on a depressing note. We need some actionable advice, some 'agak-agak' solutions, or maybe some solid strategies for our listeners who are in this situation.
RB WP MD PRP VB . PRP MD RB RB VB DT NN IN DT JJ NN . PRP VBP DT JJ NN , DT `` JJ HYPH JJ POS NNS , CC RB DT JJ NNS IN PRP$ NNS WP VBP IN DT NN .

First, communication is key, but it has to be structured. Families need to have a 'family meeting' when everyone is calm, not during a crisis. Sit down, list out all the caregiving tasks, and divide them based on ability and resources, not just proximity. If a sibling cannot provide physical care, they must contribute financially to hire a part-time helper or a day care service. It has to be fair.

And for those who can afford it, explore elderly day care centers. My dad goes to a day care center twice a week now. It is not cheap, but it gives me a break, and he gets to socialize with other uncles, play mahjong, and do light exercises. He actually enjoys it. It is a win-win.

That is a great point. Utilizing community resources. There are also many voluntary welfare organizations, like the Agency for Integrated Care (AIC), that can provide assessments and link you to the right services. Do not try to be a superhero and do everything alone. That is the 'kiasu' mentality talking, thinking you must do it all perfectly by yourself.

Yes, let go of the perfectionism. Your parents do not need you to be perfect; they need you to be present and sustainable. If that means ordering food delivery because you are too tired to cook, so be it. If that means hiring a part-time cleaner, do it. Protect your own mental health first, because if you collapse, who will take care of them?

Also, set boundaries with your employer. I know it is scary, but you have to advocate for yourself. If your company has a flexible working arrangement policy, use it. Ask to work from home two days a week so you can be around for your parents. Frame it in terms of productivity. 'I can work more effectively from home because I can manage my family responsibilities without the stress of commuting.'

And to the employers listening to this podcast, please take note. Supporting your employees' caregiving responsibilities is not just a nice-to-have CSR initiative. It is good business. If you lose a trained, experienced employee because they had to resign to care for a parent, the cost of recruiting and training a replacement is far higher than granting them some flexible hours.

Well said, Marcus. We need a cultural shift in the workplace. We need to normalize talking about eldercare the same way we talk about childcare. Paternity leave is becoming more common; caregiver leave should be the next frontier.

That is a very valid point. We have gamified and digitized every aspect of our survival here. But let us talk about the physical toll. Because it is not just mental. Darren, you review gadgets. You must see the physical issues people are facing.

Oh, the physical issues are rampant. We call it 'text neck'. People are walking around with their heads tilted down at a 45-degree angle all day. The cervical spine is not meant to hold the weight of a human head in that position. I see teenagers at the bus stop, their necks are practically bent like a prawn. And then there is the thumb strain. Carpal tunnel syndrome, but in your thumb. I actually had a friend who had to go to the polyclinic because his thumb was locked in a bent position from scrolling too much. The doctor literally told him to stop using his phone.

The revenge bedtime procrastination. We stay up late scrolling because it is the only time of the day we have control over our own time. During the day, we belong to our bosses, our clients, our families. At night, in bed, with the lights off, the phone is our little escape.

That is so true, Marcus. It is a psychological rebellion. 'I don't want tomorrow to start yet, so I will just stay in this digital bubble for a few more hours.' But the blue light suppresses melatonin, so your brain thinks it is still daytime. You are trapped in a vicious cycle. You are tired, so you scroll to relax. But the scrolling ruins your sleep, so you wake up more tired, and you need more coffee and more scrolling to get through the day.

I think the key is not a full detox, but 'friction'. You need to add friction between you and the apps that suck your time. For example, I deleted the TikTok and Instagram apps from my phone. I only access them through the web browser on my laptop. It is so much more annoying to log in, to navigate the UI on a browser, that I only do it when I actually need to post something for work. I don't just mindlessly scroll on the toilet anymore.

I turned off all non-human notifications. If it is not a direct message or a call from a real person, I don't want to know about it. My phone is completely silent unless someone is actually trying to reach me. It changed my life. I am not constantly in a state of alert.

topic: Renting a room vs buying a flat
data: Welcome back to The Wealth Kopi podcast, everybody. I am your host Ryan, and sitting across from me today is a man who needs no introduction in the Singapore property and finance scene. He is a financial specialist, a property investor, and someone who literally crunches the numbers so we don't have to cry over our bank statements. Please welcome Melvin. Melvin, welcome to the show.

Thanks Ryan. Happy to be here. Wah, you make me sound very serious lah. Actually I just a guy who likes to count money and complain about HDB rules sometimes.

Haha, I think the audience loves the complaining part. That is why your Telegram group so active. But today, we are tackling the ultimate Singaporean headache. The debate that causes fights at the dinner table, arguments between couples, and a lot of anxiety for our fresh grads. We are talking about: Renting a room versus buying a flat. Is it better to just rent a master room in a condo, or should you stretch yourself to buy a BTO or a resale HDB? Melvin, let us start with the basics. When a 23-year-old fresh grad comes to you, earning maybe three thousand, three thousand five hundred dollars, what is the first thing you tell them?

The first thing I tell them is, relax, do not panic. You know Singaporeans right, very kiasu. They see their friends posting on Instagram, 'Yay, got keys for our BTO!', then they start to panic. They think they are falling behind in life. But I always say, renting a room when you are young and starting your career is not a failure. It is actually a very strategic move, if you do it right.

Strategic move? But the traditional Asian mindset, especially our parents, will say renting is throwing money away. 'You pay rent, the money go into the landlord's pocket. You buy house, the money go into your own pocket.' That logic is wrong meh?
JJ NN . CC DT JJ JJ NN , RB PRP$ NNS , MD VB NN VBZ VBG NN RB . `` PRP VBP NN , DT NN VBP IN DT NN POS NN . PRP VBP NN , DT NN VBP IN PRP$ JJ NN . '' DT NN VBZ JJ RP .

That logic is very outdated, Ryan. Let us break it down. When you rent a room, yes, you are paying for a roof over your head. That is a consumption cost, just like you pay for makan, you pay for Grab, you pay for Netflix. You are buying a service. But when you buy a flat, people think all the money goes into their own pocket. Alamak, that one is a huge illusion. When you buy a flat, you pay buyer stamp duty, you pay legal fees, you pay renovation costs. Then you take a loan, and for the first ten years, the majority of your monthly installment is just paying the bank's interest. You are not building equity; you are paying the bank for the privilege of borrowing their money. Plus, you got property tax, you got maintenance fee if it is a condo, or the sinking fund for HDB. So the idea that buying is purely 'saving' and renting is purely 'wasting' is a myth.

That is a very good point. So you are saying the cost of housing is there whether you rent or buy. The difference is just how you manage your cash flow and your opportunity cost.
DT VBZ DT RB JJ NN . RB PRP VBP VBG DT NN IN NN VBZ RB IN PRP VBP CC VBP . DT NN VBZ RB WRB PRP VBP PRP$ NN NN CC PRP$ NN NN .

Exactly. Let us look at the opportunity cost. Suppose you rent a nice master room with attached bath in a condo near the CBD. Let us say it costs you two thousand dollars a month. If you buy a one-bedder condo in the same area, your mortgage installment, including interest, property tax, and MCST, might be three thousand dollars a month. Plus you need a huge downpayment in cash and CPF. If you rent, you keep that huge downpayment invested in the S&P 500 or a robo-advisor, historically returning seven to eight percent a year. Over ten years, that compound interest can be very significant. So financially, renting and investing the difference can sometimes beat buying, especially in a high-interest-rate environment.

But Melvin, let us be real. The psychological factor. When you rent a room, you are always at the mercy of the landlord. The landlord can decide to sell the house, and suddenly you have to move out in two months. Or the landlord decide to increase the rent by three hundred dollars. You have no control. When you own your flat, at least you have security. You can knock down a wall, you can paint it pink, you can get a dog. That intangible benefit, how do you quantify that?

You cannot quantify it in a spreadsheet, Ryan, but it is very real. The 'own stay' factor. In Singapore, we have this deep-seated desire for security. When you own your home, nobody can chase you out. You have the kampong spirit, you can renovate to your exact taste. But then again, we must ask, is that security worth the financial stretch? I have clients who bought a resale flat, stretched their TDSR to the max, 55 percent. Then they lose their job, or they get their bonus cut. Suddenly, that 'security' becomes a huge burden. They are house rich, but cash poor. They cannot even afford to go for a holiday. So the psychological stress of the mortgage can sometimes outweigh the psychological stress of renting.

That is very true. The 'house poor' phenomenon. Let us talk about the couple scenario. This is very common. A couple, both working, combined income maybe eight thousand dollars. They want to get married, they want to apply for a BTO. But the wait time for a BTO now is like four, five years, especially if they want a mature estate like Bidadari or Tampines. So they say, 'Okay, we wait for the BTO, but in the meantime, we need a place to stay. Should we rent a room, or should we just buy a resale flat now so we can move in immediately?'

MD PRP VB DT NN , CC MD PRP RB VB DT NN JJ RB IN PRP MD VB IN RB . ''

This is the classic dilemma. The wait time for BTO is the biggest driver of the rental market in Singapore. If you wait four years for your BTO, and you rent a room for those four years, let us do the math. You pay maybe fifteen hundred dollars a month for a decent room. Over four years, that is seventy-two thousand dollars. That is a lot of money. Your parents will say, 'Wah lao, seventy-two thousand! You can use that to pay for the renovation of your BTO!' Yes, that is true. But if you buy a resale flat now, you need to pay cash over valuation, you need to pay agent fees, and you are locking up your CPF. If your career is not stable yet, or you plan to have kids and maybe one parent wants to stop working to take care of the baby, that resale flat mortgage will choke you. So my advice to couples is, if your career is still in the growth phase, renting a room or a small studio gives you the flexibility to move closer to your new office, to downgrade or upgrade as your income changes. You are buying flexibility.

Flexibility is a good word. But let us talk about the 'House Hacking' strategy. This is very popular among the young investors. They buy a four-room or five-room HDB flat in a non-mature estate, where the price is still relatively affordable. They stay in the master bedroom, and they rent out the other three bedrooms to tenants. The rental income covers the entire mortgage, and sometimes even gives them a positive cash flow. They call it 'using other people's money to pay for your house'. Is this a good strategy, or is it just a recipe for high blood pressure?

Haha, the 'House Hacking' strategy. On paper, it is beautiful. You get to live in a big house, you build equity, and your tenants pay for your mortgage. It is the ultimate kiasu move because you are getting an asset without using your own cash flow. But in reality? It is very sian. You are basically running a mini hotel. You have to deal with tenants. The tenant in the common room, he flush the toilet with wet wipes, the pipe block. Who you call? You call. The tenant in the other room, he bring his girlfriend back every weekend, use up all your water and electricity. You want to complain, but you also know you need his rent money. So you swallow your anger. And remember, HDB has strict rules. You can only rent out the bedrooms, you cannot partition the living room. You must register the tenants with HDB. If you try to do funny things, like subletting without approval, you can get fined or even have the flat compulsorily acquired. So yes, financially it makes sense, but emotionally, you need to have the patience of a saint.

The patience of a saint, I like that. I remember you actually tried the co-living model yourself, right? You rented a place, fitted it up, and rented out the rooms. Tell us a bit about that experience. Is it different from just being a normal landlord?

Yes, I did that as a social experiment, and also to understand the co-living business model. I rented a three-bedroom apartment, and I fitted it up to make it look very aesthetic, very 'Instagrammable'. I targeted young professionals, expats, people who want a community, not just a room. I provided cleaning services, high-speed wifi, and a nice living area. The margin was actually quite decent. But the headache is the operations. You are not just a landlord; you are a customer service officer. The tenant complain the aircon not cold, you must go and check. The tenant lose his access card, you must go and replace. And the cost of customer acquisition is high. You need to pay for listings on PropertyGuru, or pay agents. If the room is vacant for one month, your profit for that month is zero. So co-living is not passive income. It is a very active business. If you are a normal person who works full-time and just want to earn some passive income, doing co-living will make you siao.

So for the normal person, just renting out a room in your own HDB is already enough headache. Let us shift to the intangible factors. The 'kampong spirit'. When you rent a room in an HDB, you are living with the owner, or you are living with other tenants. You share the living room, the kitchen. Sometimes you bond, you eat together, it is very warm. But sometimes, you just want to be alone, and you hear the owner's family arguing in the living room, or the other tenant cooking curry at 11 PM and the whole house smell of spices. How do you navigate that?

That is the gamble of renting a room in an HDB. The human element. If you get a nice, chill owner who respects your privacy, it is very shiok. You come home, they offer you a piece of watermelon, you chat a bit. It feels like home. But if you get a strict, noisy, or intrusive owner, it is a nightmare. You feel like you are living in a dormitory. You cannot bring friends back, you cannot cook what you want, you have to schedule your bathroom time. This is why many young professionals prefer to rent a condo, even if it is just a single room. They want the privacy, the facilities, the gym, the pool. They don't want to deal with the 'auntie' factor. But of course, condo room rental is more expensive.

Let us bring in some voices from our audience. We always ask you guys on our Telegram and Reddit to share your stories. This week we asked: 'What is your biggest regret or realization about renting versus buying?' Let us read a few. This one is from user 'Tengah_Waiter'. He says: 'I applied for a BTO in Tengah three years ago. I thought, okay, I will just rent a room in the city while I wait. I paid fifteen hundred a month. Now my BTO is almost ready, but when I look at the resale prices in my area, they have gone up so much that my BTO grant feels like nothing. I feel like I paid rent for nothing, and I still have to take a huge loan for the BTO renovation.' Melvin, what do you say to him?

I say to him, first of all, don't regret. The rent you paid gave you a place to sleep, allowed you to work, and enjoy your youth in the city. You cannot time the market perfectly. If you bought a resale flat three years ago, maybe the price went up, but you also would have paid interest, tax, and maintenance. The property market in Singapore always goes up in the long run because of land scarcity and inflation. The key is not to regret the rent, but to use the BTO as a springboard. When you get your keys, renovate wisely, don't overspend. Live there for the MOP, and then you can decide whether to sell or rent it out. The BTO is still a heavily subsidized asset. You are still winning compared to those who buy private property at the peak.

Very good advice. Don't regret the past, plan for the future. Here is another one from 'Single_And_Sian'. She says: 'I am 32, single, earning six thousand. I can afford to buy a two-room BTO or a small resale flat. But I love my freedom. I love traveling three times a year. If I buy a house, my cash will be locked up, and I will have to downgrade my lifestyle. Is it wrong to just keep renting a nice condo room and invest the rest in stocks?'

This is a very valid lifestyle choice. We call it 'rentvesting' or just prioritizing lifestyle over property. If you are single, you don't need a huge space. You don't need to worry about school districts for your kids. If you love traveling, locking up fifty thousand dollars in cash for a downpayment and renovation is a bad idea. That fifty thousand could fund ten amazing trips to Europe or Japan. As long as you are disciplined to invest the difference in a diversified portfolio, you are building wealth. The only risk is if the property market rallies very hard, and your investment portfolio doesn't keep up. But historically, a well-diversified global portfolio can match or beat local property appreciation, without the hassle of being a landlord. So no, it is not wrong. It is just a different priority.

I love that. It is about aligning your financial decisions with your life goals, not just following the herd. Let us talk about the new HDB classification. The government introduced the Standard, Plus, and Prime models. For the Plus and Prime flats, there is a 15-year Minimum Occupation Period before you can sell or rent out the whole flat. How does this change the rent versus buy calculation for young couples?

PRP VBP DT . PRP VBZ IN VBG PRP$ JJ NNS IN PRP$ NN NNS , RB RB VBG DT NN . VB PRP VB IN DT JJ NNP NN . DT NN VBD DT NNP , NNP , CC JJ NNS . IN DT JJ CC NNP NNS , EX VBZ DT CD HYPH NN NNP NNP NNP IN PRP MD VB CC VB RP DT JJ JJ . WRB VBZ DT VB DT NN IN VB NN IN JJ NNS .

This is a game-changer, Ryan. The 15-year MOP for Plus and Prime flats basically kills the 'asset progression' strategy for those flats. In the past, people would buy a BTO, wait five years MOP, then sell it, collect the cash proceeds, and use it to buy a condo or a bigger flat. That was the classic Singaporean wealth-building playbook. But now, if you buy a Plus or Prime flat, you are locked in for 15 years. You cannot sell, you cannot rent out the whole flat. You are basically buying it for 'own stay' for the long term. So if you are a young couple, and you think you might want to upgrade to a condo in seven years, do not buy a Plus or Prime flat. You will be trapped. In that case, renting a room or buying a Standard flat, or even a resale flat with a shorter MOP, gives you much more liquidity and flexibility. The government is trying to remove the speculative element from public housing, and it is working. People must now buy a home to live in it, not to flip it.

That is a very crucial point. The end of the 'flipping' era for HDBs. So for our listeners who are currently renting a room, and they are thinking, 'Should I buy now before prices go up again?', what is your final verdict? What is the 'agak-agak' framework they should use to decide?

DT VBZ DT RB JJ NN . DT NN IN DT `` NN '' NN IN NNS . RB IN PRP$ NNS WP VBP RB VBG DT NN , CC PRP VBP VBG , `` MD PRP VB RB IN NNS VBP RP RB . '' , WP VBZ PRP$ JJ NN . WP VBZ DT `` JJ HYPH JJ '' NN PRP MD VB TO VB .

Okay, here is my framework. Number one, check your career stability. If you are in an industry that is volatile, or you plan to start a business, do not take on a huge mortgage. Rent a room, keep your cash liquid. Number two, calculate your total housing cost, not just the monthly installment. Include the interest, the tax, the maintenance, the opportunity cost of the downpayment. Compare that to the rent of a similar place. If the cost of buying is more than 1.5 times the cost of renting, it might be better to rent and invest the difference. Number three, look at your life stage. If you are getting married and having kids soon, the stability of owning a home, being able to renovate the kids' room, being near a good primary school, these are very important. The intangible benefits of ownership are high. But if you are single, or a young couple who loves to travel and value flexibility, renting is perfectly fine. Do not let society pressure you into buying a house if it makes you financially stressed. A house should be a place of peace, not a source of anxiety.

topic: Tuition and enrichment classes for kids
data: Welcome back to The Kopi O Parents podcast, everybody. I am your host Marcus, and sitting across from me today are two people who I know are probably running on three hours of sleep and a lot of caffeine. Please welcome Sarah, a mother of two, and David, a father of one. Welcome to the show, guys.

How much is too much? And are we actually helping our kids, or are we just feeding our own kiasu anxiety? Sarah, let us start with you. You have two kids, one in secondary school, one in primary. When did the tuition journey actually begin for your family?

Alamak, where to start? I think it started even before they can walk properly. You know the environment lah. When my daughter was in K1, I was already getting messages from my friends about phonics classes and Kumon. I told myself, 'No, I will let her be a kid. Let her play.' But then, by K2, all her classmates were already reading chapter books and doing two-digit addition. I started to panic. So, I enrolled her in a reading and math enrichment center just to make sure she can cope with Primary 1. That was the first domino. Once you start, you cannot stop.

That is the classic FOMO, Sarah. The Fear Of Missing Out. But I think it is not just FOMO, it is also the Fear Of Falling Behind. When my son was in P1, the MOE said no mid-year exams, no ranking. All the parents in the school group chat were like, 'Wah, so good, less stress.' But then, two months later, the same parents start whispering, 'Eh, how do you know your kid is doing okay? Should we get a private tutor to assess him?' The anxiety just shifted from the exams to the unknown. We are all cheong, just trying to guess what is going on.

Because nowadays, the labels are very blurred. You send your kid for 'enrichment', but it is basically just advanced math. Or they call it 'brain training'. What is the difference?

Very sian, Marcus. Very sian. Saturday morning, eight to ten, she has Math tuition at a center in Bishan. Then from ten fifteen to twelve, she has Physics tuition. Then we rush to eat a quick lunch at the mall, usually just a quick plate of chicken rice, and then at one o'clock, she has English tuition. Then at three, she has her violin lesson. By the time we reach home, it is already five thirty. She will go to her room, close the door, and just sleep. She does not even want to talk to me. Sometimes I look at her and I think, 'Am I a mother, or am I just a chauffeur and a ATM machine?'

That is heavy. The emotional toll on the kids is something we rarely talk about openly. We always focus on the financial cost, but the burnout is real. David, you mentioned your son is only seven. Do you see him showing signs of stress? Or is he still too young to realize he is in a rat race?

He is starting to realize. Just last week, he was playing with his Lego, and he suddenly said, 'Daddy, if I don't do my Kumon worksheet, will I fail P3?' I was shocked. Seven years old! Where did he even learn the word 'fail'? He probably heard me and my wife talking at the dinner table. It made me step back and think. We are so obsessed with preparing them for the future that we are ruining their present. But then again, if I stop the Kumon, and his classmates are all doing it, he will struggle in school. And if he struggles, his self-esteem will drop. It is a very vicious cycle.

Haha, oh my god, I can relate so much. The prestige of the tuition center. There are these famous centers in Bukit Timah or Bishan, and parents will queue up like it is a concert ticket sale just to get a slot. They think the building itself got some magic that will make their kid score an A. But honestly, at that age, it is more about the peer group. If you put a kid in a center where everyone is fighting to be the top, the kid will either thrive or he will break. It is a huge gamble.

Oh, my father is the worst. He is retired, he got nothing to do, so he reads all these newspaper articles about the PSLE cut-off points. He will call me and say, 'Eh, David, you know the cut-off point for ACS is 180 now? You better make sure your boy is ready.' I am like, 'Dad, he is in P2, he is still learning how to tie his shoelaces properly. Can you relax?' But they come from a different generation. They believe that education is the only way up, and that if you don't score well, you will go and sweep the streets. They don't understand that the world has changed. The definition of success is much broader now.

My mother-in-law is very kiasu also. She always compare my kids with her friends' grandchildren. 'Eh, Sarah, your girl got how many distinctions this term? My friend's granddaughter got six A-stars.' I just smile and nod, but inside I am screaming. Because I know my daughter is trying her best, but she is not a genius. She is an average kid. And in Singapore, being average is treated like a crime. The system is not designed for the average kid to feel good about themselves. It is designed to sort them, to rank them. And as parents, we are just the enforcers of this sorting mechanism.

Because the parents are also stressed! You cannot blame them entirely. The parents are working full time, they are dealing with their own bosses, they are worrying about the cost of living, the BTO loan, the retirement. When they look at their kid's education, it is the one thing they feel they can control. They think, 'If I can just make sure my kid gets good grades, then I have done my job as a parent.' It is a way to cope with their own anxieties. We project our fears onto our kids' report books.

That is very insightful, Sarah. We project our fears. We think that if we just spend enough money, if we just sign them up for enough classes, we can guarantee their success. But success is not a straight line anymore. Let us talk about the alternatives. Are there parents who are opting out of this rat race? How do they survive the social pressure?

I have a colleague who opted out. His kid is in P5 now, and he doesn't go for any tuition. No enrichment. The kid just goes to school, comes home, plays football, reads books. My colleague says, 'I want him to have a childhood. If he fails, he fails. I will support him.' But you know what? Even he admits it is hard. Because when the other parents ask, 'Eh, your boy going for which center?' He has to lie. He says, 'Eh, we are doing home tutoring,' just to avoid the judgment. Because in Singapore, if you don't do tuition, people think you are a bad parent, or you are poor, or you don't care about your kid's future.

That is so true. The stigma of not doing tuition. I have a friend who actually pulled her kid out of the math tuition because the kid was crying every day. She said her kid told her, 'Mommy, I hate numbers. I just want to draw.' So she stopped the math, and she signed him up for an art class instead. The kid is so much happier. But when she told this to her mother group, the other aunties looked at her like she was crazy. 'Wah, you let him draw? How he going to get into a good secondary school? Art cannot eat lah!' They just cannot understand that mental health and happiness are also important for learning.

'Art cannot eat lah.' The ultimate Singaporean pragmatism. But actually, with the new AL scoring system, and the changes in the Direct School Admission, DSA, maybe the definition of what is valuable is shifting. Have you guys noticed any changes in the tuition landscape because of these MOE policies?

Actually, the AL system made it worse, in my opinion. Before, under the T-score system, you know exactly where you stand. You are in the top ten percent, you are safe. Now, under AL, every mark counts. If you score 90, you get AL1. If you score 89, you get AL2. The difference is one mark, but it feels like a huge gap. So parents are even more paranoid. They are sending kids for 'careless mistake prevention' classes. I am not joking. There are actual tuition centers marketing themselves as 'Zero Careless Mistake Bootcamps'. It is absurd. They are monetizing our paranoia.

And the DSA is also a huge driver for enrichment. Parents think, 'Okay, my kid is not good at academics, let me find a backdoor.' So suddenly, everyone is doing robotics, everyone is doing debate, everyone is doing sports. The enrichment classes are no longer for fun; they are for building a portfolio. My daughter plays the violin, not because she loves music, but because we are hoping she can get DSA into a good school through the symphony orchestra. The joy of learning is completely stripped away. It is all transactional.

Transactional learning. That is a very sad state of affairs. We are treating our kids' hobbies like investments. 'If I put in ten thousand dollars for piano lessons, I should get a return in the form of a school admission.' Let us read one more comment from our audience. This is from 'Working_Mom_Sian'. She says: 'I work long hours. I reach home at seven pm every day. I feel so guilty that I don't have time to help my kid with his homework or play with him. So I signed him up for a 'study and care' center after school. They do his homework, they give him a snack, and they send him home. It is expensive, but it is the only way I can keep my job and not feel like I am failing my kid. Am I a bad mother?'

Oh, this one hits hard. The guilt of the working parents. This is a whole different layer. For many families, tuition and after-school care are not about the kiasu rat race; they are about survival. Both parents need to work to pay for the mortgage, for the car, for the basic living. They don't have grandparents around to help. So they outsource the parenting to the tuition centers. The center becomes the surrogate parent. And the parents feel guilty, so they overcompensate by buying them things, or by pushing them to score, because they want to see a 'return on investment' for the time they are missing.

Exactly. And the tuition centers know this. They market themselves not just as academic help, but as 'safe havens' and 'character building' centers. They tell the parents, 'Don't worry, we will take care of your child's holistic development.' So the parents feel better. They think, 'Okay, I am paying this center five hundred dollars a month, so they are doing the parenting for me.' But the kid just wants his mom and dad to read him a bedtime story. No amount of Kumon can replace that.

That is a very powerful point. No amount of Kumon can replace a bedtime story. So, as we wrap up this episode, what is your advice to the parents who are listening right now, who are feeling the pressure, who are looking at their kids' schedules and feeling like they are drowning? How do we find the balance?

My advice is to have a very honest conversation with your partner, and then with your kid. Sit down and look at the schedule. Is the kid happy? Is he sleeping enough? Is he having time to just be bored and use his imagination? If the answer is no, you need to cut something. And you need to be strong enough to face the other parents. You need to be okay with being the 'chill' parent. Your kid will thank you when he is older. Don't let the FOMO dictate your parenting choices. Your kid is an individual, not a project to be managed.

I agree with David. And I would add, redefine what success means for your family. Success is not just getting into Raffles or ACS. Success is raising a kind, resilient, and curious human being. If your kid is good at making friends, if he is empathetic, if he loves to read for fun, those are huge wins. Don't let the tuition centers convince you that only math and science scores matter. Protect your kid's childhood. Once it is gone, you cannot get it back. And honestly, the extra money you save from not paying for five tuition classes, you can use to bring them on a family holiday. That is the real enrichment.

topic: Local musicians and the Singaporean music scene
data: Welcome back to The Red Dot Sessions, everybody. I am your host Marcus, and today we are diving into a topic that is very close to my heart, and I am sure, to a lot of you watching at home. We are talking about the local music scene. The original sounds of Singapore. And to help me unpack this, I have two heavyweights in the industry with me today. First, an independent singer-songwriter who has been grinding in the local scene for over a decade, please welcome Jia En.

Let us start right at the elephant in the room. Or rather, the stadium in the room. We keep hearing about how Singapore is becoming the concert hub of Asia. Taylor Swift, Coldplay, Blackpink, everyone coming here. The government is spending millions to bring these acts. As local musicians, when you see these headlines, what is going through your mind? Is it a trickle-down situation, or are you just watching from the sidelines?

VB PRP VB RB IN DT NN IN DT NN . CC RB , DT NN IN DT NN . PRP VBP VBG IN WRB NNP VBZ VBG DT NN NN IN NNP . NNP NNP , NNP , NNP , NN VBG RB . DT NN VBZ VBG NNS TO VB DT NNS . IN JJ NNS , WRB PRP VBP DT NNS , WP VBZ VBG IN PRP$ NN . VBZ PRP DT NN HYPH RP NN , CC VBP PRP RB VBG IN DT NNS .

Honestly? It is a very mixed feeling. On one hand, you are happy that people are coming to Singapore, the economy is buzzing, the hotels are full. But on the other hand, you look at your own bank account, and you look at the attendance at your own gigs, and you realize the trickle-down is not reaching the ground level. I have friends who are amazing musicians, they play original songs, and they are struggling to sell fifty tickets at twenty dollars each. But the same Singaporeans will complain, then turn around and pay five hundred dollars for a concert ticket where they cannot even see the stage properly. It is a very weird cognitive dissonance.

CC IN DT JJ NN , PRP VBP IN PRP$ JJ NN NN , CC PRP VBP IN DT NN IN PRP$ JJ NNS , CC PRP VBP DT NN HYPH NN VBZ RB VBG DT NN NN . PRP VBZ DT RB JJ JJ NN .

I think Jia En hits the nail on the head. The 'concert hub' narrative is great for tourism, great for the F&B sector around the stadium, and great for the government's KPIs. But for the local grassroots music scene? It is almost like a completely different ecosystem. The money that comes in for these mega-events is top-down. It goes to the international promoters, the global ticketing platforms, the international crew. The local guy who plays at the pub down the street does not get a single cent from that. In fact, sometimes it makes it worse, because the local audience's entertainment budget is completely wiped out for the year. They spend all their money on the big acts, so when you try to book a local gig, they say, 'Eh, sorry lah, my wallet already empty, next time hor.'

That is a very harsh reality. The wallet is empty for local music. But why is that? Why is there this perception that local music is 'free' or should be very cheap, but international music is worth paying a premium for?
DT VBZ DT RB JJ NN . DT NN VBZ JJ IN JJ NN . CC WRB VBZ DT . WRB VBZ EX DT NN IN JJ NN VBZ `` JJ '' CC MD VB RB JJ , CC JJ NN VBZ JJ VBG DT NN IN .

I think it goes back to this deep-seated colonial hangup we have in Singapore. We always think that anything from overseas is automatically better. The ang moh sound, the Western production, it just sounds more 'legit' to the average consumer. When a local artist releases a song, the immediate reaction from a lot of Singaporeans is to critique it. 'Wah, the mixing not good lah', 'The accent very cringe lah', 'Why you sing in Singlish, so kampong lah'. But the moment that same artist goes to SXSW in Austin, or gets featured on a European indie blog, suddenly the same Singaporeans are like, 'Wah, so proud! Our local boy making it overseas! Let us go support!' We need that external validation before we are willing to open our wallets. It is very kiasu, and very sad.

PRP VBZ RB JJ , CC RB JJ .

Exactly. We call it the 'overseas validation loop'. You cannot be a hero in your own country until you have been knighted by the West. I see this all the time with the bands I work with. They will play a gig here, and maybe thirty people show up. Half of them are the other bands playing later, and the other half are the soundman and the bartender. But then they go on a small tour in Germany or the UK, and they come back and say, 'Darren, the crowd was so receptive, they actually bought our vinyls!' And I have to tell them, 'Yeah, that is how it is supposed to be everywhere, but in Singapore, people think buying your vinyl is like doing you a favour.'

Let us talk about that favour mentality. I remember a few years ago, a very prominent local indie band tried to charge a slightly higher ticket price for their album launch gig. Maybe thirty-five dollars instead of the usual fifteen. The internet went crazy. People were calling them greedy, saying they are just a local band, who do they think they are? But these are people who will queue for six hours to buy a limited edition Starbucks cup.

Wah, do not remind me. That album launch, I was the opening act. I remember standing backstage, looking at the ticket sales, and the organizer was just crying. Because people were messaging them, 'Eh, you are my friend right? Can I get comp ticket? I will promote for you on my Instagram story.' Like, bro, my Instagram story got like forty followers, and half of them are my relatives. How is that going to help me pay the sound engineer? The local scene is built on this expectation of free labour. We are expected to play for exposure. But exposure cannot pay my CPF, exposure cannot buy my rice and vegetables at the market.

And this 'exposure' culture is heavily reinforced by the venues as well. A lot of F&B outlets in places like Haji Lane or Clarke Quay will tell the bands, 'Eh, you play for free, but I will give you a free beer and a plate of fries.' And the young bands, they are so desperate to play, they say yes. Then they wonder why the industry is not professionalized. If you do not value your own time, how do you expect the audience to value it? You are training the market to think that live music is just background noise, like the air-conditioning or the lighting.

That is a very good point. The background noise mentality. Which brings me to the National Day Parade. The NDP. Every year, they feature local artists. The singers, the bands, they get to perform on national television. Millions of people watching. It must be a huge boost for their career, right?

Haha, Marcus, you are so innocent. The NDP effect is real, but it lasts exactly fifteen minutes. We call it the 'NDP Orphan' syndrome. You practice for three months, you sing the song, you cry on stage, the whole nation is very touched. The next day, you release your original EP. And nobody listens. Because the aunties and uncles who watched you on TV, they only know you as the 'NDP singer'. They do not know your genre, they do not care about your original art. They just want you to sing the patriotic song at the National Day concert at the community club. And if you say no, they get very angry. 'Eh, why you never sing the NDP song? You think you very famous already ah?'

It is true. The NDP is a double-edged sword. It gives you visibility, but it typecasts you. And the production value of the NDP is so high, it is almost impossible to replicate in a normal club gig. So when the artist goes back to their regular venue, the audience is like, 'Wah, your sound very thin today, where is the backing track? Where is the laser show?' It sets an unrealistic expectation for the local live scene.

So if the gigs do not pay, and the NDP is a trap, how do local musicians actually survive? What is the hustle like? Because we see the Instagram posts, the aesthetic photos in the studio, it looks very shiok.

The Instagram is a lie, Marcus. The reality is, ninety percent of local musicians have a day job. Or they do multiple side hustles. I teach piano to kids who do not want to practice, I do vocal coaching for corporate executives who want to sing at their company dinner, and I do wedding gigs. You have to be a chameleon. You have to be able to play Adele, then play Jay Chou, then play some Top 40 EDM, just to make sure you get the gig. The original music is the passion project, but the bread and butter is the cover band circuit.

And then there is the corporate sync and event market. I produce a lot of music for local advertisements, for government campaigns, for bank commercials. That is where the real money is. But it is very restrictive. You cannot be too edgy, you cannot say anything controversial. You just make a nice, uplifting, generic pop track. And a lot of indie artists hate doing it, but they do it because it pays five thousand dollars for two days of work. Compared to playing three original gigs and making maybe six hundred dollars. The math just does not lie.

Let us talk about the physical spaces. The venues. Darren, you run a live house. You must see the struggle of finding a place to play, and the struggle of keeping the doors open. What is the state of live venues in Singapore right now?
VB PRP VB IN DT JJ NNS . DT NNS . NNP , PRP VBP DT JJ NN . PRP MD VB DT NN IN VBG DT NN TO VB , CC DT NN IN VBG DT NNS JJ . WP VBZ DT NN IN JJ NNS IN NNP RB RB .

It is jialat. Honestly, it is very difficult. The biggest issue is the noise. Singapore is a very dense city. You open a live music venue, and the next day, the condo next door starts complaining about the bass. Then the NEA comes down, measures the decibels, and gives you a warning. Then you have to spend twenty thousand dollars to soundproof the walls. And even after you soundproof, some uncle will still call the police because he can feel the vibration in his toilet bowl. Because of this, a lot of the classic rock venues and indie spaces have closed down. We are left with very few mid-sized venues. You have the big arenas, and you have the small pubs that only want cover bands. The middle ground, the 300 to 500 capacity venues where a local band can actually grow and build a fanbase, is almost non-existent.

And when you do find a space, the rental is crazy. The landlord does not care if you are nurturing local talent. They just want their commercial rent. So the venue owner has to charge high bar minimums, or charge the bands a pay-to-play fee. Which again, hurts the grassroots scene. I remember back in the 2000s, there were so many live houses in the Bras Basah and Bugis area. You could just walk in, there was always a band playing. Now, everything is gentrified. It is all artisanal coffee shops and boutique fitness studios. You cannot play a punk rock gig next to a Pilates class.

That is a very vivid image. Punk rock next to Pilates. So the ecosystem is shrinking. What about government support? We have the National Arts Council, we have grants. Is that helping the local musicians?

The grants are there, yes. But the barrier to entry is very high. To apply for an NAC grant, you need to write a very detailed proposal, you need to provide financial projections, you need to have a proper accounting system. Most indie musicians are just guys in a bedroom with a laptop. They do not know how to write a grant proposal. So they have to hire a consultant or a producer to write it for them, which costs money. And by the time the grant is approved, six months have passed, and the momentum is gone. The grants are great for established companies and big festivals, but for the raw, emerging talent? It is very hard to access.

Also, the grant system sometimes forces you to create art that fits their criteria. They want 'community engagement', they want 'multiracial harmony', they want 'educational value'. Which are all good things, but art is not always about ticking boxes. Sometimes you just want to write a sad song about your breakup, or an angry song about the cost of living. But if you want the funding, you have to frame it as 'youth mental health awareness' or 'financial literacy through music'. It is very bureaucratic, and it stifles the raw creativity.

Let us shift gears a bit and talk about identity. The 'Singaporean Sound'. Is there such a thing? When we listen to local music, what makes it sound Singaporean? Is it just the accent, or is there something deeper?
VB PRP VB NNS DT NN CC VB IN NN . DT `` JJ NNP '' . VBZ EX PDT DT NN . WRB PRP VBP IN JJ NN , WP VBZ PRP VB JJ . VBZ PRP RB DT NN , CC VBZ EX NN JJR .

I think for a long time, local musicians tried very hard to sound American or British. They would sing with a fake transatlantic accent, they would write lyrics about snow and autumn and things they have never experienced. Because they thought that was what 'good music' sounded like. But recently, I think there is a shift. Artists are starting to embrace their local identity. You hear Singlish in the lyrics, you hear references to local food, to HDB living, to the MRT. You hear the fusion of different languages, like English, Mandarin, Malay, Tamil, all in one song. It sounds messy, it sounds chaotic, but that is exactly what Singapore is. It is a rojak. And I think the younger generation, the Gen Z artists, they are not afraid of that anymore. They think the ang moh sound is a bit cringe, and they want to sound like themselves.

I agree with Jia En. The 'Singaporean Sound' is not a specific genre. It is not like 'Singaporean folk' or 'Singaporean metal'. It is the attitude. It is the pragmatism, it is the humor, it is the underlying anxiety of living in this small red dot. When you listen to local hip hop, like the guys from Yung Raja or Subhas, they are rapping about their real lives in Singapore, the pressures, the NS, the cultural expectations. That is very authentic. And even in the indie pop scene, artists like Inch Chua or Charlie Lim, they write very observant, very local lyrics. They are not trying to be the next Ed Sheeran; they are trying to be the voice of their generation here.

But do you think the general public is ready for that? Because we still see a lot of comments online saying, 'Eh, Singlish in a song very unprofessional lah.'

The older generation, definitely. They still hold on to this idea that English must be 'proper' English. But the younger generation? They love it. When an artist drops a line in Hokkien or Singlish, the comments section goes wild. They feel seen. They feel like, 'Wah, this guy actually understands my life.' I think the internet and social media have broken down the traditional gatekeepers. You do not need the radio DJs to approve your song anymore. If you make a catchy song with local slang, TikTok will push it, and the kids will stream it.

And that is the other big factor. Social media. The algorithm. Local musicians now have to be content creators first, and musicians second. You have to do the TikTok dances, you have to post the behind-the-scenes vlogs, you have to reply to every single comment. It is very exhausting. I have artists who are so burnt out from the marketing that they do not even have the energy to write new songs. The industry has shifted from a meritocracy of talent to a meritocracy of attention. If you can grab attention for three seconds on a scrolling feed, you win. If you are just a great guitar player but you are shy on camera, you lose.

That is a very bleak picture. The content creator trap. So, for the young kids watching this, the ones who are in secondary school or poly, picking up the guitar, dreaming of being the next local superstar. What is your advice to them? Do you tell them to go for it, or do you tell them to study accounting?

DT VBZ DT RB JJ NN . DT NN NN NN . RB , IN DT JJ NNS VBG DT , DT NNS WP VBP IN JJ NN CC JJ , VBG RP DT NN , VBG IN VBG DT JJ JJ NN . WP VBZ PRP$ NN IN PRP . VBP PRP VB PRP TO VB IN PRP , CC VBP PRP VB PRP TO VB NN .

Haha, I always tell them, study accounting first, get a stable job, and then do music on the side. Because the music industry is very cruel. But if they really, really cannot live without music, I tell them to find their tribe. Do not try to appeal to the mass market. The mass market in Singapore is very fickle. Find the fifty people who really, really love your sound. Build a community with them. Sell them your merch, invite them to your secret basement gigs. If you can get a thousand people to pay you twenty dollars a year, that is already twenty thousand dollars. It is not a lot, but it is a start. And most importantly, do not compare yourself to the artists on Spotify. The streaming numbers are fake, the playlists are curated by algorithms. Just focus on the real human connection.

My advice is similar. Learn the business side. Understand what a copyright is, understand what a publishing deal is, understand how to read a contract. A lot of young artists just sign whatever the label puts in front of them because they are just happy someone wants to work with them. Then three years later, they realize they do not own their own masters, and they are in debt to the company. Protect yourself. And also, collaborate. The local scene is very small, we should not be competing with each other. If you are a producer, work with a vocalist. If you are a band, invite a visual artist to do your visuals. Build a collective. There is strength in numbers.

Strength in numbers. I like that. Because right now, it feels very fragmented. Everyone is in their own little silo. Let us bring in some voices from our audience. We asked our viewers on Instagram and YouTube to send in their questions and comments for our guests. Let us look at this one from 'GuitarBoy_SG'. He asks: 'How do we get local radio to play more original local music? It is always the same ten songs on repeat, and they are all from the 90s or 2000s.' Darren, what do you think?

Wah, the local radio question. That is a whole other headache. The radio stations in Singapore are commercial entities. They are driven by ratings and advertising revenue. And the data shows that the average Singaporean listener, especially the ones driving in the car during peak hour, they just want to hear familiar songs. They do not want to hear a new, unknown local indie track. They want to hear Backstreet Boys, they want to hear Mayday, they want to hear the old local classics like Kit Chan or Dick Lee. So the radio programmers play it safe. It is a vicious cycle. The radio does not play new local music, so the public does not know the new local music, so the radio says, 'See, nobody wants to listen to it.'

But I think the radio is becoming less and less relevant, honestly. The young kids are not listening to FM radio. They are on Spotify, they are on YouTube, they are on TikTok. So instead of begging the radio DJs to play our songs, we should focus on the digital playlists. Pitch to the independent curators, build your own playlists, get your fans to add your songs to their personal playlists. The algorithm on Spotify will eventually pick it up if the engagement is there. The radio is a legacy medium. We have to move on.

Wah, Auntie Rose, thank you so much for asking! That is so sweet. I would tell her, first of all, just go to the venues that are still surviving. That is great advice. It really comes down to community. The audience and the artists need to bridge that gap. We cannot just be consumers; we have to be participants. Darren, you have been in the industry for a long time, both as a producer and a venue owner. When you look back at the last ten years, what is the one thing that has changed for the better, and what is the one thing that has gotten worse?

DT VBZ JJ NN . PRP RB VBZ RP IN NN . DT NN CC DT NNS VBP TO VB DT NN . PRP MD RB RB VB NNS : PRP VBP TO VB NNS . NNP , PRP VBP VBN IN DT NN IN DT JJ NN , CC IN DT NN CC DT NN NN . WRB PRP VBP RB IN DT JJ CD NNS , WP VBZ DT CD NN WDT VBZ VBN IN DT JJR , CC WP VBZ DT CD NN WDT VBZ VBN JJR .

The one thing that has changed for the better is the quality of production and the diversity of genres. Ten years ago, if you were a local band, you basically had to play indie rock or pop punk if you wanted to get gigs. Now, I am producing local hip hop, local R&B, local electronic, local folk. The talent pool is incredibly deep, and the production quality is on par with international standards. The kids today have access to the same plugins and tutorials as the kids in Los Angeles. The barrier to entry for making good music is zero.

I totally agree with Darren. The production quality is insane now. I listen to some of the demos that young producers send me, and I am like, 'Wah, this sounds like a Grammy-winning album, and you made it in your HDB bedroom?' That is amazing. But the mental health aspect is very real. The constant comparison on social media, the pressure to always be relevant, it is very heavy. I think the industry needs to normalize taking a break. You do not have to release a single every two months. It is okay to touch grass, go to the park, live a normal life, and then write about it.

topic: Western influence vs Asian values in Singapore
data: Welcome back to The Red Dot Debate, everybody. I am your host Marcus, and today we are diving into a topic that constantly sparks fierce debates at our dinner tables, in our office pantries, and all over our local Reddit threads. We are talking about the tug-of-war between Western influence and traditional Asian values in Singapore. And to help me unpack this cultural minefield, I have two guests with very different perspectives. First, a local media producer and cultural commentator who needs no introduction, Chloe. Welcome back.

Haha, the TikTok comment section is definitely a warzone. And joining us today is someone who has been observing this battlefield from the outside, but is now living right in the middle of it. David, an American expat who moved here from Chicago four years ago to work in the tech sector. Welcome to the show, David.

Thanks Marcus, thanks Chloe. Happy to be here. And yeah, four years in, I think I am finally starting to understand the unspoken rules of this place. Though I still occasionally get the 'auntie stare' when I forget to return my tray at the hawker centre.

The auntie stare! A classic Singaporean rite of passage. David, let us start with you. When you first moved here, what was your biggest culture shock regarding the values of the society? Was it the strict laws, or something else?

Honestly, it was not the laws. I mean, coming from the US, you hear all these stories about Singapore being a 'fine' city, chewing gum bans, caning, all that. I thought that would be the hardest part to adjust to. But the real culture shock was the social contract. The sheer level of communal policing. In Chicago, if you do something weird on the train, people might ignore you, or maybe one crazy guy will yell at you. Here, the whole carriage will stare at you in silent judgment. It is this collective enforcement of social norms. It took me a while to realize that this is not just about 'Asian values' in some abstract philosophical sense; it is about survival in a high-density environment. You have to conform so that the machine keeps running smoothly.

That is very true, David. You hit the nail on the head. The 'machine' must keep running. Because we are a tiny red dot, we do not have the luxury of individualism running rampant. If everyone just does whatever they want, the whole system collapses. That is the fundamental Asian value of communitarianism over individualism. In the West, especially in America, the highest virtue is personal freedom. 'My right to do what I want.' But here, the highest virtue is social harmony. Even if it means suppressing your own ego a little bit.

But Chloe, is that narrative changing? Because if you look at the younger generation, the millennials and Gen Z, they are heavily influenced by Western media, Western internet culture. They are talking about 'mental health', 'setting boundaries', 'quiet quitting'. These are very Western, individualistic concepts. Do you see a clash between the older generation's survivalist mindset and the younger generation's desire for self-actualization?

Alamak, the clash is very real, Marcus. I see it in my own family. My parents came up in the 70s and 80s. They went through the 'strawberry generation' debates, they worked crazy hours to buy their HDB flat, to pay for my tuition. Their whole identity is built on this Asian value of filial piety and sacrifice. You work hard so the family can eat. But my younger cousins, they are in their twenties now. They watch all these American podcasts, they see the corporate burnout culture, and they are like, 'Why must I sacrifice my youth for a company that does not care about me? I want work-life balance.' The older generation looks at them and says, 'Wah, you guys so soft already. Last time we walk to school uphill both ways, now you want four-day work week?' It is a massive generational disconnect.

I see that in the office too. I manage a team of local Singaporean devs. A few years ago, if I asked the team to stay back on a weekend to fix a bug, they would just do it. No complaints. It was the 'kiasu' mentality, the fear of losing face, or just the ingrained respect for hierarchy. But recently, I had a junior dev tell me, 'David, I value my weekends for my mental health. I can look at it on Monday.' I was shocked at first. In the US, we have been fighting for that kind of boundary for decades. But in Singapore, that is considered almost rebellious. It is challenging the traditional Asian corporate hierarchy.

Let us dig deeper into that. The phrase 'importing Western ideas'. We hear this a lot in local discourse, especially on social media. Whenever there is a debate about social issues—whether it is about race, gender, or LGBTQ+ rights—someone will inevitably chime in and say, 'Eh, do not import Western ideas here, Singapore is different.' Chloe, what does that phrase actually mean to the average Singaporean? Is it a valid defense of our unique context, or is it just a shield to avoid uncomfortable conversations?

VB PRP VB RBR IN DT . DT NN `` VBG JJ NNS '' . PRP VBP DT DT NN IN JJ NN , RB IN JJ NNS . WRB EX VBZ DT NN IN JJ NNS : IN PRP VBZ IN NN , NN , CC NNPS NNS : NN MD RB VB RP CC VB , `` UH , VBP RB VB JJ NNS RB , NNP VBZ JJ . '' NNP , WP VBZ DT NN RB VB IN DT JJ NNP . VBZ PRP DT JJ NN IN PRP$ JJ NN , CC VBZ PRP RB DT NN TO VB JJ NNS .

It is a very loaded phrase, Marcus. And I think it is used in two very different ways. On one hand, there is a genuine, pragmatic argument. Singapore is not a Western country. We are in Southeast Asia. Our demographic makeup, our history, our geopolitical position—everything is different. The Western model of liberal democracy and identity politics was built on a completely different historical context, like centuries of colonialism, the civil rights movement, the Enlightenment. If you just copy-paste those social frameworks onto Singapore without understanding our local context, it can be very destructive. For example, the way race is handled in the US is based on a black-and-white binary, a history of slavery and systemic oppression. Here, we have the CMIO model—Chinese, Malay, Indian, Others. We have our own unique racial tensions, our own history of riots in the 60s. So when people say 'do not import Western ideas', they mean, 'Let us solve our own problems using our own local context.'

But Chloe, is it always used pragmatically? Because from my outsider perspective, and even reading the local forums, it sometimes feels like a dismissal. Like a way to shut down progress.
CC NNP , VBZ PRP RB VBN RB . IN IN PRP$ NN NN , CC RB VBG DT JJ NNS , PRP RB VBZ IN DT NN . IN DT NN TO VB RP NN .

You are right, David. That is the dark side of the phrase. Sometimes, it is used to gaslight people, to invalidate their lived experiences. For example, when ethnic minorities in Singapore speak up about microaggressions or systemic biases, the immediate pushback from the majority is often, 'You are just importing Western woke culture. You are trying to create division. In Singapore, we are all equal, just don't make a fuss.' It is used to protect the status quo. It is a way of saying, 'Your discomfort is a Western import; our harmony is more important.' And that is where it becomes very jialat. Because it stops us from having honest conversations about our own local prejudices, like Chinese privilege, which is a very real thing here, even if people do not want to admit it.

That is a very crucial distinction. The difference between protecting a unique cultural context and using 'Asian values' as a blanket excuse to avoid accountability. David, how does an American, coming from a society that is constantly tearing itself apart over identity politics, view Singapore's approach to race and social cohesion?
DT VBZ DT RB JJ NN . DT NN IN VBG DT JJ JJ NN CC VBG `` JJ NNS '' IN DT NN NN TO VB NN . NNP , WRB VBZ DT NNP , VBG IN DT NN WDT VBZ RB VBG PRP RB IN NN NNS , VBP NNP POS NN IN NN CC JJ NN .

It is fascinating, and honestly, a bit of a paradox. In the US, we have absolute freedom of speech, but the social fabric is incredibly polarized. We are constantly fighting in the culture wars. Here in Singapore, the government takes a very paternalistic approach. They manage race relations very strictly. You have the Ethnic Integration Policy in HDBs to prevent ghettos. You have strict laws against hate speech. And on the surface, it works. I can walk through Little India, then Chinatown, then Kampong Glam, and it feels safe, it feels integrated. But the trade-off is that you cannot have the messy, loud, organic conversations about race that happen in the West. You are not allowed to rock the boat. So when a Singaporean says, 'We cannot import Western identity politics,' what they are really saying is, 'We cannot afford the luxury of your culture wars. If we fight like that, we might literally break.' And coming from a place that is currently breaking, I can understand that fear.

Exactly, David. That existential fear is baked into our DNA. Our founding fathers, Lee Kuan Yew and his team, they saw the racial riots of the 60s. They saw Malaysia separate from Singapore partly due to racial tensions. Their number one priority was survival. So they engineered this multiracial harmony from the top down. It is very pragmatic. It is very Asian. The collective survival of the group is more important than the individual's right to express their identity. But the problem is, the younger generation, they did not live through the riots. They only see the restrictions. They see the rules, and they feel suffocated. They look at the West, where people are marching in the streets, expressing themselves, and they think, 'Wah, so free. Why we cannot be like that?' They do not see the chaos and the division behind the freedom.

Let us talk about that concept of 'freedom' and how it applies to the workplace and meritocracy. Because the 'Singapore Dream' is very heavily tied to Asian values of hard work, education, and providing for the family. The 5Cs. But Western influence is bringing in ideas like 'follow your passion', 'the hustle culture is toxic', 'you are more than your job'. Are we seeing a shift in how Singaporeans define success?

VB PRP VB IN DT NN IN `` NN '' CC WRB PRP VBZ IN DT NN CC NN . IN DT `` NNP NNP '' VBZ RB RB VBN IN JJ NNS IN JJ NN , NN , CC VBG IN DT NN . DT NNP . CC JJ NN VBZ VBG RP NNS IN `` VB PRP$ NN '' , `` DT NNP NN VBZ JJ '' , `` PRP VBP JJR IN PRP$ NN '' . VBP PRP VBG DT NN IN WRB NNPS VBP NN .

I think the definition of success is fracturing. When I first arrived, everyone I met was obsessed with the KPIs of life. Good grades, good university, good MNC job, BTO flat, marriage, kids. It is a very linear, very Confucian path. Respect your elders, study hard, secure the rice bowl. But lately, I am meeting young Singaporeans who are opting out. They are starting niche cafes, they are doing freelance art, they are taking gap years. They are rejecting the traditional Asian definition of success because they realize it makes them miserable. They are adopting this Western ideal of individual self-actualization. But the safety net here is not like the West. If you fail in the US, you can just declare bankruptcy and start over. Here, failure carries a lot of stigma. The 'face' factor is still very strong.

The 'face' factor, or 'mianzi', is a huge anchor holding us back from fully embracing that Western individualism. In the West, if you start a business and it fails, people say, 'Oh, you tried, you learned, good for you.' Here, if you fail, your aunties at the Chinese New Year dinner will ask, 'Eh, your business still open or not? Why you never get promotion?' The social pressure is immense. But I do think the pandemic changed things a lot. A lot of people realized that working 60 hours a week just to pay for a bigger flat that you never spend time in... it is very sian. It is meaningless. So they are starting to redefine success. Success is not just the 5Cs anymore. Success is having the time to go for a run, to eat a good meal with your friends, to not be kan cheong all the time. That is a very Western, post-materialist value creeping in.

Very traditional Asian expectations. But I have been dating an American girl for two years. I feel like I am torn between two worlds. I love my culture, but I also love her. Why must tradition always mean suffering?'

Wah, that one is very heavy. Very classic Asian family drama. The conflict between filial piety and personal happiness. In the West, you just marry who you want, and if your parents disapprove, you just cut them off or set a boundary. But in Asia, the family is an extension of yourself. You are not just marrying the person; you are marrying into their family, and your family is marrying into theirs. The idea of 'disowning' is a massive threat because it means you are severing your roots. But I think the younger generation is starting to push back. They are saying, 'I will respect you, but I will live my own life.' It is a painful transition, but it is happening.

Oh, the parenting debate! The ultimate battleground for cultural values. Chloe, is Singaporean parenting becoming too Westernized, or is the 'kiasu' Asian tiger mom still alive and well?

Haha, the tiger mom is still very much alive, Marcus, but she is evolving. The old school Asian parenting was very authoritarian. 'Because I say so, that is why.' Very high on discipline, very low on emotional validation. But now, these young parents, they have read all the Western psychology books. They know about 'gentle parenting', 'validating feelings', 'giving children agency'. So you get this weird hybrid. They want their kid to be emotionally healthy and expressive, like a Western kid, but they still want them to score straight A's and get into Raffles, like a traditional Asian kid. They want the best of both worlds. But the kids are very smart. They know how to use the Western language of 'mental health' and 'boundaries' to get out of doing their Kumon homework. It is a very funny dynamic.

I love that observation. The kids are code-switching not just linguistically, but culturally. They use Singlish and Asian deference when talking to their grandparents, but they use Western therapy-speak when negotiating with their parents. 'Mom, I cannot study right now, my cognitive load is too high and I need to protect my mental bandwidth.' And the mom is just like, 'What is cognitive load? Go and eat your bird's nest!'
