Haha, that is brilliant. Let us talk about another area where this clash is very visible: the concept of 'Cancel Culture' and public shaming. In the West, cancel culture is a massive phenomenon, driven by social media, holding powerful people accountable. In Singapore, we have our own version of public shaming, often through platforms like HardwareZone, Reddit, or TikTok. But the motivation seems different. In the West, it is often framed as social justice. In Singapore, it often feels like it is about enforcing social norms and 'kiasi' rule-following. Do you guys agree?

Absolutely. In the US, cancel culture is deeply tied to identity politics and moral righteousness. It is about punishing people for violating progressive social norms. Here in Singapore, the 'cancel culture' is more about punishing people for being a 'scum' or a 'piah kiam' (nuisance) who disrupts the social order. If someone cuts the queue at the MRT, or if an auntie chope five tables with one packet of tissue and then yells at the cleaner, the internet will hunt them down. It is not about social justice; it is about communal enforcement of the 'social contract'. We are all watching each other. It is a digital extension of the auntie stare I mentioned earlier.

Yes, but David, there is also a darker side to it. Sometimes, the local internet mob uses the language of Western social justice to attack people, but the underlying motivation is just mob mentality and bullying. For example, when there is a dispute between a delivery rider and a customer, the internet will immediately jump to conclusions, label the customer as an 'atas' (snobbish) entitled person, and doxx them. They use the Western language of 'class warfare' and 'privilege', but it is just raw, unfiltered anger. It is the Asian concept of 'losing face' weaponized on a digital scale. Once you are shamed online, your face is gone, and it is very hard to recover.

That is a very insightful point. The weaponization of 'face' in the digital age. Let us touch upon the geopolitical and macro level. Singapore sits right at the crossroads of the US and China. We have Western political and economic systems, but we are geographically and demographically Asian. How does this dual identity affect our national psyche? Are we more Western or more Asian when push comes to shove?

From a geopolitical standpoint, Singapore is incredibly pragmatic. We do not have the luxury of ideological purity. We trade with everyone. We adopt Western legal and financial systems because they work and they attract global capital. But socially and culturally, the government constantly reinforces Asian values to maintain stability. It is a very calculated balancing act. You can see it in the education system. We teach math and science using Western pedagogies, but we have National Education and moral education that emphasizes Asian filial piety, racial harmony, and respect for authority. It is a hybrid model. But I think when it comes to the core survival instincts of the state, the Asian communitarianism always wins out over Western individualism.

I agree with David. At the macro level, the state is very Asian in its paternalism. It knows best. It will plan your housing, your retirement via CPF, your healthcare via Medisave. It is a very Confucian social contract. The government is the strict but benevolent father. But at the micro level, the individual citizen is becoming more Westernized in their desires. They want to consume Western media, they want to travel, they want to express themselves. The tension is that the state is designed for a collective, but the citizens are increasingly thinking as individuals. That is the biggest challenge for Singapore's future. How do you govern a population that is starting to think like Western individualists, using a system designed for Asian communitarians?

That is the million-dollar question. Let us look at one more comment from our audience before we wrap up. This is from 'Uncle_Tan_VoidDeck'. He says: 'I am 65 years old. I see the young people now, they speak English very well, very confident. But they do not know how to greet their elders properly, they do not know our traditions, they only know how to look at their handphones. We are losing our Asian roots. If we become just like the West, we lose our soul. We become just a little America, but without the freedom. What is the point?'

Wow. Uncle Tan really captured the anxiety of the older generation. The fear that in the pursuit of modernization and Western efficiency, we are losing our cultural soul. That we are becoming a sterile, rootless society.

It is a valid fear, Marcus. But I also think Uncle Tan is looking at it a bit too pessimistically. Culture is not static. It is not a museum piece that you just preserve under glass. It evolves. The young people might not know how to perform the traditional nine-bow worship, but they are creating their own culture. They are mixing Hokkien with English, they are creating local art, they are redefining what it means to be a good Singaporean. We are not becoming a 'little America'. We are becoming something entirely new. A global Asian city. We take the best of the West—innovation, rule of law, meritocracy—and we filter it through our Asian values—community, family, pragmatism.

A global Asian city. A hybrid identity. I think that is a beautiful note to end on. We are not just a collision of East and West; we are the alchemy that happens when they mix. David, Chloe, thank you so much for this incredibly rich, nuanced, and honest conversation. It is not easy to dissect your own cultural DNA while you are still living inside it.

Thanks for having me, Marcus. It is always fascinating to step back and analyze the machine I am living in. But the ship still needs to sail forward.

topic: Eco-friendly projects and recycling in Singapore
data: Welcome back to The Green Red Dot, everybody. I am your host Marcus, and today we are tackling a topic that is literally close to home, and quite literally, close to our shores. We are talking about eco-friendly projects, the reality of recycling in Singapore, and whether we are actually saving our little red dot, or just greenwashing our way into a landfill. And to help me unpack this, I have two heavyweights in the local ecology and sustainability scene. First, a marine ecologist who spends more time underwater than on dry land, please welcome Dr. Chloe Lim.

Hello everyone. Hi Marcus. Wah, you make it sound very dramatic, like I am Aquaman or something. Actually, I just spend a lot of time scraping algae off coral reefs and measuring water quality around our coastal areas. But yes, happy to be here.

Haha, I think scraping algae is very glamorous in the science world. And next to her, an urban ecologist and waste systems researcher who basically knows exactly where your trash goes after you throw it down the HDB rubbish chute. Darren Tan. Welcome to the show.

Thanks Marcus. Happy to be here. And yeah, I wish I could tell people their trash goes to a magical fairy land, but the reality is a bit more... fiery. And smelly. So I understand if people don't want to think about it too much.

Exactly. And that is why we need to talk about it. Because the clock is ticking. Semakau Landfill is projected to run out of space by 2035. That is less than ten years away. Chloe, from an ecological perspective, why should the average Singaporean care about a landfill that is basically an island far away from our main shores?

RB . CC DT VBZ WRB PRP VBP TO VB IN PRP . IN DT NN VBZ VBG . NNP NNP VBZ VBN TO VB IN IN NN IN CD . DT VBZ JJR IN CD NNS RB . NNP , IN DT JJ NN , WRB MD DT JJ JJ NN IN DT NN WDT VBZ RB DT NN RB RB IN PRP$ JJ NNS .

That is a very good question, Marcus. A lot of people think, 'Wah, Semakau is so far, I cannot see it, so out of sight, out of mind.' But the thing is, Singapore is a tiny, low-lying island. Everything is connected. The leachate, the liquids that seep out from the waste, if not managed properly, can affect the surrounding marine environment. And Semakau is not just a trash dump; it is surrounded by coral reefs, seagrass meadows, and mangrove ecosystems. We have done surveys there and found biodiversity that is actually thriving, surprisingly. But the margin for error is very small. If the landfill membrane fails, or if the waste management system upstream fails and we start dumping more than we can handle, the marine ecosystem will take the hit. And if the marine ecosystem dies, our coastal protection is compromised. You know we are facing rising sea levels and coastal erosion. The mangroves and corals are our natural buffers. So caring about Semakau is actually caring about whether your HDB flat in the East Coast gets flooded in twenty years.

Very well said, Chloe. And to add to that, the 'out of sight, out of mind' mentality is exactly why our waste generation is so high. Singaporeans are very clean, very kiasu about our immediate surroundings. You walk into an HDB corridor, it is spotless. The void deck, very clean. But that cleanliness comes at a cost. We are just shifting the dirt from our living room to the incineration plant, and then to the landfill. We are consuming the land, consuming the resources, and we think because we don't see the rubbish, it just disappears. But as an urban ecologist, I always tell my students, there is no 'away'. When you throw something 'away', you are just sending it to another part of your own house.

'There is no away.' I like that. Very profound. But let us talk about the mechanics of this 'away'. The blue recycling bin. The single-stream recycling system. Darren, be honest. When I throw a dirty pizza box, or a half-empty bottle of bubble tea into the blue bin, what actually happens?

`` EX VBZ DT RB . '' PRP VBP DT . RB JJ . CC VB PRP VB IN DT NNS IN DT `` RB '' . DT JJ NN NN . DT JJ HYPH NN NN NN . NNP , VB JJ . WRB PRP VBP DT JJ NN NN , CC DT JJ HYPH JJ NN IN NN NN IN DT JJ NNP , WP RB VBZ .

Alamak, Marcus, you are hitting the pain point already. Okay, so in Singapore, we use a single-stream system for HDBs. You just throw everything recyclable into the blue bin. Paper, plastic, cans, glass. The logic is, if we make it very convenient, people will recycle more. And it works, the participation rate is quite high. But the problem is contamination. If you throw a half-empty bubble tea into the blue bin, the sugary liquid leaks onto the paper cards, the newspapers, the cardboard boxes. Once paper is contaminated with food or liquid, it cannot be recycled. It is considered trash. And because it is single-stream, the whole batch in that blue bin might be rejected by the recycling collector. They will just send the whole thing to the incineration plant. So your good intention, you washed your milk carton, you separated your cans, all go to waste because somebody decided the blue bin is a convenient trash can for their dirty tissue paper.

And this is where the education gap is huge, right? I see a lot of campaigns, 'Please wash your recyclables'. But let us be real about the Singaporean context. We live in HDBs, many of us are working long hours. You come home tired, you cook dinner, you have a greasy wok, a plastic container with curry stains. Are you going to use your precious drinking water and dish soap to scrub that plastic container until it is sparkling clean just for recycling? A lot of people say, 'Cannot tahan, just throw into the green bin already.' And I don't blame them. The infrastructure needs to support the behavior. If the system is too rigid, people will just give up.

PRP VBP RB JJ , PRP VBP NN , PRP VBP DT NN NN , DT JJ NN IN NN NNS . VBP PRP VBG TO VB PRP$ JJ NN NN CC JJ NN TO VB DT JJ NN IN PRP VBZ VBG JJ RB IN NN . That is a very valid point, Chloe. In countries like Germany or Japan, the recycling rules are super strict. You have to wash everything, separate by color, remove the labels. But their water infrastructure and public mindset are different. Here, the NEA and the waste collectors know that if they enforce a super strict washing rule, the contamination rate in the blue bins would drop, but the participation rate would also drop to zero. People will just say, 'Sian, so troublesome, I throw normal trash already.' So we have this middle ground. We rely on the Materials Recovery Facilities, the MRFs, to sort and clean. But manpower is very expensive in Singapore. We don't have armies of people to sit and wash dirty plastics. So the economics just don't work. That is why the golden rule in Singapore is: If it is dirty and you cannot easily wipe it clean, just throw it in the regular trash. Do not contaminate the blue bin. Better to burn it for energy than to ruin a whole batch of good cardboard.

Wow. So sometimes, throwing it in the regular trash is actually the more eco-friendly choice? That is very counter-intuitive.
UH . RB RB , VBG PRP IN DT JJ NN VBZ RB DT JJR NN HYPH JJ NN . DT VBZ RB JJ JJ JJ .

It is counter-intuitive, but it is the reality of our waste management economics. The incineration plants in Singapore are very advanced. They burn the trash, and the heat generates electricity for our grid. Then the ash is sent to Semakau. So if you put a dirty plastic container in the blue bin, it goes to the MRF, gets rejected, gets sent to the incinerator anyway, but you also wasted the fuel of the recycling truck coming to collect it, and you ruined the paper around it. It is a net loss for the environment. So we always say, 'Recycle right'. Only put clean, dry recyclables in the blue bin.

But Marcus, this brings us to the whole 'reusable' culture. The tote bags, the KeepCups. We see this a lot. Supermarkets charge you for plastic bags, so everyone buys a canvas tote bag. Very good intention, right? But from an ecological footprint perspective, is it actually helping?
CC NNP , DT VBZ PRP IN DT JJ `` JJ '' NN . DT NN NNS , DT NNPS . PRP VBP DT DT NN . NNS VBP PRP IN NN NNS , IN NN VBZ DT NN NN NN . RB JJ NN , JJ . CC IN DT JJ NN NN , VBZ PRP RB VBG .

Oh, the tote bag phenomenon. I have so many stories about this. I have friends who have a collection of over twenty tote bags at home. They get them from conferences, from supermarket promotions, from bank events. 'Oh, this one got a cute design, I keep.' But the reality is, a cotton tote bag requires a massive amount of water and pesticides to grow the cotton, and the weaving process is very energy-intensive. Studies show that you need to use a cotton tote bag thousands of times to offset the carbon footprint of its production compared to a single-use plastic bag. If you have twenty tote bags at home and you only use each of them once a month, you are actually doing more harm to the environment than if you just reused your plastic bags as bin liners. It is the ultimate greenwashing of the consumer mind. We feel good because we bought a 'green' product, but we are just consuming more.

That is a very sharp observation. Shopping our way to saving the planet. Let us talk about another massive trend: Corporate eco-friendly projects. We see these press releases all the time. Big multinational companies partnering with local NGOs for sustainability drives. But recently, there was a huge scandal involving a global sports brand, a local sports NGO, and a shoe recycling initiative. Darren, you wrote a very detailed breakdown of this on your blog. What actually happened there?

Wah, that one was a classic case of PR spin meeting ground reality. Okay, so the narrative was very beautiful. A big petrochemical and sports brand partners with a local sports NGO. They say, 'Bring your old shoes to our bins across Singapore. We will recycle them into running tracks and playgrounds for the community.' Very noble, right? They even got government endorsements, ministers praising the initiative. Singaporeans, being very kiasu and wanting to do our part, we all dug out our old shoes. We cleaned them, we donated them. The bins were overflowing. But then, an investigative journalism team decided to put Bluetooth trackers inside some of the donated shoes to see where they actually go.

Oh no. Let me guess. They didn't end up on a running track.

Not even close. The trackers showed that the shoes were just being collected, mixed with general waste, and sent to the incineration plant. Or in some cases, they were just shipped overseas to be dumped. Why? Because the local waste management infrastructure simply cannot handle the complex material composition of a modern sports shoe. A shoe is not just one material. It has rubber, EVA foam, synthetic leather, mesh, metal eyelets, strong chemical adhesives. To recycle it properly, you need specialized machinery to shred it and separate the different polymers. We don't have that specialized plant in Singapore. The local recycling facilities are mostly geared towards paper, cardboard, and simple plastics. So the NGO and the brand knew this, but they pushed the PR campaign anyway. They collected the shoes, fulfilled their corporate social responsibility KPIs, took the photos, and then just disposed of the shoes the normal way. It was a massive greenwashing exercise.

That one really made me so angry, Marcus. Because it exploits the goodwill of the public. Singaporeans want to help. We see a bin, we see a good cause, we throw our things in. But when you find out that your effort was just used for a corporate photo-op, it kills the passion. It makes people cynical. 'Okay, next time you ask me to donate, I also suspect you just throw into the green bin.' And the worst part is, the brand got all the positive publicity, the NGO got their funding, but the environment got zero benefit. In fact, probably negative benefit because of the carbon emissions from collecting and transporting all those shoes just to burn them.

And this is the danger of diffusion of responsibility. The brand says, 'We partnered with the NGO.' The NGO says, 'We relied on the waste contractors.' The waste contractors say, 'We just follow the manifest.' Nobody takes ownership of the actual environmental impact. They just look at the top-line numbers. 'We collected ten thousand pairs of shoes! Success!' But if you look at the lifecycle assessment, it is a disaster. This is why we need stricter regulations on green claims. We cannot just let companies self-declare that they are recycling things when the local infrastructure physically cannot process them.

That is incredibly frustrating. So as consumers, how do we not get tricked? How do we know if a project is real or just greenwashing?

I think the first step is to ask the 'where' and 'how' questions. If a campaign says 'we recycle', ask them, 'Where is the recycling facility? What is the end product?' If they give you vague answers like 'it will be repurposed into new materials' without naming the actual plant or the process, it is a red flag. Also, look at the material. If it is a complex, multi-layered material like a shoe, or a tetra pak, or a soiled food container, and they claim it is easily recyclable in Singapore, be skeptical. We have to be smart consumers. We cannot just blindly trust the green leaf logo on the packaging.

Oh, the space constraint in HDBs is a very real issue. Not everyone has a condo where you can have a nice, hidden recycling corner. In a small BTO kitchen, a box of dirty milk cartons will definitely attract flies and smell. My advice to this auntie is, prioritize the high-value, low-smell recyclables. Cardboard, paper, cans. Rinse them quickly, let them dry, and flatten them. They don't smell. For the smelly stuff, like food containers, if you cannot wash them immediately, just throw them in the regular trash. Do not let it rot in your kitchen. Your mental health and your marital harmony are also important! Sustainability should not cause you to fight with your husband. Start small, do what is convenient, and build the habit. If the blue bin downstairs is full, just use the next one. The collectors will eventually clear it.

Haha, the Singapore weather factor. You cannot escape it. We are on the equator, it is thirty degrees with ninety percent humidity all year round. Cycling is definitely good for reducing transport emissions, no car exhaust, no petrol. But the 'rebound effect' is real. Because you are hot and sweaty, you consume more resources to cool down and clean up. But I think the net impact is still positive. A car emits kilograms of CO2 per kilometer. Your shower and paper towels, while wasteful, have a much lower carbon footprint than driving a Toyota Camry. The solution is not to stop cycling; the solution is to adapt the infrastructure. More shower facilities in offices, more air-lockers, maybe offices can relax the dress code so you don't need to wear a full suit and tie to cycle to work. If the corporate culture supports it, the eco-friendly behavior becomes much easier to sustain.

Let us talk about food waste. Because from what I understand, food waste makes up a huge percentage of the total waste generated in Singapore. And it is very heavy, very wet, which makes it terrible for incineration. Darren, what is the current situation with food waste management?
VB PRP VB IN NN NN . IN IN WP PRP VBP , NN NN VBZ RP DT JJ NN IN DT JJ NN VBN IN NNP . CC PRP VBZ RB JJ , RB JJ , WDT VBZ PRP JJ IN NN . NNP , WP VBZ DT JJ NN IN NN NN NN .

Food waste is the elephant in the room. It accounts for about half of the total waste generated by households in Singapore. And it is the most problematic because it is wet. When you burn wet food waste in the incinerator, it lowers the combustion temperature, which means the plant has to use more energy to keep the fire going. It is very inefficient. And it rots very fast. If you leave food waste in the HDB chute, it smells, it attracts pests, it creates leachate that corrodes the pipes. The government has been pushing hard on this. They introduced the mandatory food waste segregation for large commercial generators, like malls, hotels, and big factories. They have to install on-site food waste treatments, like digesters that turn the food into water or compost. But for households, it is still voluntary. We have the blue food waste bins in some HDB estates, and the food waste chutes in newer BTOs. But the adoption rate is quite low. People are still used to just wrapping their food in newspaper and throwing it down the rubbish chute.

And I think the cultural aspect of food in Singapore makes this very hard. We are a nation of foodies. We love to eat, we love to buy in bulk, we love to 'chope' food. The wet markets, the supermarkets, the hawker centres. The amount of unsold food that gets thrown away every day is mind-boggling. There are some grassroots initiatives, like food rescue networks, where volunteers collect unserved food from events or supermarkets and distribute it to the needy. That is very shiok, very good. But it is very manual, very labor-intensive. We need a more systemic approach. Maybe we need to look at how other countries do it, like Japan or South Korea, where they use mandatory pay-as-you-throw systems for food waste. If you have to pay for every kilogram of food waste you throw, suddenly you will think twice about letting the rice go bad in the fridge.

The pay-as-you-throw system is very effective, but it is also very hard to implement in high-density HDB living. How do you track which household threw which bag of food waste? In landed properties, maybe easier. But in an HDB with hundreds of families sharing a central chute, it is a logistical nightmare. People will just illegally dump their food waste into the normal trash bins to avoid the fee. So the NEA is taking a more gradual approach. First, educate, provide the infrastructure, and see if the behavior changes. The tray return scheme at hawker centres is a good example. At first, people were very resistant. 'Eh, I pay for the food, why I need to clean my own table?' But now, with the threat of fines, and the enforcement by ambassadors, people are slowly getting used to it. It is about changing the social norm. Once it becomes the normal thing to do, the kiasu factor will kick in. Nobody wants to be the only one who doesn't return their tray and get scolded by the auntie.

The kiasu factor used for good. I like that. Chloe, let us bring it back to the marine environment. We talk a lot about what happens on land, but what about the oceans? Singapore is a major port, we have a lot of maritime activity. How is the marine ecology faring with all this development and waste?

DT NN NN VBN IN JJ . PRP VBP DT . NNP , VBD PRP VB PRP RB IN DT NNP NN . PRP VBP DT NN IN WP VBZ IN NN , CC WP IN DT NNS . NNP VBZ DT JJ NN , PRP VBP DT NN IN JJ NN . WRB VBZ DT JJ NN VBG IN PDT DT NN CC NN .

It is a constant battle, Marcus. Singapore is one of the busiest ports in the world. The sea traffic is insane. And with that comes the risk of oil spills, chemical spills, and marine debris. We have seen incidents where ships collide or leak, and the oil washes up on our beaches. It is devastating for the intertidal zones, the mangroves, the corals. But the more insidious problem is the microplastics and the nutrient pollution. When the incinerator ash from Semakau leaches, or when the storm drains flush out the dirty water from our streets after a heavy rain, it all ends up in the sea. The nutrients from the fertilizers used in our parks, the soap from our laundry, it causes algal blooms. The algae blocks the sunlight from reaching the seagrass and the corals, and they die. And then there are the microplastics. Every time it rains, the plastic waste from our streets gets washed into the canals, into the sea. The fish eat the microplastics, the birds eat the fish, and it goes up the food chain. We are literally eating our own trash.

And this is why the 'clean our streets' campaign is not just about aesthetics. It is about marine conservation. If you litter a plastic bottle on the side of the road, it doesn't just stay there. The next heavy thunderstorm, typical Singapore weather, flash flood, and that bottle is in the ocean. So the connection between the HDB drain and the coral reef is very direct. People need to understand that.

Exactly. And it is not all doom and gloom. There is a lot of good work happening. The coral reef restoration projects, like the one by the Nature Society and various volunteer groups. They are growing coral fragments in nurseries and transplanting them onto degraded reefs. And it is working. The biodiversity is coming back. We have box crabs, seahorses, nudibranchs, all thriving just a few kilometers from the busiest port in the world. It shows that nature is very resilient if you just give it a chance and reduce the stressors. But we cannot keep adding stressors. We have to stop the pollution at the source.

But I feel like a lot of the jobs are just in compliance, making sure companies follow the government rules, or in PR, writing the sustainability reports. Is there any real, ground-up ecology work happening in Singapore, or is it all just desk jobs and greenwashing?'

Oh, this is a very important question for the younger generation. I understand the frustration. A lot of the corporate sustainability roles are indeed just tick-box exercises. But there is a growing demand for real, technical expertise. We need people who understand the science of waste management, who can design better recycling technologies, who can audit the supply chains to find the real carbon hotspots. And on the ecology side, there are consultancies doing environmental impact assessments for construction projects, making sure we don't destroy the green corridors. It is not just PR. But you have to be careful who you work for. Look for companies that are actually investing in R&D, in new technologies, not just buying carbon credits to offset their emissions. The future of sustainability in Singapore is in innovation. We are land-scarce, we are resource-scarce. We have to be the laboratory for circular economy solutions. If you are passionate, there is work to be done. It is just not always the glamorous, Instagram-friendly work.

I agree with Darren. And I would add, if you want to do ground-up ecology work, join the NGOs, join the volunteer groups. Go to the beaches, do the reef surveys, monitor the otters, track the hornbills. The data collected by these citizen scientists is invaluable. The government agencies like NParks and NEA cannot be everywhere. They rely on the eyes and ears of the ground. So if you want to get your hands dirty, literally, the opportunities are there. You just have to look beyond the corporate offices.

topic: Multi-ethnic weddings: Chinese, Malay, Indian customs
data: Welcome back to The Void Deck Sessions, everybody. I am your host Marcus, and today we are tackling a topic that is basically the ultimate test of diplomacy, logistics, and stomach capacity in Singapore. We are talking about multi-ethnic weddings. The beautiful, chaotic, and very expensive reality of blending Chinese, Malay, and Indian customs under one roof. And to help me navigate this cultural minefield, I have two guests who have been in the trenches. First, we have Priya, a wedding photographer who has shot literally hundreds of weddings across all the different communities in Singapore. Priya, welcome.

Hello Marcus! Hi everyone. Wah, thank you for having me. And yes, I have seen it all. From the 5 AM Chinese gatecrashing to the midnight Indian fire ceremonies. My camera battery usually die before the wedding even end, so I know the struggle is real.

Haha, I can imagine. And next to Priya, we have Wei Jie. Wei Jie is a recent groom who just survived a Chinese-Malay multi-ethnic wedding last year. Wei Jie, you look well-rested, but I know the trauma is still there. Welcome to the show.

Thanks Marcus. Hello Priya. And yes, I look well-rested because it has been a year already. If you ask me to do the gatecrashing again today, I think I will faint. But honestly, it was worth it. Just... very jialat to plan.

Let us start right there. The planning. Wei Jie, you are Chinese, your wife is Malay. When you first got engaged, how did the conversation even start about the wedding customs? Because you know, both sides have very strict, very deep-rooted traditions.
VB PRP VB RB RB . DT NN . NNP NNP , PRP VBP JJ , PRP$ NN VBZ NNP . WRB PRP RB VBD VBN , WRB VBD DT NN RB VBP IN DT NN NNS . IN PRP VBP , DT NNS VBP RB JJ , RB RB HYPH VBN NNS .

Alamak, where to start. So, my parents are very traditional Chinese. They want the full package: the guo da li, the tea ceremony, the big banquet at a nice hotel, the yam seng. But my wife's parents are Malay Muslim. So immediately, we know the banquet cannot have any pork, no alcohol, and it must be Halal-certified. And then there is the solemnization, the akad nikah, which is a religious requirement. So when we first sat down to talk, my dad was like, 'Eh, so we doing the Chinese dinner or not?' And my father-in-law was like, 'Cannot have alcohol in the hall, the religious authority will come and check.' So the first meeting was basically just a negotiation of what is allowed and what is not allowed. Very sian.

I hear this all the time from my couples, Wei Jie. The food is always the first battlefield. Because in a Chinese wedding, the yam seng with the Martell or the beer is like the climax of the night. But you bring that into a Malay Muslim context, it is a huge no-no. So how did you compromise?

We compromised by doing two separate events. Which, by the way, doubles the budget. First, we did the Malay side. We had the akad nikah at the mosque in the morning. Very solemn, very beautiful. My wife's father recited the ijab kabul, I had to say the Arabic vows. Walao, I practiced for three months. If I stutter one word, the marriage is not valid, you know? The pressure is very high. After that, we had the Malay reception in the evening. The bersanding, where we sit on the pelamin like a king and queen. We just sit there, smile, and wave to the guests. The food was nasi minyak, rendang, very shiok. But my Chinese parents, they were at the Malay reception, and they were like, 'Eh, why no beer? Why no yam seng?' I was like, 'Dad, please, just eat the rendang and be quiet.'

Haha, the classic Singaporean uncle complaint. No beer means the wedding is not complete. But Priya, from a photographer's perspective, how do you capture these two very different vibes in one weekend?
NNP , DT JJ JJ NN NN . DT NN VBZ DT NN VBZ RB JJ . CC NNP , IN DT NN POS NN , WRB VBP PRP VB DT CD RB JJ NNS IN CD NN .

It is actually my favorite thing to shoot, Marcus. Because the contrast is so beautiful. The Chinese side is very loud, very chaotic, very red and gold. The gatecrashing is just pure madness. The bridesmaids will make the groomsmen do the most embarrassing things. I shot a wedding last year where the bridesmaids made the groomsmen eat a bitter gourd and drink a mixture of vinegar and wasabi. The faces they make, the camera capture everything. It is very raw, very emotional, very funny. But then you switch to the Malay side, or the Indian side, and the vibe is completely different. It is more elegant, more graceful, very focused on the solemnity and the family blessings. The colors are so vibrant, the baju kurung and the baju melayu, the songket fabrics. The lighting in the mosque or the pelamin setup is usually very soft and golden. So as a photographer, I get to shoot two completely different genres in one day. It is very shiok, but my memory card always full.

Let us talk about the Chinese customs for a bit, since Wei Jie just mentioned the gatecrashing. The men chu. For the uninitiated, this is where the groom and his boys go to the bride's house, and the bridesmaids block the door and make them play games before they can see the bride. Wei Jie, what did your wife's bridesmaids make you do?

VB PRP VB IN DT JJ NNS IN DT NN , IN NNP NNP RB VBD DT NN . DT NNS VBP . IN DT JJ , DT VBZ WRB DT NN CC PRP$ NNS VBP IN DT NN POS NN , CC DT NNS VBP DT NN CC VB PRP VB NNS IN PRP MD VB DT NN . NNP NNP , WP VBD PRP$ NN POS NNS VBP PRP VB .

You know, my wife is very competitive. Her bridesmaids are all her secondary school and poly friends, very kiasu. They prepared a whole list of games. First, they made me and my groomsmen wear these very tight, very pink tutu skirts over our suits. Then they made us do a choreographed dance to a Blackpink song in the HDB corridor. The neighbors were all watching, some aunty even recorded us and put it on TikTok. I was so embarrassed. But the worst part was the 'finding the shoe' game. They hid the bride's red shoe somewhere in the 4-room flat. I was crawling under the bed, I was looking inside the rice cooker, I was looking inside the fridge. Finally, my best man found it inside the pillowcase. When I finally opened the door and saw my wife in her qipao, I was sweating so much my makeup was melting. But honestly, seeing her face, all the tiredness just disappear. Very touching.

That is the beauty of the Chinese tea ceremony and the gatecrashing, though. It is supposed to be a test of the groom's sincerity and patience. And the tea ceremony, gong fu cha, that is the most emotional part for me to shoot. When the couple kneels and serves tea to the parents, and the parents give them the ang bao and the gold jewelry, you can see the tears in the parents' eyes. It is the passing of the torch. The mother will usually cry, the father will try to be stoic but his voice will shake. Even the most tough, gangster-looking uncles will cry during the tea ceremony. It is very uniquely Asian, that expression of love without actually saying 'I love you'. They just give you a red packet and a gold necklace, and that means everything.

Very true. The ang bao and the gold. Let us shift gears to the Indian customs. Priya, you have shot so many Indian weddings. For a lot of Singaporeans who are not Indian, the Indian wedding seems like a massive, multi-day festival. Can you break down the key customs for us?
RB JJ . DT NN NNP CC DT NN . VB PRP VB NNS IN DT JJ NNS . NNP , PRP VBP VBN RB JJ JJ NNS . IN DT NN IN NNPS WP VBP RB JJ , DT JJ NN VBZ IN DT JJ , JJ JJ NN NN . MD PRP VB RP DT JJ NNS IN PRP .

Oh, Indian weddings are not just weddings, Marcus. They are a full-blown production. A Bollywood movie. If a Chinese wedding is a 12-hour marathon, an Indian wedding is a three-day ultra-marathon. But let us focus on the main day. The morning usually starts with the mehendi, which is the henna application, but that is mostly for the women. The main event is the baraat, the groom's procession. The groom does not just walk to the altar. He arrives on a white horse, or sometimes an elephant if the council permits, or now very often a vintage car or a decorated bicycle. He is surrounded by his family and friends, and there is a live band playing the dhol and the nadaswaram. The music is so loud, so energetic. The whole neighborhood will come out to watch. When he reaches the venue, the bride's mother will do the aarti, and then the bride and groom exchange the varmalai, the flower garlands. This is a very crucial moment. They have to lift the garland and place it over each other's heads. But the bride's brothers and friends will try to stop the groom, they will pull the garland down, they will tease him. It is very playful. And then the actual ceremony, the kanyadaan, where the father gives the bride away, and they walk around the sacred fire seven times. The fire is the witness to the marriage. The chanting, the smell of the jasmine flowers, the camphor burning, the vibrant silk sarees... it is a sensory overload. It is so beautiful.

I attended an Indian wedding as a guest once, with Wei Jie's colleague. I was blown away. The scale of it. And the food! The banana leaf meal. I ate so much rice and curry, I had to unbutton my pants under the table. But one thing I noticed, the jewelry. The bride was wearing so much gold, I thought she would get a neck injury. Is that a big part of the custom?

PRP VBD DT JJ NN IN DT NN RB , IN NNP NNP POS NN . PRP VBD VBN RB . DT NN IN PRP . CC DT NN . DT NN NN NN . PRP VBD RB JJ NN CC NN , PRP VBD TO NNP PRP$ NNS IN DT NN . CC CD NN PRP VBD , DT NN . DT NN VBD VBG RB JJ NN , PRP VBD PRP MD VB DT NN NN . VBZ DT DT JJ NN IN DT NN .

Yes, Wei Jie, the gold is very significant. In Indian culture, gold is not just for showing off, although we definitely like to show off a little bit. It is considered the wealth of the woman. It is her streedhan, her financial security. The parents will spend years saving up to buy the gold jewelry for the bride. The mangalsutra, the thali that the groom ties around her neck, that is the most important piece. It signifies that she is married. And the bangles, the anklets... the sound of the glass bangles and the silver anklets when she walks, it is very traditional. When I shoot the close-up shots of the bride's hands, the details of the mehendi and the gold, it is just stunning. But you are right, it is very heavy. I always tell my brides, 'Eh, you need to strengthen your neck muscles before the wedding.'

Let us talk about the logistics of blending these customs. Because if you are a mixed couple, say Chinese and Indian, or Malay and Indian, the complications multiply. Wei Jie, you only had to deal with Chinese and Malay. But what about the outfits? Because I know you had to change clothes multiple times.

VB PRP VB IN DT NNS IN VBG DT NNS . IN IN PRP VBP DT JJ NN , VBP JJ CC JJ , CC NNP CC NNP , DT NNS NN . NNP NNP , PRP RB VBD TO VB IN JJ CC NNP . CC WP IN DT NNS . IN PRP VBP PRP VBD TO VB NNS JJ NNS .

Don't even talk about the outfits. That was my biggest struggle. For the Chinese side, I wore a standard Western suit for the morning gatecrashing, then I changed into a traditional Chinese tunic suit, the changshan, for the tea ceremony because my grandma insisted. Then for the evening banquet, I changed back into a tuxedo. But then for the Malay side, I had to wear the baju melayu for the akad nikah, and then a different, more elaborate songket baju melayu for the bersanding on the pelamin. So in two days, I changed clothes five times. And you know the Singapore weather. Thirty-two degrees, very humid. I was wearing three layers of clothes, sweating like a pig. The makeup auntie had to keep coming to powder my face. But my wife, she had it worse. She had the qipao, the white wedding gown, the evening gown, and then the baju kurung and the songket for the Malay side. She was exhausted. By the end of the second day, she was sleeping in the car with her makeup still on.

The outfit changes are the silent killer of wedding energy. I always tell my couples, if you are doing a multi-ethnic wedding, you must budget for a very good makeup artist who can do different cultural looks. Because you cannot just wear the same dress for the tea ceremony and the bersanding. The aesthetics are completely different. For the Chinese tea ceremony, the bride usually wears a qipao or a traditional kua, which is very heavily embroidered with gold and red. It is very structured. For the Malay bersanding, she wears the baju kurung or kebaya, with the intricate songket and the elaborate headpiece, the cucuk sanggul. The makeup has to change too. The Chinese look is usually very red lip, very sharp. The Malay look is more soft, more glowing, very elegant. And the Indian look, if they add it in, is very dramatic, very heavy on the eyes and the bindi. So the makeup artist needs to be a chameleon. If your makeup artist only knows how to do one style, you will look very out of place in the other cultural outfits.

That is a very good point. The makeup artist needs to be a chameleon. Let us bring in some voices from our audience. We asked our listeners on our Telegram and Instagram to share their most confusing or funny experiences attending or planning a multi-ethnic wedding. Let us read a few. Priya, you want to take the first one?

Sure. This one is from user 'Rojak_Romeo'. He says: 'I am Indian, my fiancée is Chinese. We are planning our wedding next year. Her parents insist on having a vegetarian menu for the Chinese banquet because her grandparents are strict Buddhists. But my side, we are non-veg, and our relatives will complain if there is no chicken or mutton. How do we compromise without offending anyone?' Wah, Rojak_Romeo, this is the classic food dilemma. What is your advice, Wei Jie?

Oh man, I feel his pain. The food is the most sensitive topic. Because in Singapore, food is religion. If you serve bad food, people will talk for the next ten years. If you serve the wrong food, people will get offended for a lifetime. For Rojak_Romeo, my advice is, you cannot please everyone. You have to pick a hotel that is very experienced in multi-ethnic banquets. Most of the 5-star hotels in Singapore, like the Shangri-La or the Marina Bay Sands, they have separate kitchens. They can do a vegetarian menu for the Chinese tables, and a non-vegetarian, maybe even Halal menu, for the Indian tables. But you must be very clear with the catering manager. You must specify, 'No pork, no lard, no beef' for the Chinese vegetarians, and 'No pork, no alcohol' for the Muslim guests if any. And then you just have to tell your relatives, 'Eh, this is a mixed wedding, we are trying to respect everyone. If you want to eat chicken rice, go eat outside.' You have to be firm. Otherwise, the aunties will just complain and ruin the mood.

They told the Chinese relatives, 'You can drink, but you must buy your own alcohol and drink it at the after-party at a pub.' The wedding dinner itself was completely dry. The Chinese uncles were very sad, but they survived. They just drank the iced lemon tea very aggressively.

How do we handle the older generation's kiasu mentality?' Oh, this is the ultimate Singaporean problem. Losing face. Wei Jie, how did you handle your parents?

Wah, this one is very hard. The 'losing face' concept is very real. For my parents, inviting 300 people to the banquet is not about feeding them. It is about showing off that they have a successful son who is marrying a nice girl, and they can afford a big dinner. It is their way of social networking. When my wife and I said we only wanted 50 pax for a casual garden wedding, my mom almost had a stroke. She was like, 'Wah, so cheap one. People will think we have no money.' So what we did was, we compromised. We did the micro-wedding for us, our close friends, and the young cousins. Very chill, very aesthetic. But then, we organized a separate 'appreciation dinner' for the parents. Just 10 tables, very traditional, at a nice Chinese restaurant. We let them invite all their friends, their colleagues, their mahjong kakis. We did the tea ceremony there, we let them collect the ang bao from their friends, and they were very happy. They got their face, and we got our intimate wedding. It cost more money, but it saved my sanity.

That is a very smart strategy, Wei Jie. The 'two-event' compromise. I see a lot of couples doing that now. They do the solemnization and the intimate lunch for their friends, and then they let the parents host the big dinner banquet at night. It is the best of both worlds. But you have to set the budget early. Because if you do two events, the cost is not 50% more, it is 100% more. You are paying for two venues, two caterers, two photographers. The ang bao you collect might cover the banquet, but it will not cover the intimate garden wedding. The young couples must be prepared to top up their own savings.

Let us talk about the kids. Because in a multi-ethnic marriage, the question of the children's race and religion always comes up. In Singapore, the child's race is registered according to the father. But what about the cultural upbringing? Wei Jie, your wife is Malay, you are Chinese. How are you navigating that for your future kids?

VB PRP VB IN DT NNS . IN IN DT JJ JJ JJ NN , DT NN IN DT NNS POS NN CC NN RB VBZ RP . IN NNP , DT NN POS NN VBZ VBN VBG IN DT NN . CC WP IN DT JJ NN . NNP NNP , PRP$ NN VBZ NNP , PRP VBP JJ . WRB VBP PRP VBG IN IN PRP$ JJ NNS .

This is something we discuss a lot. Legally, the kid will be registered as Chinese because I am the father. But culturally, we want the kid to be a true Singaporean 'rojak'. We want them to speak Mandarin, because my parents only speak Mandarin and Hokkien. But we also want them to speak Malay, because my wife's family speaks Malay, and it is the national language. We celebrate all the festivals. CNY, we do the lo hei, we give ang bao. Hari Raya, we wear the baju melayu, we visit my in-laws, we eat the rendang. Deepavali, we visit our Indian friends, we eat the murukku. The kid will have so many ang bao and duit raya, they will be very rich. But the challenge is the religious aspect. Islam is a very structured religion. If my wife wants the kids to be Muslim, that is a very serious discussion we had before we got married. In the end, we agreed that the kids will be raised with the values of both cultures, but the religious choice will be up to them when they are older. My in-laws were not very happy at first, but they understand that in a modern mixed marriage, you cannot force religion. You have to lead by example. So we just show the kids the beauty of both cultures, and let them decide when they grow up.

That is a very mature approach, Wei Jie. I think in Singapore, a lot of mixed couples are adopting this 'cultural buffet' approach for their kids. They expose the kids to everything. I have Indian-Chinese friends whose kids wear the cheongsam for CNY and the pavani for Deepavali. They eat both the mooncakes and the laddoos. The kids grow up very open-minded. They don't see race as a barrier. To them, it is just normal. They ask, 'Mom, why is our food different from our friends?' And the parents say, 'Because we are special, we get to eat all the good food.' It is a very beautiful way to raise the next generation of Singaporeans. They are truly the 'rojak' generation, in the best way possible.

The 'rojak' generation. I love that. Let us look at one more comment before we wrap up. This is from 'Camera_Shy_Groom'. He says: 'I am marrying a Malay girl next month. I am very camera shy. Oh, Camera_Shy_Groom, do not worry. First of all, most grooms are camera shy. You think you are the only one? No, 90% of the men I shoot are very stiff. They don't know what to do with their hands. My advice is, do not try to pose. Posing looks fake. Instead, just interact with your bride. When you are on the pelamin, just whisper to her, ask her if she is hungry, tell her a joke. The camera will capture the natural smile, the natural eye contact. For the gatecrashing, just embrace the embarrassment. If the bridesmaids tell you to dance, just dance badly. The worse you dance, the more fun the video will be. The camera loves authenticity. If you try to look cool and you fail, it looks weird. If you try to be funny and you fail, it is still hilarious. So just let go of your ego. You are there to celebrate your wife, not to model for a magazine. And trust your photographer. I will always direct you. If I say 'look at her', just look at her. If I say 'walk', just walk. Don't overthink it.

That is very good advice. I remember during my bersanding, I was so stiff. I was sitting like a statue. Then my wife pinched my leg under the table to make me smile. The photographer captured that exact moment, and it is actually our favorite photo. Because I am looking at her with this very shocked, pained expression, and she is laughing. It is very real. So yeah, just interact with your partner. The camera will do the rest.

It is a lot to take in. But at the end of the day, a multi-ethnic wedding is a celebration of love that transcends the traditional boundaries. It is a reflection of what Singapore is all about. Different races, different religions, different customs, all sitting at the same table, eating the same food, and celebrating the same union. Priya, Wei Jie, thank you so much for sharing your insights and your war stories with us today.

topic: Learning Mandarin or Malay in school — still relevant?
data: Welcome back to the show, guys. Today we are diving into a topic that I know every single Singaporean student can relate to, whether you absolutely love it or you dread it with your whole soul. We are talking about Mother Tongue.
VBP RP IN DT NN , NNS . NN PRP VBP VBG IN DT NN WDT PRP VBP DT JJ JJ NN MD VB IN , IN PRP RB VBP PRP CC PRP VBP PRP IN PRP$ JJ NN . PRP VBP VBG IN NNP NNP .

Wah lao, don't even remind me. Just hearing the words 'Mother Tongue' already make me feel sian. Like, why we still talking about this in twenty twenty-four? I thought we already survive the PSLE and O-level trauma, why you want to open old wounds?
JJ NN , VBP RB RB VB PRP . RB VBG DT NNS `` NNP NNP '' RB VBP PRP VB JJ . UH , WRB PRP RB VBG IN DT IN CD CD HYPH CD . PRP VBD PRP RB VBP DT NN CC JJ HYPH NN NN , WRB PRP VBP TO VB JJ NNS .

Because it is a valid question, leh. A lot of people are asking, is it still relevant? Like, we grow up in a country where English is the main language of instruction, the language of business, the language we use to talk to our friends. So why are we still forcing kids to spend so many hours learning formal Mandarin or formal Malay?

IN PRP VBZ DT JJ NN , RP . DT NN IN NNS VBP VBG , VBZ PRP RB JJ . UH , PRP VBP RP IN DT NN WRB NNP VBZ DT JJ NN IN NN , DT NN IN NN , DT NN PRP VBP TO VB IN PRP$ NNS . RB WRB VBP PRP RB VBG NNS TO VB RB JJ NNS VBG JJ NNP CC JJ NNP .

I mean, I get the logic. But let's be real. For a lot of us, the 'Mother Tongue' we speak at home is not the one they teach in school. Like, my family speaks a mix of English, Hokkien, and very broken Mandarin. We call it rojak. But in school, they teach you this very standard, textbook Mandarin. It is so chim. Sometimes I listen to the listening comprehension paper and I am like, who talks like this in real life?

Exactly. It is that disconnect. You learn all these fancy chengyu and formal sentence structures, but then you go to the hawker centre and the uncle just look at you like you are an ang moh if you try to order in proper Mandarin. You just end up pointing and saying, 'Uncle, this one, one plate, no cucumber, more chili.' That is the extent of our daily usage.

Right? And the oral exam. Alamak, the oral exam is a whole different level of stress. They show you a picture, right? And you have to describe what is happening. But the picture is always so random. Like, a family having a picnic, but one person is looking sad. And the examiner will ask, 'What do you think the boy is feeling?' And you just staring at the picture like blur sotong, trying to remember the vocabulary for 'melancholy' in Mandarin.

And if you make one mistake, the teacher will correct you immediately. It is very intimidating. But then, the government and the educators always push this narrative that it is about 'knowing your roots'. They say if you don't learn your Mother Tongue, you will lose your cultural identity.

But what is cultural identity, really? Is it being able to recite a poem by Li Bai, or is it knowing how to respect your elders, knowing which festivals to celebrate, and knowing how to behave at a family dinner? I think a lot of Singaporeans have the cultural values down pat, even if their grammar is not perfect. My Ah Ma doesn't care if I use the correct measure word for a piece of fruit. She just happy I visit her and eat the food she cook.

That is a very fair point. But then we have to look at the other side of the coin. The economic argument. People always say, 'Eh, China is a superpower now. If you can speak Mandarin, you have a huge advantage in the job market.'

I agree, but with a caveat. The Mandarin they teach in our local schools is not necessarily the Mandarin that is useful for doing business in China today. The language evolves, right? In China, they have so much internet slang, new buzzwords, and regional accents. If you only learn the textbook version, you go there and you still cannot communicate effectively. You become the one who is blur.

True. And also, a lot of MNCs in Singapore operate in English. Even when they deal with Chinese clients, many of those clients also speak English very well. So the 'Mandarin advantage' is sometimes overstated. It is good to have, sure, but is it worth the immense stress and the opportunity cost of not learning other more practical skills, like coding or data analytics?

And what about Malay? We cannot forget that Malay is the national language of Singapore. Our national anthem is in Malay. But how many ethnic Chinese or Indian Singaporeans actually take the effort to learn it beyond the basic 'selamat pagi' and 'terima kasih'?

That is a very interesting point. I think there is a missed opportunity there. If we are in Southeast Asia, being able to speak Malay or Bahasa Indonesia is actually a massive advantage. Malaysia, Indonesia, Brunei... that is a huge market. But the way it is taught in school is very academic. They focus on grammar rules and formal writing, rather than conversational fluency.

Exactly. Like, who says 'Saya hendak pergi ke tandas' in real life? We just say 'I going toilet' or 'gi tandas' only. If the curriculum was more focused on practical, everyday communication and maybe even some business Malay, I think more people would actually find it useful and enjoy learning it.

But then you have the kiasu parents. If you make it too practical or too easy, they will complain that the standard is dropping. 'Eh, why my child not learning the complex grammar? How they going to score A1?' It is a vicious cycle. The system is designed for examination, not for actual language acquisition.

Wah, you hit the nail on the head. The whole system is geared towards the exam. You learn to pass the test, not to speak the language. After the exam is over, you immediately forget everything. And this brings us to the impact on Singlish. Some linguists argue that our Mother Tongue education actually shapes Singlish in a positive way. The grammar structures, the particles, the vocabulary. Like, we use 'makan' from Malay, 'siam' from Malay, 'kiasu' from Hokkien. Our English is enriched by these languages.

I agree. Singlish is very efficient. It takes the grammatical structure of Chinese or Malay and applies it to English vocabulary. Like, 'You go where?' instead of 'Where are you going?' It is direct, it is fast, and it gets the point across. But the establishment always looks down on it. They say it is 'grammatically incorrect' and 'bad English'.

But is it bad, or is it just different? Language is a tool for communication. If I can communicate perfectly with my fellow Singaporean using Singlish, then it is serving its purpose. The problem is when we try to force a very rigid, formal Mother Tongue standard on kids who grow up in an English-speaking environment. It feels unnatural.

Let's talk about the SAP schools for a bit. Special Assistance Plan schools. They are supposed to be the bastion of bilingualism and Chinese culture. Yeah, because peer pressure is real. If everybody else is speaking English, you don't want to be the odd one out speaking Mandarin. You will be seen as trying too hard, or 'acting Chinese'. And then you have the reverse situation. You have kids from Chinese-speaking families who are forced to take English as their Mother Tongue because their parents want them to focus on English, but the system doesn't really allow that easily. Or they take Mandarin, but they are already fluent, so the class is boring for them. They are not challenged.

So the question is, what is the solution? Do we make Mother Tongue optional? If we make it optional, I guarantee you, ninety percent of students will drop it. Because let's be honest, it is the easiest subject to drop to free up time for Math and Science, which carry more weight in the overall scoring.

But if ninety percent drop it, then what happens to the next generation? They won't even be able to read the Chinese New Year couplets on the door. They won't be able to talk to their grandparents. Is that a trade-off we are willing to make for 'efficiency'?

I don't think it has to be a binary choice. Maybe we can revamp the curriculum. Make it less about rote memorization and exams, and more about culture, media, and practical communication. Yes, the diglossia is very strong in Malay too. There is the high variety used in news and school, and the low variety used in daily life. The gap is huge. It really highlights that 'Mother Tongue' is a bit of a misnomer for many Singaporeans. For a lot of us, English is the de facto mother tongue. It is the language we dream in, the language we swear in. The 'Mother Tongue' is more like a 'heritage language' that we are required to study.

'Heritage language'. I like that term. It is more accurate. It acknowledges that it is part of our background, but it doesn't carry the heavy expectation that it is our primary mode of communication. If the policy shifted to treating it as a heritage language, maybe the pressure would be lower, and the focus could be on appreciation rather than proficiency.

But then the government will say, 'If we lower the standard, our global competitiveness will drop.' They are always worried about the PISA rankings and how we compare to other countries.

But are we competing with China or Indonesia in terms of language proficiency, or are we competing in terms of innovation, critical thinking, and adaptability? I would argue that forcing kids to spend hundreds of hours memorizing characters they will forget is not the best use of their time. That time could be spent on coding, robotics, or even just playing and developing soft skills.

That is a very strong argument. The opportunity cost is real. Every hour spent on drilling Mother Tongue is an hour not spent on something else. And if the return on investment is low, then we need to rethink the strategy.

But then again, if we drop it completely, we lose a unique part of our Singaporean identity. Our multilingualism is what makes us, us. It is what gives us that cultural agility. We can navigate different cultural spaces because we have been exposed to these languages, even if we are not fluent.

topic: Family pressure to get married before 30
data: Welcome back to the show, everyone. So today we are tackling something that... honestly, every single person in their late twenties has to deal with at least once a month. Family pressure to get married before thirty.
VBP RP IN DT NN , NN . RB NN PRP VBP VBG NN WDT : RB , DT JJ NN IN PRP$ JJ NNS VBZ TO VB IN IN JJS RB DT NN . NN NN TO VB VBN IN CD .

Wah, straight to the jugular ah? Okay, I see how it is. But yeah, you cannot run away from it. The moment you hit twenty-nine, suddenly every auntie at the kopitiam, every uncle at the family dinner, they all become matchmakers.

It is the annual tradition, right? Chinese New Year, Mid-Autumn, Christmas... doesn't matter the festival. The question is always the same: 'So when you bringing someone back?' And if you say 'no', the follow-up is immediate: 'Eh, you already how old liao?'

Exactly. And it is not just the questioning. It is the subtle hints. The passive-aggressive comments. 'Oh, your cousin already pregnant second child.' 'My friend son just bought BTO, now renovating.' It is like a competition, but the prize is just... stress.

I mean, from the parents perspective, I get it. They worry. In their generation, marrying early was the norm. You finish school, you get job, you marry, you buy house, you have kids. It is a straight line. But now, the timeline is completely different. People are focusing on career, traveling, saving up, figuring out who they are. The script has changed, but the older generation still reading from the old playbook.

And that is where the clash happens. They see you turning thirty and they think 'panic mode'. But we are like, 'Chill lah, I still trying to pay off my student loan and figure out if I actually like my job.' The priorities are so misaligned.

Let's talk about the cultural weight of it. Especially in Asian families, marriage is not just about two people. It is about the family lineage, about saving face, about fulfilling expectations. When you don't get married, some parents feel like they failed, or that their face is lost in the community. So the pressure trickles down to you.

Ya, it is very true. My mum always say, 'If you not marry soon, how I face my mahjong friends? All of them already got grandchildren.' I am like, 'Mum, your mahjong friends don't pay my bills though.' But you cannot say that out loud, because then you become the unfilial child. So you just nod, smile, and change the subject.

The guilt trip is real. They don't even need to yell. They just use the silent treatment, or the heavy sigh when you bring up work. 'Oh, so busy with work ah? But work won't take care of you when you old.' That kind of thing. It plants a seed of anxiety even if you know logically you are doing fine.

And what about the practical side? The cost of getting married in Singapore is insane. You need the banquet, the photography, the wedding planner, the honeymoon, and don't even get me started on the housing market. How can you rush into marriage when you haven't even figured out your own finances?

This is the part that makes no sense to me. Parents pushing you to marry before thirty, but then also complaining that weddings nowadays are too expensive. 'Why you need so many tables? Why you need that fancy venue?' So do you want me to marry or not? You want the grand wedding photos to show off, but you don't want to pay for it. Make up your mind lah.

Exactly. And then there is the BTO queue. You apply, you wait three, four years. By the time you get the keys, you already thirty-one or thirty-two. So the timeline is completely out of sync with the 'marry before thirty' expectation. Unless you rent, but then the parents say 'Why you renting? Waste money.' It is a catch-twenty-two.

I think we also need to address the gender difference here. The pressure on women to settle down before thirty is still significantly higher. There is this lingering stigma about 'leftover women' or 'old maids', which is such an outdated and offensive term. But it still lingers in the older generation's mindset.

So true. I have female friends who are doctors, lawyers, high-flying executives, but the first thing their relatives ask is 'Got boyfriend already?'. topic: Ozempic and weight loss trends in Singapore
data: Welcome back to the show, guys. Today we are tackling a topic that is literally taking over our Instagram feeds, our TikTok For You pages, and of course, our WhatsApp group chats. It is the Ozempic craze. And we are not just talking about the global Hollywood trend; we are talking about how this is playing out right here in our little red dot. Joining me today are two very special guests. First, we have Chloe, who is a local model and content creator, and she has seen the fashion and entertainment industry up close. Welcome, Chloe.

But wait, Ozempic is originally a diabetes medication, right? How does it even work for weight loss?
CC VB , NNP VBZ RB DT NN NN , JJ . WRB VBZ PRP RB VB IN NN NN .

Correct. The active ingredient in Ozempic is semaglutide. It was developed to treat type 2 diabetes. It works by mimicking a hormone called GLP-1, which is released in our gut after we eat. This hormone tells our brain that we are full, and it slows down gastric emptying. So basically, you feel full very quickly, you stay full for a long time, and your appetite just drops significantly. For a diabetic, this helps control blood sugar. But for someone without diabetes, this massive reduction in appetite leads to rapid weight loss.

And the side effects, right? Because I have friends who tried it, and they say they literally lose the desire to even look at food. Which, in Singapore, is like a sin. We are a food paradise. If you don't want to makan, what are you even living for?

Exactly. The side effects are very real. Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation. Some people describe it as feeling like they have a mild food poisoning every day. And because you are eating so much less, you also lose muscle mass, not just fat. That is a big concern for us in the medical field.

You might not be getting the genuine product, or the dosage might be wrong. And then there is the black market. People buying it from unofficial Telegram groups. That is playing Russian roulette with your health.

Honestly? Very tempted. There was this one period, maybe two years ago, I had a few big campaigns, and I needed to fit into some very specific designer clothes. I was starving myself, doing all the crash diets. And then I saw my peer getting the injection, and she just melted away. She was eating only like three bites of salad a day, and she looked amazing in the clothes. I was like, 'Why am I suffering when there is a shortcut?' But then I did my research, and I saw the side effects. The 'Ozempic face' is very real. Your face becomes very gaunt, your skin loses elasticity because you are losing fat too fast. You look tired, you look older. And I thought, 'I am a model, my face is my asset. I cannot afford to look like I haven't slept in a month.'

And that is a huge psychological concern. Food is not just fuel; it is culture, it is social connection. When a drug removes your appetite, it also removes the joy of eating. And then you start to associate food with nausea. That can lead to a very unhealthy relationship with food, even after you stop the medication. We are seeing cases of orthorexia, or just general food anxiety, because people are terrified of eating because they don't want to feel sick.

And what happens when you stop taking it? Because it is not like you can stay on it forever, especially at these prices. Do you just gain the weight back?

That is the biggest catch-22 of the obesity medication industry. The clinical trials show that when patients stop taking semaglutide, they regain about two-thirds of the lost weight within a year. Because your body's natural hunger hormones come back, and sometimes they overcompensate. You get the 'rebound hunger'. So unless you are willing to stay on the injection for the rest of your life, or you have completely rewired your lifestyle and eating habits, the weight will come back. And in Singapore, where we are surrounded by hawker centres, food delivery apps, and a very strong makan culture, rewiring your habits is extremely difficult.

Yes, and the medicalization of cosmetic weight loss is a slippery slope. These drugs are life-changing for people who are clinically obese and have comorbidities like diabetes or heart disease. You are exposing yourself to potential thyroid tumors, pancreatitis, and gallbladder issues, just to fit into a size four dress.

I don't just say no. I try to understand their 'why'. Why do they want to lose weight? Is it for health, or is it for societal pressure? I explain the science, the side effects, the rebound effect. And then we work on what I call 'food architecture'. How can we navigate the Singapore food environment without feeling deprived? Because if you just tell a Singaporean to eat boiled chicken breast and broccoli every day, they will quit in three days. You need to find a way to enjoy local food but in a modified way.

Like what? Because I feel like every hawker stall is deep-fried or cooked in lard. Char kway teow, roti prata, curry puffs. How do you eat healthy at a hawker centre?

It is about choices and portions. You can still eat at the hawker centre. Instead of char kway teow, you can go for thunder tea rice. It is very nutritious, lots of vegetables, good fats. Or you can eat economic bee hoon, but ask for less noodles, more vegetables, and don't drink the gravy. The gravy is where all the sodium and fat is. Or yong tau foo, but choose the non-fried items and ask for clear soup instead of the sweet sauce. It is about being smart. And also, the protein is very important. When you eat at a zi char stall, order a steamed fish, a blanched vegetable, and a small bowl of rice. You can still eat out with your friends, you just choose differently.

That is a very profound point. It medicalizes the pursuit of an unrealistic body ideal. And it creates a two-tier system. The rich can afford the $1,000 a month injections to stay thin, while the lower-income groups, who actually suffer from higher rates of obesity because of cheaper, calorie-dense food, cannot afford it. They are left to deal with the systemic issues of food deserts and lack of time to exercise.

Absolutely. It is a socioeconomic issue as well. Obesity is often linked to poverty and stress. People working multiple jobs don't have time to cook healthy meals; they rely on fast food or cheap hawker food that is high in carbs and fat. Giving them a drug is not solving the root cause. We need to look at urban planning, food subsidies for healthy options, and education. But instead, the narrative is just 'take this injection and fix yourself'.

Exactly. And we must not forget the supply chain issue. Because so many people are using it off-label for cosmetic weight loss, there is a global shortage of semaglutide. This means that actual diabetics who need this drug to manage their blood sugar and prevent complications like blindness or amputation, they cannot get it. Their insurance or government subsidies might not cover the 'weight loss' version, and the 'diabetes' version is out of stock. That is a massive ethical dilemma. We are prioritizing cosmetic thinness over life-saving medication for chronic diseases.

It is very delicate. I have to draw the line between health and psychology. If a client comes to me with a BMI of 19, and they want to lose another three kilos, I will not give them a diet plan. I will gently suggest that they speak to a psychologist or a counselor. Because no amount of kale and quinoa will fix the mental anguish they are feeling about their body. And as a dietitian, my code of ethics prevents me from helping someone become malnourished. But it is tough, because they will just go to another clinic that doesn't ask questions.

My advice is to look at the long term. Ask yourself, 'Am I willing to take this injection every week for the rest of my life? Can I afford it? Am I okay with the potential side effects?' If the answer is no, then this is not for you. Focus on sustainable changes. Find an activity you enjoy, whether it is swimming, cycling, or just walking around East Coast Park. Find a way to eat that is satisfying but nutritious. And most importantly, make peace with your body. Your worth is not determined by the number on the scale or the size of your dress.

Ah, the 'chope' culture! This is a very sacred Singaporean tradition. The tissue packet is a legally binding contract in the hawker centre ecosystem. If you see a packet of tissue, a umbrella, or even a weirdly shaped rock on a table, that table is taken. You cannot sit there. It's the most efficient queuing system in the world. No one is standing around waiting for a table. You just 'chope' it, go buy your food, and by the time you come back, the table is yours.

'Xia sui'. This is a very important one. It means embarrassing, or losing face. But it's not just 'embarrassing'. It's that specific type of embarrassment where you do something stupid in front of other people, and now you have to live with the shame. Like, if you are giving a presentation and you call your boss by the wrong name, that is 'xia sui'. Or if you wave at someone thinking they are your friend, but it's actually a stranger. Very 'xia sui'.

'Atas' literally means 'upstairs' or 'high' in Malay. But in Singlish, it means high-class, expensive, fancy, or pretentious. If you go to a restaurant in Marina Bay Sands and a meal costs $150, that is an 'atas' restaurant. If your friend starts using big words and acting like a philosopher, you can say, 'Eh, don't act so atas lah.' It's a way to humble people. Singaporeans love to humble each other. [Laughs] 'Shiok' is the ultimate positive word. It's peak satisfaction. But your mom is right, it's very visceral. It's a physical feeling. You cannot use 'shiok' for something intellectual. You cannot say, 'This book is very shiok.' You say, 'This book very 'chim'.'

'Blur sotong'. That's a classic. 'Blur' means clueless, unaware of what's going on. Like a squid floating in the water, just drifting. 'Blur sotong' is the full phrase. If you are in a meeting and you have no idea what the KPIs are, you are a 'blur sotong'. But it's not a harsh insult. It's more like, 'Wake up, man!'

I love how the insults are never truly mean. They are always playful. Even when someone calls you 'goblok' or 'bodoh', which I know means stupid, it's usually said with a laugh.

Well, 'goblok' and 'bodoh' are a bit stronger, those are actual Malay words for stupid. You don't use those lightly. But 'siao', 'blur', 'kiasu', those are affectionate. We use them to bond. When you can insult your friend in Singlish and they laugh, that's when you know you've truly integrated into the culture.

That's a great point. It's the ultimate test of belonging. But let's talk about the code-switching. Because I've noticed that you, Darren, and all my Singaporean friends, you change your accent depending on who you are talking to. When you talk to me, you speak very clearly, almost like an American news anchor. But when you turn to your local friend, suddenly you are speaking this rapid-fire, tonal dialect.

[Laughs] Guilty as charged. That is the Singaporean chameleon effect. We are very aware of our audience. When we speak to foreigners, we consciously 'enunciate'. We drop the particles, we slow down, we use standard grammar. We call it 'Tanjung Pagar English' or 'CBC English' - Chinese body, Chinese heart, but speaking Western English. But when we are with our own people, we switch to the 'basement' dialect. The pure Singlish. It's a way of signaling intimacy. If I speak full Singlish to you, it means I consider you one of us. I don't need to filter myself.

topic: Online dating app experiences in SG
data: Welcome back to the show, guys. Today we are tackling a topic that is literally the bane of every single millennial and Gen Z in Singapore. Online dating apps. And joining me today are two people who have survived the digital meat market. First, we have Marcus, who has been on Tinder, Bumble, Hinge, and probably even Paktor since the stone age. Welcome, Marcus.

Wah, stone age is a bit harsh lah. But yes, I have seen things. I have seen the evolution of the Singaporean dater. From the blurry mirror selfies in the gym toilet to the professional LinkedIn headshots. It is a journey, man. A very tiring journey.

Thanks for having me. And honestly, 'trenches' is the right word. I deleted all my apps last week. My thumb actually got muscle strain from swiping left. I think I need to go see a physio for my right index finger. The amount of guys holding fish in their profile pictures... I thought I was on a fishing app, not a dating app.

[Laughs] The fish photos! That is a universal red flag, right? But let's break down the landscape first. Because in Singapore, we don't just have one app. We have the whole ecosystem. You got Tinder for the... you know, the 'casual' crowd. You got Bumble where the girls have to text first. You got Coffee Meets Bagel, which is very serious, very 'I want to find my BTO partner' energy. And then you got Hinge, which tries to be the 'designed to be deleted' app. Marcus, which one are you currently on?

Honestly? I am on all of them. I am what you call a 'kiasu' swiper. I treat dating apps like Shopee during the 11.11 sales. I want to see all the options, add to cart, compare prices, read the reviews, but I never checkout. I just swipe, swipe, swipe. But if I have to choose, Bumble is okay because at least it saves me the effort of thinking of a good opening line. The girls will message first. But then, the pressure is on them, so their first message is usually just 'Hi' or 'How was your weekend?'. And then the conversation die already.

That is the problem with the 'talking stage' in Singapore. It is so dry. You match, you say hi, you ask what they do for a living, and then... silence. Or they give you one-word answers. 'Oh, you work in finance? Cool.' 'Yeah, you?' 'Marketing.' 'Nice.' Then what? We are just doing a job interview. Very sian.

Exactly. The 'interview mode'. Singaporeans are very practical, right? We want to know your credentials immediately. 'You study where? Work where? Stay where? Got car or not?' It is like checking off a checklist for the HDB BTO application. 'Okay, you are a Singapore Citizen, you earn more than $5k, you don't own a private property. Pass. Let's go for coffee.'

[Laughs] Very accurate. But you cannot blame them, right? In Singapore, everything is about efficiency. We don't have time to waste. If I know you are not looking for a serious relationship, or you are still studying and I am already working and thinking about retirement, why we need to pretend? But then, some people take it too far. I had this one girl, first message she ask me, 'What is your opinion on the current property cooling measures?' I was like, 'Eh, we are on Hinge, not a property seminar.'
