Wait, but isn't that a green flag for some people? Because if you are looking to settle down, you need to know if your financial values align. In Singapore, if you cannot talk about BTO, CPF, and inflation, how you going to survive a marriage? Marriage here is not just about love, it is a merger of two corporations.

That is very true. The financial pressure in Singapore is real. When you are dating, you are not just dating the person, you are dating their financial portfolio. And that brings us to the profile pictures. Because the profiles on these apps in Singapore are very... specific. Everyone is either in Bali, in Japan eating omakase, or at a beach club in Sentosa. Hello, we live in Singapore, got also eating economic rice at Ghim Moh, right? Where is the authenticity?

The 'atas' flex is very strong. You see a guy, normal looking, but every photo is him leaning on a different supercar. You are like, 'Bro, is this your car or your friend's car? Or you just rent it for the weekend to catfish?' And the girls, everyone looks like an influencer. Got the ring light, got the aesthetic cafe background. But then you meet them in real life, and they look like their secondary school graduation photo. The catfishing here is very subtle. They don't use a completely different face, they just use the best Snapchat filter and a very good angle.

I experienced that. I matched with this guy, his photos were all him playing golf, very sporty, very healthy. I thought, 'Okay, this one must be active.' We meet for a coffee at a cafe in Tiong Bahru. He arrives, and he is out of breath. He tell me he walked from the MRT station. I was like, 'Wait, I thought you play golf?' He say, 'Ya, I play mini golf with my nephew.' I nearly choke on my latte. That is not the same thing!

[Laughs] Mini golf! That is a classic. But let's talk about the first date logistics. Because the first date in Singapore is a whole science. You cannot just meet anywhere. If you meet at a fancy restaurant in Marina Bay Sands, it is too much pressure. If you meet at a hawker centre, it might be too casual, or the noise level is so loud you cannot hear each other talk. So where is the sweet spot?

The sweet spot is a cafe in the CBD after work. It is convenient, you can just take the MRT or walk from your office. You order a coffee or a beer. If the date is going well, you can say, 'Eh, you hungry or not? Let's go eat somewhere.' If the date is going badly, you can just say, 'Eh, I got early morning meeting tomorrow, I need to go.' Very strategic.

But the CBD after work is very crowded. Everywhere also full. You have to queue for a table, or you have to 'chope' a seat. And that is where the first test happens. How does your date handle the hawker centre or cafe queue? If they start complaining about the ERP, or the parking, or the service charge, that is a red flag. You know your date is a keeper if they don't complain about the SMRT when you both are stuck in a crowd.

And then there is the bill. The great Singaporean bill-splitting debate. In the West, maybe the guy pays, or they split it. But in Singapore, we have the 'PayLah dance'. You know what I mean? The bill comes, the guy reaches for his wallet, 'Eh, let me pay.' The girl says, 'No, no, I PayLah you.' The guy says, 'Eh, cannot, I invited you.' The girl says, 'Okay, but next time you pay.' And then there is no next time because you both ghosted each other.

[Laughs] The PayLah dance is a cultural heritage. But honestly, for the first date, I always insist on paying. Not because I am a traditionalist, but because I don't want the awkwardness of calculating who owes what. If it is $40, just PayLah $20 each, very clean. But some girls get offended if you ask to split. They think, 'Wah, this guy very calculative.' But then, if you pay for everything, they think you are trying to 'buy' them or you are a sugar daddy. Damn hard to please.

'Seeing how'. The most dangerous phrase in Singapore English. It means everything, and it means nothing. You can be 'seeing how' for six months. You go on dates every weekend, you meet each other's friends, but if someone ask, 'Eh, you guys dating ah?' You will say, 'Eh, just seeing how first.' It is a defense mechanism. We are so kiasi of getting hurt, or kiasi of committing too early, so we stay in this limbo.

But the problem with 'seeing how' is that it leads to parallel dating. Because you are not exclusive, the other person is also not exclusive. So you are texting this guy, thinking he is special, and then you find out he is also 'seeing how' with three other girls from Bumble. It is like a job market. You are interviewing, they are interviewing, and the one with the best package gets the offer.

That is the paradox of choice. Dating apps give us the illusion that there is always someone better out there. Just one more swipe, one more match. Now, you are dating someone, but you keep one eye on the app. 'Eh, this girl is quite cute, maybe I should match also.' It makes people very disposable.

It is very lonely, honestly. You train 365 days a year. You wake up at 5 AM, you go to the track, you do the gym, you eat your boiled chicken breast, you say no to your friends when they want to go for zi char supper. You sacrifice your youth, your social life, and sometimes your career. And for what? For that two-week window where people suddenly care. The rest of the year, you are just a guy running in circles at the Singapore Sports Hub while people walk their dogs. And the worst part is, when you don't win a medal, the narrative changes very fast. Suddenly, you are a waste of taxpayer money.

Wah, that is very harsh. But I understand where the public comes from, Ryan. No offense. But Singapore is a very pragmatic society. We are a small city-state, we have no natural resources. Everything is about ROI, return on investment. When the government pours millions into SportSG, into the Singapore Sports Institute, into sending athletes overseas for training, the common folk will ask, 'Okay, but what is the return? We got medals or not?' If we spend ten million and we get zero medals, the aunties and uncles at the kopi shop will say, 'Wah, waste money only. Better use that money to give everyone a CDC voucher.'

I understand the pragmatism. I am Singaporean also, I grew up in the same system. But sports is not just about the ROI in terms of medals. It is about national identity. It is about showing the world that Singapore can compete on the global stage. When Joseph Schooling won gold in Rio, do you know how many kids suddenly jumped into the swimming pool? When Shanti won gold in the 100m, the track and field clubs suddenly got a massive spike in registrations. That is the ROI. You cannot put a price tag on inspiration. But I admit, the pressure is very heavy. When you are on the starting block, you are not just running for yourself. You are carrying the weight of three million people who are expecting you to make them proud. And if you trip, the internet will eat you alive.

Okay, I will be very honest. When I am doing my IPPT, and I am struggling to pass the 2.4km run, and my lungs are burning, and I see on the news that some athlete got an exemption because they are 'training for a major games', I feel a bit salty. They just see the 'exemption' and think it is a free pass.

Let's talk about the lifestyle of an athlete in Singapore. Because Singapore is a food paradise. We are surrounded by hawker centres, Michelin-starred street food, late-night supper spots. Ryan, you are a middle-distance runner. You have to watch your diet. How do you survive in a country where 'makan' is a national obsession?

[Laughs] Wah, this one is the real torture. You ask any Singaporean athlete, the hardest part is not the training, it is the food. Mark, you mentioned the office culture. Let's pivot to the corporate world. In Singapore, we have a very strong hustle culture. Long hours, high stress. How does the average working adult find time for sports? Is there a sporting culture in the offices, or is it all about the tuition culture for their kids?

The office culture? Very hard. Most of my colleagues, after work, they just want to go home, lie on the sofa, and watch Netflix. Or they go to the gym, but they do it for vanity, not for sports. They want to look good for Instagram, they want to build the 'bat wing' shoulders. But actual sports? Playing a team sport like football or basketball? Very difficult. The courts are always fully booked, and if you want to play, you have to pay for the court rental, which is very expensive now. And for the kids, wah, don't even talk. The parents are so kiasu. Saturday morning, you go to any sports complex, you see the kids. But they are not playing for fun. They are in a structured academy, being drilled by a coach, because the parents want to put 'Captain of the Football Team' in their portfolio for primary school registration. It is not about the love of the game; it is about the portfolio.

That is the tragedy of Singapore sports. The 'performing monkey' syndrome. I have friends who are coaches at the grassroots level. They see kids who have natural talent, kids who just love to run, to kick the ball. But the parents will pull them out at age 13 because 'Secondary school coming, need to focus on studies, sports cannot buy you BTO.' The parents view sports as a distraction from academic success. In Europe or the US, sports and education go hand in hand. Here, they are mutually exclusive. You either be a scholar, or you be a 'performing monkey' who entertains people on the field but has no future. That mindset is killing our grassroots.

Wah, my parents almost disown me. [Laughs] No, seriously, they were very against it. My dad is a very traditional Asian father. He wants me to go to a local university, get a degree in engineering or finance, get a stable job in a GLC, and then get married. When I told them I want to be a full-time athlete, my dad said, 'You think you can eat the medals? You run until you old, then what? You go become a PE teacher?' It took me years to prove to them that this is a viable career. And even now, when I go to family gatherings, my aunties will ask, 'Eh, Ryan, still running ah? When you going to get a proper job?' Very jialat. You have to have a very thick skin to survive in this environment.

I think that is why we don't have a strong domestic league culture. Look at football. The Singapore Premier League is struggling. The stadiums are empty. Why? Because the Singaporean fan is very kiasu and very demanding. We watch the English Premier League on Saturday night. We see the world-class facilities, the massive stadiums, the superstar players. Then we go to the local stadium at Jalan Besar or Our Tampines Hub, and the standard is just... different. The fans will complain, 'Wah, the passing very sloppy, the tactics very chim.' They compare our local boys to Son Heung-min or De Bruyne. It is an unfair comparison, but that is the reality. The local athletes cannot meet the unrealistic standards set by the international broadcasts.

Of course it does. You are human. When you compete, you want to hear the crowd. You want to feel the energy. In the SEA Games, when the Indonesian or Vietnamese fans come, the stadium is shaking. The drums, the chanting, the flares. It is intimidating, but it is electric. You rise to the occasion. But in Singapore, when we have local competitions, the stands are filled with maybe three family members and a few friends holding a banner. It is very quiet. You can hear the coach breathing. But then, you have to remind yourself, you are not doing this for the crowd. You are doing this for the person in the mirror. You are doing this because you love the sport. But I won't lie, it would be nice to have a home crowd that actually shows up and supports the local scene.

So, how do we change this? How do we build a sustainable sporting culture in Singapore? I think it has to start from the media and the schools. The local media needs to cover the domestic leagues more. Not just when there is a scandal, or when someone wins a gold medal. And the schools need to de-emphasize the academic-only route. Celebrate the sports achievements as much as the academic achievements. If a kid breaks a national record, give him the same assembly stage time as the kid who scored 290 for the PSLE. Change the narrative.

And I think as consumers, we also need to be more gracious. We need to understand that these athletes are not machines. They are young kids, mostly in their 20s, carrying the weight of the nation. When they lose, they are probably more devastated than we are. We don't need to tear them down on social media. A little bit of empathy goes a long way. If your son fails his math test, you don't post on Facebook, 'My son very stupid, waste my money.' You encourage him. But for athletes, we give them zero grace. We need to change that mindset.

Very well said, Mark. Empathy and grace. Ryan, what is your message to the young kids out there who are watching this, who love sports, but are terrified of telling their parents that they want to pursue it seriously?
RB RB VBD , NNP . NNP CC NN . NNP , WP VBZ PRP$ NN IN DT JJ NNS RB RB WP VBP VBG DT , WP VBP NNS , CC VBP VBN IN VBG PRP$ NNS IN PRP VBP TO VB PRP RB .

My message to them is: follow your passion, but be smart about it. Don't just be a dreamer; be a planner. If you want to be an athlete, show your parents that you are disciplined. Show them that your grades are still okay, that you are managing your time. Prove to them that sports can teach you resilience, teamwork, and leadership, which are skills that will help you in any career. And don't be afraid to fail. The track will teach you how to lose, and how to get back up. That is the most valuable lesson you can learn. And to the parents watching, please, don't crush their dreams. Let them run. Let them play. Because one day, when they are sitting in an office cubicle staring at a spreadsheet, they will look back and wish they had spent more time on the field.

Just start small, lah. Don't think you need to run a marathon tomorrow. Just go down to the park connector, walk a bit, jog a bit. Find a community. Join a social run group, or a casual basketball group. There are so many free community clinics out there. The most important thing is just to move. Singapore is a very stressful place, and sports is the best way to clear your head. Don't do it for the Instagram post, do it for your mental health. And if you see an athlete training at the track, don't just stare at them. Give them a thumbs up. A little encouragement goes a long way.
